name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads and routes users to XR services, case studies, pop-up configurator, events, and contact flows using dynamic content.

core_features: Load homepage content (hero, featured service categories, flagship service packages, featured case studies, upcoming events) via getHomePageContent on initial page load.
Display hero content with dynamically defined primary calls-to-action that route to quote, project brief, or pop-up configurator flows.
Present featured service categories with direct navigation into the relevant service_category.html page using category slugs as URL parameters.
Expose flagship service packages with navigation to service_package.html using the selected package ID as a URL parameter.
Highlight featured case studies with navigation to case_study_detail.html and a global link to case_studies.html.
List upcoming events/workshops with navigation to event_detail.html and a global link to events.html.
Expose global quick-access actions from the homepage to contact.html and popup_configurator.html using URL parameters to preserve intent and context.
user_workflows: Explore services from the homepage: 1) User lands on index.html. 2) The page calls getHomePageContent and receives featuredServiceCategories. 3) User selects a featured service category tile. 4) The click handler reads the category.slug from the bound ServiceCategory object and navigates to service_category.html?serviceId={slug}.

Open a flagship service package: 1) User lands on index.html. 2) getHomePageContent returns flagshipServicePackages. 3) User clicks on a package card. 4) The handler reads the ServicePackage.id and navigates to service_package.html?servicePackageId={id}.

Start a quote or contact flow: 1) User lands on index.html and getHomePageContent.hero provides primaryCtas with an actionType such as request_quote. 2) User clicks a relevant CTA rendered from hero.primaryCtas. 3) The handler maps actionType=request_quote to navigation: contact.html?source=home&intent=request_quote. 4) On contact.html, the form can pre-select the quote-centric options based on the URL parameters.

Start a project brief flow: 1) User lands on index.html and getHomePageContent.hero.primaryCtas contains an item with actionType=start_project_brief (if provided). 2) User clicks this CTA. 3) The handler navigates to contact.html?source=home&intent=start_project_brief so the downstream page can show project-brief-specific UI or guide the user to choose a ServicePackage.

Configure a pop-up installation: 1) User lands on index.html; hero.primaryCtas includes an actionType=configure_popup. 2) User clicks this CTA or a global quick-action. 3) The handler navigates to popup_configurator.html?source=home. 4) The configurator page uses its own interfaces to create an ExperienceConfiguration.

Browse featured case studies from the homepage: 1) getHomePageContent returns featuredCaseStudies. 2) User clicks on a case study card. 3) The handler navigates to case_study_detail.html?caseStudyId={id}. 4) Alternatively, the user clicks the 'View all case studies' link which routes to case_studies.html?source=home_featured.

Discover upcoming events or workshops: 1) getHomePageContent returns upcomingEvents. 2) User clicks an event card. 3) The handler navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={id}. 4) Or the user clicks the 'View all workshops' link, which navigates to events.html?source=home_events.

Deep-link to homepage sections: 1) A user arrives at index.html with a URL parameter such as ?section=events or ?section=case-studies. 2) On load, a section navigation handler reads window.location.search, and after getHomePageContent resolves, it scrolls or focuses the corresponding section component. 3) If a highlightId parameter is present (e.g., highlightCaseStudyId or highlightPackageId), the relevant component uses that ID to emphasize or auto-scroll to the corresponding item within the loaded data.
interactions: On page load, the homepage data loader component calls getHomePageContent with no parameters and distributes the response data to bound components.

Hero CTA clicks: for each item in hero.primaryCtas, clicking triggers a mapped navigation based on actionType, using URL parameters to encode intent and source (for example, contact.html?source=home&intent=request_quote).
Service category tile clicks: clicking a category tile uses the associated ServiceCategory.slug to navigate to service_category.html?serviceId={slug}.
Flagship service package card clicks: clicking a package card uses ServicePackage.id to navigate to service_package.html?servicePackageId={id}.
Featured case study card clicks: clicking uses CaseStudy.id to navigate to case_study_detail.html?caseStudyId={id}.
Clicking the 'View all case studies' control navigates to case_studies.html?source=home_featured.
Upcoming event card clicks: clicking uses Event.id to navigate to event_detail.html?eventId={id}.
Clicking the 'View all workshops' control navigates to events.html?source=home_events.

Global quick-action clicks: a persistent quick-actions component exposes actions equivalent to hero.primaryCtas (where applicable). Clicking these actions navigates to contact.html or popup_configurator.html with URL parameters indicating the user’s intent and the source page.
In-page section navigation (e.g., a minimal top-of-page link list) updates the URL query parameter section without reloading the page (using history.pushState) and scrolls to the selected section.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not maintain persistent client-side state via localStorage; instead, it uses URL parameters and in-memory state derived from getHomePageContent. The initial state is created by calling getHomePageContent once per page load. Optional URL parameters are used as follows: 1) section: determines which main section to focus or scroll to after data load (e.g., index.html?section=services, index.html?section=case-studies, index.html?section=events). The section-navigation handler reads this parameter and calls scroll/focus on the corresponding component once the relevant data is available. 2) highlightPackageId: if present, the flagship service packages component uses this ID to locate a ServicePackage in flagshipServicePackages and mark it as highlighted (for example by focusing it or positioning it within view). If the ID does not match any loaded package, the component does nothing beyond the default rendering. 3) highlightCaseStudyId: similarly used by the featured case studies component to highlight or focus a CaseStudy in featuredCaseStudies. 4) source and intent URL parameters are added during navigation from the Home page to other pages (contact.html, popup_configurator.html, events.html, case_studies.html, service_category.html, service_package.html) to preserve the context of how the user arrived and their intended action; these parameters are only attached on outbound links and do not change how getHomePageContent behaves. The Home page itself does not modify the response from getHomePageContent based on URL parameters; it only uses URL parameters to control focusing, highlighting, and outbound navigation.

components.functionality: Calls getHomePageContent on initial load, stores the returned hero, featuredServiceCategories, flagshipServicePackages, featuredCaseStudies, and upcomingEvents in in-memory state, and passes the data to child components as props or via a shared state mechanism.

Uses the hero object from getHomePageContent to render the hero’s primaryCtas. Each CTA’s label is displayed as provided by the API, and clicking a CTA triggers navigation based on its actionType while preserving context via URL parameters.

Displays the featuredServiceCategories returned by getHomePageContent as clickable tiles. Each tile uses the ServiceCategory fields for text and uses the slug field to build navigation links to the corresponding service_category.html page.

Renders flagshipServicePackages as cards. Each card can be clicked to view its detailed service package page. The component can also expose per-card quick actions, such as starting a quote for a specific package, by routing to contact.html with the package ID in the query string.

Displays featuredCaseStudies as a horizontally scrollable list or strip of cards. Each card uses CaseStudy fields and is clickable to open the detailed case study page. A control within the component navigates to the full case study listing page.

Lists upcomingEvents returned by getHomePageContent. Each event entry is clickable to navigate to the detailed event page. A separate control routes to the events.html page to browse all events or workshops.

Provides always-visible quick-access actions that mirror relevant hero.primaryCtas (when present) so users can request a quote, start a project brief, or configure a pop-up installation from anywhere on the Home page. It reuses the same mapping logic from actionType to target URLs and URL parameters.

Synchronizes the visible section of the Home page with the section URL parameter. On initial load, after getHomePageContent has resolved, it reads the section parameter (if present) and scrolls or focuses the corresponding component (e.g., services grid, case studies strip, events list). It also updates the section parameter when users navigate between sections using in-page links.

Reads optional highlightPackageId and highlightCaseStudyId URL parameters and, after getHomePageContent data has been loaded, locates the matching ServicePackage or CaseStudy in the corresponding arrays and requests that their components highlight or scroll to those items. If no match is found, it leaves the UI unchanged.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, tall hero section at the top that fills the initial viewport, mirroring the mockup: a wide, pill-shaped navigation/action bar centered horizontally at the very top; a left-aligned hero content column (headline, subcopy, CTAs) sitting in the upper-middle; and a detached scroll cue centered at the bottom. Below the hero, content flows linearly down the page in clearly separated sections (services grid → flagship packages → case studies strip → events), all aligned to the same invisible central content column, maintaining a strong, editorial vertical stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, tall hero section at the top that fills the initial viewport, mirroring the mockup: a wide, pill-shaped navigation/action bar centered horizontally at the very top; a left-aligned hero content column (headline, subcopy, CTAs) sitting in the upper-middle; and a detached scroll cue centered at the bottom. Below the hero, content flows linearly down the page in clearly separated sections (services grid → flagship packages → case studies strip → events), all aligned to the same invisible central content column, maintaining a strong, editorial vertical stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the content column sits inside a centered container that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving about 15–20% margin on the left and right, matching the cinematic framing from the mockup. The hero occupies the full height of the initial viewport: the pill-shaped global quick actions/nav bar occupies the top 10–15% of the height, the hero text and CTAs fill roughly the middle 40–50%, and the bottom 15–20% is largely empty aside from the centered scroll indicator. Each subsequent section has generous vertical margins (64–96px) above and below to keep them visually isolated and airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a low content density: each section emphasizes a single primary content block (grid, list, or carousel) instead of cramming multiple modules together. Within sections, components have generous internal padding (16–24px vertical, 24–40px horizontal) and large gaps (32–64px) between cards or rows. Substantial negative space around the hero text, between sections, and at the edges of the container reinforces a premium, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top with the glowing pill-shaped global quick actions/nav bar, then drops into the bold hero headline and supporting copy in the upper-middle. From there, the eye is guided down to the hero CTAs, then to the bottom-centered scroll chevron, encouraging continuation. As the user scrolls, the flow follows a clear vertical storyline: service categories grid (what the studio does at a high level), flagship packages list (deep-dive into key offerings), featured case studies strip (proof and inspiration), and finally upcoming events list (time-based calls to action). The quick actions bar remains visually available near the top edge, while subtle highlighting behaviors guide attention to hovered or currently relevant items.

layout.overall_description: The homepage opens on a matte-black, full-viewport hero that feels like a cinematic console. At the very top, a wide, pill-shaped global quick actions bar floats centered, spanning about two-thirds of the screen width with ample breathing room above and to its sides. Below this, set into an invisible central column, a bold, oversized hero headline is left-aligned in the upper-middle of the screen, with a short line of subcopy directly beneath it, marked by a thin vertical neon line on its far left. Just below, still anchored to the same left edge, sit the primary hero actions in pill-shaped buttons, while the rest of the hero remains mostly dark, emphasizing negative space. At the bottom center of the viewport, a small neon double-chevron scroll cue floats independently of the text column, inviting the user downward. As the user scrolls, they encounter a sequence of generously spaced sections—service categories, flagship packages, featured case studies, and upcoming events—each confined to the same central content width, left-aligned and surrounded by large gutters, giving the impression of editorial spreads in a high-end magazine. The navigation/action pill subtly persists near the top as an anchor, while invisible routing and highlight handlers orchestrate section changes and focus states without disrupting the clean spatial composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-data-loader acts primarily behind the scenes and does not occupy a fixed position in the visible grid under normal conditions. Conceptually, it sits at the root of the page layout, covering the full viewport (100% width and height) as an invisible layer that initializes content. If a loading state is needed, it temporarily overlays the entire viewport with a matte-black veil and a subtle, centered neon progress indicator, keeping the user fully within the dark, futuristic aesthetic. During this brief overlay, it sits above all other components, but once data is loaded it becomes non-visual and no longer affects the spatial arrangement of the hero or subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-screen, fixed-position overlay (top: 0, left: 0, width: 100vw, height: 100vh) only while loading; otherwise it is non-visual and does not affect layout or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hero-actions component is placed within the central hero content column in the upper-middle of the initial viewport. The content column itself begins roughly 15–20% in from the left edge of the viewport and spans about 60–70% of the width. Directly above hero-actions is the main hero headline and, immediately beneath the headline, a single line of subcopy marked on the far left by a thin vertical neon line. Hero-actions sits 24–32px below this subcopy, aligned to the same left edge, and occupies approximately the left 40–50% of the content column, leaving generous empty space to the right. The CTAs themselves appear as pill-shaped buttons or minimal controls arranged in a single horizontal row or a short two-row stack, with 16–24px internal vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, spaced 16–24px apart. Below hero-actions, the hero gradually opens into negative space, culminating in the bottom-centered scroll indicator, which is visually connected to the hero-actions via vertical alignment but not physically attached to the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section and scrolls out of view with the hero; the component does not stick or float, but its contents may use subtle micro-animations on hover or on load without changing their static spatial placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-category-feature-grid forms the first major section directly below the hero. It begins roughly 96px beneath the bottom edge of the hero section, maintaining the generous inter-section spacing. The grid is anchored to the same central content column as the hero text: the column starts about 15–20% in from the left and spans around 60–70% of the viewport width. Within this column, the grid is arranged in two to three columns depending on screen width, with each feature card occupying roughly one-third to one-half of the column width. Cards are large, with 24px vertical and 32–40px horizontal internal padding, surrounded by 32–48px gaps horizontally and 40–64px vertically. The grid's top aligns to a section heading and brief descriptor at the far left, which sit above the first row of cards with about 32px spacing. The right side of the content column may intentionally remain partially empty if fewer cards are present, reinforcing the spacious, editorial feel and ensuring the left alignment remains dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section in the scroll flow; it does not stick or overlay but can subtly animate cards into view as they enter the viewport while preserving their defined positions and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The flagship-service-packages-list occupies the next main section down the page, beginning approximately 80–96px below the service-category-feature-grid. It remains within the same central content column, left-aligned, with identical horizontal margins to the hero and services grid. This component appears as a vertical stack of large, card-like rows, each representing a flagship package. Each card spans about 70–80% of the content column width, leaving a small strip of negative space on the right that visually tempers the density. Cards have generous padding (24px vertical, 32–40px horizontal) with 32–48px vertical spacing between each. A concise section title and optional one-line description sit above the first card, left-aligned, with roughly 32px separation. The list height is substantial but visually light due to the spacing and the dark background between cards, giving the section a sense of depth without feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls normally. Cards may respond to hover with a subtle neon edge or glow driven by the highlight state, but their layout remains fixed and aligned to the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-case-studies-strip is placed about 80–96px below the flagship-service-packages-list, forming a visually distinct, horizontally oriented band. It stretches across the same central content width but is designed to feel more expansive and cinematic. At the top of this section, a left-aligned title and very short subtitle sit within the column, with 24–32px of space below them. Beneath this, a horizontal carousel of case study cards occupies most of the section's height. Each card is tall and narrow, approximately 40–60% of the column width, with only one or one-and-a-half cards fully in view at a time on desktop, creating a sense of focus. The carousel track is horizontally scrollable (or controlled with minimal arrows), while the section itself is vertically static. Cards sit on the same horizontal baseline, with 24–32px gaps between them. The strip sits on the matte black background but may use slightly elevated shadows or neon accents on actively highlighted cards, maintaining the dark, premium look without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow but horizontally scrollable within its bounds. The component stays in place as the user scrolls past; horizontal movement is user-driven via drag, scroll, or arrows, with snapping behavior to keep cards centered without shifting the section’s vertical placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming-events-list appears further down the page, roughly 80–96px beneath the featured-case-studies-strip, continuing the vertical rhythm of separate sections. It uses the same central content column and left alignment but with a slightly lighter visual weight. A compact section heading sits at the top left of the column, with minimal descriptive text or filters beneath it. The events themselves are laid out in a clean vertical list, each entry occupying about 50–60% of the content column width to preserve generous right-side negative space. Each row contains key metadata (date, title, location or format) arranged in a single line or two-line block, with 16–24px vertical padding inside each row and 16–24px gaps between rows. The overall section height is moderate, providing enough entries to feel informative without overwhelming the page. The open right side of the column and the dark background maintain the airy, editorial styling established in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list that scrolls normally. Individual rows may highlight subtly on hover or when focused (e.g., a thin neon rule on the left), but the list does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-quick-actions-bar is the visually dominant pill-shaped bar at the top of the hero, serving as both a navigation and quick action hub. It is horizontally centered within the viewport and sits near the top, occupying approximately the top 10–15% of the initial viewport height. The bar spans about 65–75% of the viewport width, leaving generous margins on both left and right. Its pill shape is defined by substantial border-radius and internal padding of around 16–20px vertically and 24–40px horizontally. Within the bar, content is arranged in a horizontal layout: brand mark or wordmark anchored to the left of the pill, primary navigation or quick actions centered, and any utility icons or status indicators aligned to the right. There is clear spacing between these zones, matching the low-density aesthetic. As the user scrolls, the bar remains visually close to the top edge, providing a persistent sense of structure, while the hero content moves beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky header-style pill: initially positioned within the hero with top margin from the viewport edge; as the user scrolls, it pins to a small offset from the top (e.g., 16–24px) and remains visible above subsequent sections. Its width and centered alignment stay constant, and it floats above content with a subtle shadow or glow, without compressing or overlapping the main content column excessively.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation-handler is an invisible orchestration layer that maps scroll positions and route states to the visible sections (hero, services, packages, case studies, events). It does not present any visible UI elements itself. Conceptually, it spans the full height of the page and is anchored to the same central column and section boundaries: invisible anchors or trigger zones are placed near the top edges of each major section, aligning with the left edge of the content column. These anchors do not occupy visible space but correspond to where the user enters a new section in the vertical flow, enabling smooth routing and deep-linking without breaking the minimalist layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, with invisible markers positioned at key vertical offsets; it does not affect the physical spacing or alignment of sections. It listens to scroll and route changes, effectively acting as an overlay logic layer rather than a spatial element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight-target-handler is an underlying state manager that coordinates visual emphasis on interactive elements such as service cards, flagship packages, carousel items, and event rows. It does not have a visible footprint of its own. Instead, it subtly modulates the appearance of existing components—e.g., adding a thin neon border, a soft outer glow, or a slight elevation—when items are hovered, focused, or programmatically highlighted. These highlights are confined to the original boundaries of each component, preserving the clean grid and spacing while momentarily increasing the perceived depth and interactivity in line with the futuristic aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual with respect to layout: it does not move, resize, or reposition elements. Highlight effects are purely stylistic overlays applied within current component bounds, ensuring that spacing, alignment, and the page’s overall rhythm remain unchanged.

name: Services Overview
title: Services
description: This page provides an overview of all service categories, typical engagement models, and popular service packages, and routes users to detailed service, package, and contact pages.

core_features: Fetch and display services overview data using the getServicesOverview interface, including introductory text, service categories, engagement models, and popular service packages.
List all available service categories as selectable items that navigate to detailed service category pages with appropriate URL parameters.
Display an overview of typical engagement models to help users understand how projects are usually structured.
Highlight popular service packages, including key details such as technology and pricing information when available, and route users to the corresponding service package detail pages.
Provide clear call-to-action elements that send users to the contact page with the inquiry type preset for project quotes.
Allow deep-linking into specific sections or highlighted categories of the page using URL query parameters, without relying on local storage.
Handle loading and error states for the services overview data to ensure users receive appropriate feedback if data is still loading or unavailable.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Discover and open a service category: 1) User navigates to services.html. 2) Page calls getServicesOverview and loads the serviceCategories list. 3) User scans the list of service categories. 4) User clicks a category item. 5) The click handler reads the clicked category.slug and navigates to service_category.html?serviceId={category.slug}.

Workflow 2 – Explore a popular service package: 1) User lands on services.html and waits for getServicesOverview to return popularServicePackages. 2) User scrolls to the popular packages section. 3) User clicks on a specific package card. 4) The click handler reads the selected package.id and navigates to service_package.html?servicePackageId={package.id}.

Workflow 3 – Learn how engagements work and request a quote: 1) User arrives on services.html. 2) User scrolls to the engagement models section populated from engagementModels. 3) User reads through each engagement model’s title and description. 4) User clicks a CTA button labeled for contacting the studio. 5) The click handler navigates to contact.html?inquiryType=project_quote.

Workflow 4 – Deep link into a section of the Services page: 1) User opens services.html with a query parameter such as services.html?section=popular_packages. 2) On load, the page-state controller reads the section parameter. 3) Once getServicesOverview resolves, the controller scrolls or focuses the corresponding section (popular packages). 4) User interacts with that section without any additional manual navigation.

Workflow 5 – Deep link that highlights a specific category: 1) User opens services.html with a query parameter such as services.html?highlightServiceId={someServiceSlug}. 2) After getServicesOverview resolves, the page-state controller compares highlightServiceId to each ServiceCategory.slug. 3) If a match is found, that category item is marked as highlighted/selected in the category list. 4) User can click the highlighted item to navigate to service_category.html?serviceId={matchedSlug}.
interactions: Initial data load: On page load, a data-loader component calls getServicesOverview with no parameters, sets a loading state, and upon success passes introText, serviceCategories, engagementModels, and popularServicePackages to child components.
Click on a service category item: When a user clicks a service category item, the event handler reads the bound ServiceCategory.slug and navigates to service_category.html?serviceId={slug}.

Click on a popular service package card: When a user clicks a popular service package card, the event handler reads the bound ServicePackage.id and navigates to service_package.html?servicePackageId={id}.
Click on a global quote/contact CTA: When a user clicks a CTA such as “Talk to us” or “Request a custom quote”, the event handler navigates to contact.html?inquiryType=project_quote.

Section-focused navigation via URL: When the page loads, the state controller reads query parameters (e.g., section and highlightServiceId) from the URL and sets internal flags that control which section is scrolled into view and which category item is highlighted.
Retry on data load error: If getServicesOverview fails, the data-loader component displays an error message with a retry button; clicking retry triggers another call to getServicesOverview.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page does not accept parameters for the getServicesOverview interface itself, but it uses URL query parameters to control UI state and deep-linking behavior. Supported query parameters on services.html include: 1) section: optional string used to indicate which section should be focused or scrolled into view after data loads. Example values could represent sections such as category list, engagement models, or popular packages. The page-state controller reads section on load, and once getServicesOverview data is available, it sets the corresponding section into a 'focused' state and scrolls it into view using the History API without reloading the page. 2) highlightServiceId: optional string intended to match a ServiceCategory.slug. After getServicesOverview resolves, the page-state controller compares highlightServiceId with the slug field of each ServiceCategory object in serviceCategories. If a match is found, that category item’s state is set to 'highlighted'. The category list component receives this highlight state and adjusts its behavior (e.g., marking the item as selected). If no match is found, no item is highlighted, and the page falls back to a neutral state. When users interact with in-page controls (such as clicking section-specific anchors or manual highlight controls), the page updates the URL query parameters using history.pushState or history.replaceState so that the current section and highlighted category remain encoded in the URL. Navigation to other pages always uses URL parameters as specified: 1) service_category.html is opened with serviceId set to the ServiceCategory.slug from the clicked category. 2) service_package.html is opened with servicePackageId set to the ServicePackage.id from the clicked package. 3) contact.html is opened with inquiryType=project_quote when a quote-related CTA is clicked. No state is stored in localStorage or similar client-side storage; all shareable state is represented via URL parameters or transient in-memory component state.

components.functionality: On initial render, call the getServicesOverview interface to retrieve introText, serviceCategories, engagementModels, and popularServicePackages. Manage loading, success, and error states and provide the loaded data to child components via props or a shared state store.
Display the introductory text for the services overview using the introText value returned from getServicesOverview. Render nothing or a placeholder message if introText is empty or undefined.

Render a list of service category items from serviceCategories. Each item uses fields from ServiceCategory (such as name, description, intro_content, hero_image) to show a concise representation. The component also accepts a highlightedServiceSlug prop derived from the highlightServiceId query parameter, and marks the corresponding item as highlighted when it matches a category.slug.

Render the engagementModels array from getServicesOverview as a list of expandable items, each showing a title and description. This helps users understand different ways of working with the studio. The component can receive an isFocused flag when the URL section parameter targets this block, and scroll into view or expand the first item accordingly.

Display popularServicePackages from getServicesOverview as a grid or list of cards. Each card binds to a ServicePackage and shows data such as name, short_summary, technology, experience_type, and pricing information when base_price and price_type are available. If pricing fields are missing, the card indicates that pricing is available on request without hardcoding values. Cards provide a primary click area to open the detailed package page.

Provide one or more call-to-action buttons for users who want to discuss a project or request a custom quote. The component does not collect form input; instead, it navigates users to contact.html with inquiryType set appropriately for project quotes.

Read URL query parameters on page load and on history changes, and synchronize them with in-page state. Specifically, it parses the section parameter to determine which section (categories, engagement models, or popular packages) should be focused, and parses highlightServiceId to determine which ServiceCategory.slug should be highlighted. After getServicesOverview completes, it passes highlightedServiceSlug and section focus flags to the respective components, and can update the URL via the History API when users change section focus manually.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: All components are arranged in a single, vertical column running down the center of the viewport, with content left-aligned inside a wide, invisible central column. The page starts with a hero-like services intro near the top, followed by a stacked sequence: service categories, engagement models (accordion), popular packages (card grid), and finally a strong quote/CTA strip. Data and state components remain non-visual, not affecting the visible stacking order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components are arranged in a single, vertical column running down the center of the viewport, with content left-aligned inside a wide, invisible central column. The page starts with a hero-like services intro near the top, followed by a stacked sequence: service categories, engagement models (accordion), popular packages (card grid), and finally a strong quote/CTA strip. Data and state components remain non-visual, not affecting the visible stacking order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, a central content container spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered horizontally, leaving about 15–20% dark margin on both left and right. Within that container, the main text column for copy-driven components (intro, categories, engagement models) uses about 55–60% of the page width, leaving breathing room to the right. The hero-style intro occupies roughly the upper 40–50% of the first viewport, the categories and engagement models each use about 25–35% of viewport height as the user scrolls, the popular packages grid fills a deeper section (~50–60% viewport height), and the quote CTA gets its own band (25–35% viewport height) near the bottom.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low and cinematic. Each section has generous vertical spacing above and below (typically 64–96px on desktop), with substantial padding inside interactive components (accordions, cards, CTA). Lists and grids are visually light: items are separated by 24–48px, and there is consistent negative space around and between elements to maintain the premium, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s top-down rhythm: users enter via the global nav and immediately meet the bold services intro text in the upper-middle of the screen. Their gaze then moves down the shared left-aligned content edge through the service categories, into the engagement models accordions, and then lands on the more visually dense popular packages grid. Finally, attention is drawn to the bottom quote CTA strip, which acts as the closing, high-contrast call to action. Throughout, alignment on the same left text edge and consistent vertical spacing create a clear, uninterrupted reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Services Overview page is a tall, dark canvas with a matte black background and a centered content container, echoing a futuristic editorial spread. After the site’s pill-shaped nav at the very top, the first viewport is dominated by a hero-style services intro: a bold, large-scale headline and supporting copy sit in the upper-middle of the screen, left-aligned within an invisible column that occupies a bit over half the page width, with generous empty space to the right and below. As the user scrolls, sections flow in a single vertical stack, all anchored to the same left text edge: first a clean, vertically spaced list of service categories; then a minimal accordion list of engagement models; then a wider, more structured grid of popular packages sitting like illuminated tiles within the darkness. The page ends with a confident quote/CTA panel that spans the central width in a strong band, visually separated by extra vertical margin and pronounced padding. Throughout, the design is sparse and high-contrast, with neon accent lines and understated dividers guiding the eye without clutter, preserving a cinematic, XR-studio aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is non-visual and does not occupy a distinct, perceivable area of the layout. It is conceptually positioned at the top of the page hierarchy, initializing before the hero-style services intro content renders. If any loading UI is needed, it would appear as a very thin neon-accented progress line or subtle pulse at the very top edge of the content area, spanning about 80–90% of the content container width and centered horizontally, but otherwise it remains invisible and does not alter the spacing or stacking of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically fixed at the top of the page render tree but visually non-intrusive; it does not affect document flow and only overlays minimal loading affordances when necessary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services intro text behaves as a hero section. On desktop, it sits in the upper half of the first viewport, starting roughly 96px below the global nav pill, with its content aligned to the left within the central container. The central container spans about 70% of the viewport width and is centered, while the intro text column itself uses roughly the left 55–60% of the viewport, leaving a broad, empty dark area to the right to preserve the premium, cinematic feel. The main headline occupies the upper-middle of this space, taking approximately one to two lines of very large, bold type. Below the headline, separated by around 24–32px, sits the supporting paragraph or short description, also left-aligned to the same text edge. There is at least 64–80px of negative space below the intro block before the next section begins, so that this hero content feels isolated and commanding within the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in normal flow, effectively forming the hero section of the page. On tall viewports, it visually fills ~40–50% of the height before scrolling is needed to fully reveal subsequent content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service category list begins just below the hero intro, with a clear vertical gap of about 80–96px to separate it as the next section. It shares the same central container and left alignment as the intro: the list’s title (if present) aligns to the identical left text edge, reinforcing the vertical spine of the page. The list itself occupies about 60–65% of the page width on desktop, leaving dark negative space to the right. Categories are arranged in a single vertical column: each category row is generously spaced, with approximately 24–32px vertical padding inside each row and 16–24px of gap between rows. Any category labels or brief descriptions are left-aligned; optional neon accent lines or subtle dividers could run vertically at the extreme left or horizontally below each item, but overall the section remains visually light. The entire list feels like a tall stack in the middle of the page, taking up roughly 25–35% of the viewport height, with at least 64px of margin before the engagement models section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block occupying the main content column; scrolls naturally with the page, maintaining alignment with the hero intro text above and the accordion section below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The engagement models accordion section sits directly beneath the service categories, separated by about 64–80px of vertical whitespace. It continues in the same central content container but can appear slightly more compact, using roughly 55–60% of the viewport width, again left-aligned to the same text edge as the sections above. The section heading appears at the top, with a small vertical neon accent bar to its left, echoing the mockup’s subheading treatment and drawing the eye downward. Below, each accordion item is a full-width row within this column: rows are about 64–80px tall when collapsed, with 24px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding. Accordion items are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps, maintaining plenty of negative space between them so the list doesn’t feel crowded. When an item expands, its details drop down beneath its title within the same width, pushing the following items further down and increasing the height of this section as needed, but still respecting the overall dark margins on both sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding/collapsing within the normal document flow as items are opened, but otherwise behaves like a standard block section aligned to the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The popular packages card grid appears as a more visually dense, structured section lower on the page, beginning about 96px beneath the engagement models section to clearly mark a new content zone. On desktop, its container spans around 70–75% of the viewport width, still centered, but the grid content itself is visually anchored to the same left text edge as previous sections. Cards are arranged in a 3-column grid on large screens (dropping to 2 columns on medium), with 24–32px of horizontal and vertical gap between cards. Each card has generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and a subtle glow or neon accent line, set against the matte black background, giving the impression of floating tiles. The grid extends downward, typically filling 50–60% of the viewport height depending on the number of packages, with the bottom of the grid ending in a substantial 80–96px margin before the CTA panel. The dark margins on the left and right of the entire grid section remain consistent with the rest of the page, framing the cards and preserving the premium, spacious aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid within the central container; cards reflow responsively but the section itself scrolls normally with the page, forming a visually substantial middle-late chapter of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote CTA panel serves as the closing, high-impact section near the bottom of the page. It is separated from the packages grid by a pronounced vertical gap of roughly 96px to make it feel like a distinct, final step. On desktop, the panel takes the form of a wide, horizontally oriented card or pill that spans about 60–65% of the viewport width and is centered within the page, yet its textual content is still left-aligned, matching the central column’s left edge. The panel has generous padding—approximately 32–40px on all sides—so the headline, brief supporting copy, and CTA button or link have ample breathing room. It may employ a slightly lighter or subtly textured dark background, or a soft neon border, to separate it from the rest of the page while remaining on-brand. There is at least 80px of negative space above and below the panel before the footer or page end, allowing it to stand alone as the last major focal point the user encounters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block near the bottom of the page. It does not remain sticky, but its isolated position and generous negative space ensure it commands attention when the user scrolls into it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state controller is entirely non-visual, responsible for managing filters, selected categories, accordion states, and any dynamic behaviors across the services overview page. It has no direct spatial footprint in the UI: it neither reserves space nor affects the geometric layout of visible components. Any UI feedback driven by it (such as highlighting a selected category or pre-expanding a specific engagement model) happens within the already defined layouts of those visual components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically persistent in the background and conceptually anchored to the page root; it does not participate in the visual document flow and exerts no direct spatial behavior beyond controlling the state of other components.

name: Service Category Listing
title: Service Packages
description: Page for exploring and filtering service packages within a selected service category, using dynamic filters, sorting, and pagination.

core_features: Identify the current service category from the URL parameter `serviceId` and load its introductory information using `getServiceCategoryIntro`.
Fetch available filter and sort options for the selected service category using `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions` and render them as interactive controls.

Apply filters for technology, experience type, industry, participants range, budget/price range, and expected reach, mapped to URL query parameters and passed as `filters` into `searchServicePackages`.
Allow sorting of packages via a sort control bound to the `sortOptions` from `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions` and the `sortBy` parameter of `searchServicePackages`.
Search and paginate service packages within the selected category using `searchServicePackages`, with `page` and `pageSize` controlled via URL parameters.
Display service packages as clickable cards using data from `searchServicePackages.results`, each navigating to `service_package.html` with `servicePackageId` in the URL.
Show a category introduction block with text from `getServiceCategoryIntro` and a generic contact CTA that links to `contact.html?inquiryType=project_quote`.
When the current category corresponds to the pop-up installations slug, display a dedicated CTA that navigates to `popup_configurator.html`.
Handle empty result states when `searchServicePackages.totalCount` is zero, with options to adjust filters or navigate to the contact page for a custom inquiry.
user_workflows:
Browse a service category:
1. User opens `service_category.html` with a `serviceId` URL parameter from navigation or another page.
2. The page controller reads `serviceId` and calls `getServiceCategoryIntro(serviceCategorySlug = serviceId)`.
3. The intro section shows the category name, descriptive content, and context-specific CTAs.
4. The page controller calls `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions(serviceCategorySlug = serviceId)` and renders filter and sort controls.
5. The page controller constructs a `filters` object from any existing filter-related URL parameters and calls `searchServicePackages(serviceCategorySlug = serviceId, filters, sortBy, page, pageSize)`.
6. The package list displays the results as cards with key info such as summary and pricing labels from the API response.
7. The user can scroll through results and use pagination controls to navigate between pages; the `page` URL parameter and `searchServicePackages` call are updated accordingly.

Filter and refine results:
1. User adjusts a filter (e.g., chooses a technology or updates a budget range) in the filter bar.
2. The filter bar component updates or adds the corresponding filter query parameter(s) in the URL and resets the `page` parameter to the first page.
3. The page controller detects the URL change, rebuilds the `filters` object based on the current URL parameters, and calls `searchServicePackages` with the updated filters and existing or updated `sortBy`.
4. The results list and active filters summary update in place without navigating away from the page.
5. The user can remove individual filters from the active filters summary, which removes those parameters from the URL and triggers another `searchServicePackages` call.

Sort packages by priority:
1. User selects a sort option in the sort control (values come from `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions.sortOptions`).
2. The sort control writes the chosen sort option into the `sortBy` URL parameter and resets `page` to the first page.
3. The page controller calls `searchServicePackages` with the updated `sortBy` and existing filters.
4. The package list re-renders according to the new sort order.
5. The chosen sort option remains selected by reading `sortBy` from the URL on subsequent loads or shares.

Open a package detail page:
1. User clicks on a package card in the results list.
2. The card click handler reads the `servicePackage.id` from the bound result item.
3. The browser navigates to `service_package.html?servicePackageId={id}`.
4. The new page is responsible for loading and displaying detailed information for that package.

Use the Pop-up Installation Configurator (for the relevant category):
1. User navigates to `service_category.html?serviceId=pop_up_installations` or equivalent slug.
2. The intro component receives the category slug from `getServiceCategoryIntro.serviceCategory.slug` and determines that the configurator CTA should be shown.
3. The user clicks the configurator CTA button.
4. The browser navigates to `popup_configurator.html` without additional parameters.
5. The configurator page is responsible for configuration and quote-related flows.

Request a custom solution via Contact:
1. User reads the category intro and decides they need a custom solution beyond listed packages.
2. The user clicks the contact CTA in the intro or empty state panel.
3. The browser navigates to `contact.html?inquiryType=project_quote`.
4. The contact page pre-populates its context based on the `inquiryType` URL parameter.

Share or bookmark a filtered view:
1. User applies filters and sorting until the results match their preferences.
2. The current state (service category, filters, sort, page) is fully represented in URL parameters.
3. The user copies the URL or bookmarks the page.
4. When the URL is opened again or shared with someone else, the page controller reads the URL parameters, reconstructs the `filters` and `sortBy`, and calls `searchServicePackages` to reproduce the same view.
interactions: On page load, read `serviceId` (and any filter/sort/pagination parameters) from the URL query string to initialize the page state.
Call `getServiceCategoryIntro` once `serviceId` is available; update the intro section with the returned `heroTitle`, `heroSubtitle`, and `serviceCategory` fields.
Call `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions` to retrieve the available `technologyOptions`, `experienceTypeOptions`, `industryOptions`, `sortOptions`, and numeric ranges; render them as filter widgets.

On any filter control change (selecting a technology, experience type, industry, or adjusting numeric ranges), update the corresponding URL parameters and reset the `page` parameter; trigger a new `searchServicePackages` call.
On clicking a 'reset filters' control, remove all filter-related parameters from the URL (except `serviceId`), reset `page`, and call `searchServicePackages` with an empty `filters` object.
On sort selection change, update the `sortBy` URL parameter and reset `page`; call `searchServicePackages` with the new sort value and existing filters.
On pagination button click (next/previous/specific page), update the `page` URL parameter and call `searchServicePackages` with existing filters and `sortBy` but new `page`.
On successful `searchServicePackages` response, render package cards using `results` and populate meta information with `totalCount`.
On clicking a package card, navigate to `service_package.html?servicePackageId={servicePackage.id}`.
If `getServiceCategoryIntro.serviceCategory.slug` indicates the pop-up installations category, render a configurator CTA; on click, navigate to `popup_configurator.html`.
On clicking any 'Contact us' CTA within the category intro or empty-state component, navigate to `contact.html?inquiryType=project_quote`.
If `searchServicePackages.totalCount` is zero, display an empty-state component suggesting filter adjustments or contact; keep filter controls active.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the URL query string. The required parameter `serviceId` maps directly to `ServiceCategory.slug` and determines the category context for all interface calls.

On initial load or when the URL changes, the page controller reads:
- `serviceId`: used as `serviceCategorySlug` for `getServiceCategoryIntro`, `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions`, and `searchServicePackages`.
- Filter-related parameters (names are implementation-level but may include values such as `technology`, `experienceType`, `industry`, `participantsMin`, `participantsMax`, `priceMin`, `priceMax`, `expectedReachMin`, `expectedReachMax`, or similar). These are combined into a `filters` object passed to `searchServicePackages`.
- `sortBy`: used directly as the `sortBy` argument for `searchServicePackages`. If absent, the UI may select a default from `sortOptions`, but that default selection is still reflected in the URL once the user interacts or on first load.
- `page`: the current results page number passed to `searchServicePackages`. If omitted or invalid, the page controller treats it as the first page and can normalize this back into the URL.
- `pageSize`: an optional parameter to control how many results are requested per page. If absent, the front-end may use its own internal default page size when calling `searchServicePackages`.

Any user interaction that changes filters, sorting, or pagination must update these URL parameters (e.g., via history APIs) so that the UI state is fully encoded in the URL. The page reacts to URL changes by re-deriving the `filters`, `sortBy`, and `page` values and re-invoking `searchServicePackages`.

No persistent client-side storage (such as localStorage) is used for page state. All sharable state is maintained through URL parameters and the latest API responses.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state management for the service category listing page. Reads URL parameters, calls the assigned interfaces (`getServiceCategoryIntro`, `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions`, `searchServicePackages`), and provides data and state to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URL parameters (including `serviceId`) and trigger initial calls to `getServiceCategoryIntro` and `getServiceCategoryFilterOptions`. Once both return, assemble a `filters` object from URL parameters and call `searchServicePackages`.

onUrlChange: When the URL query string changes (e.g., via filter changes, sort, pagination), recompute the `filters`, `sortBy`, `page`, and `pageSize` arguments and call `searchServicePackages` again.
onInterfaceError: Handle errors from any of the three interfaces by notifying dependent components (e.g., intro, filters, results) so they can render an appropriate error state.

components.functionality: Displays service category information at the top of the listing: category name, descriptive text, and contextual CTAs. It shows a generic 'Contact' CTA for all categories and, when the category slug matches the pop-up installations slug, a configurator CTA.
event_handlers: onClickConfiguratorCTA: When `serviceCategory.slug` indicates the pop-up installations category, navigate to `popup_configurator.html`.

components.functionality: Renders selectable filter controls (technology, experience type, industry, numeric ranges) and a sort control for the current service category, based on available options. It updates URL parameters to reflect user selections and triggers result refreshes via the page controller.
event_handlers: onFilterChange: When a discrete filter value (e.g., technology, experience type, industry) changes, update corresponding filter parameters in the URL and reset `page` to the first page.
onRangeChange: When numeric filters (price, participants, expected reach) change, update their min/max parameters in the URL and reset `page`.
onSortChange: When the sort option changes, update the `sortBy` parameter in the URL and reset `page`.
onResetFilters: Clear all filter-related URL parameters (retain `serviceId`) and reset `page`; the page controller will then call `searchServicePackages` with an empty `filters` object.

components.functionality: Displays the currently applied filters as removable items (e.g., chips or tags) and offers a control to clear all filters at once. Keeps the display synchronized with filter-related URL parameters.
event_handlers: onRemoveFilterItem: Remove the corresponding filter parameter(s) from the URL and reset `page` to the first page.
onClearAllFilters: Remove all filter-related URL parameters except for `serviceId` and reset `page`.

components.functionality: Shows metadata about the current results set, such as total number of matching packages and which sort order is currently applied, to provide context to the user.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When `searchServicePackages` results or relevant URL parameters change, recompute and display the updated meta information.

components.functionality: Renders a list or grid of service package cards using the `results` from `searchServicePackages`. Each card shows key package information and links to its detailed page when clicked.
event_handlers: onPackageClick: Navigate to `service_package.html?servicePackageId={servicePackage.id}` for the clicked result item.
onResultsUpdate: Re-render the list whenever `searchServicePackages.results` changes.

components.functionality: Provides controls for navigating between pages of search results. Uses `totalCount`, `page`, and `pageSize` to determine the available pages and updates the `page` URL parameter when the user navigates.
event_handlers: onPageChange: When the user selects a different page (next, previous, or specific page number), update the `page` parameter in the URL; the page controller then calls `searchServicePackages` with the new `page`.
onPageSizeChange: If a page-size selector is present, update the `pageSize` parameter in the URL, reset `page` to the first page, and trigger `searchServicePackages` with the new `pageSize`.

components.functionality: Appears when `searchServicePackages.totalCount` is zero. Informs the user that no packages match the current filters and suggests actions such as adjusting filters or contacting the studio.
event_handlers: onClickAdjustFilters: Focus or highlight the filter bar so the user can modify filters.
onClickContactCTA: Navigate to `contact.html?inquiryType=project_quote` for a custom request.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: All content sits within a single centered content container that respects a 12-column-like grid. The page-controller occupies a pill-shaped bar at the very top, echoing the hero nav from the mockup. Directly beneath it, the category-intro-section forms a hero-style header block aligned to the left within the central column. Below the hero, the filter stack (service-filter-bar, active-filters-summary, results-meta-bar) forms a compact control band before the main service-package-list. Pagination-controls sit at the bottom center of this central column. The empty-state-panel replaces the list and pagination region when no results are present.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All content sits within a single centered content container that respects a 12-column-like grid. The page-controller occupies a pill-shaped bar at the very top, echoing the hero nav from the mockup. Directly beneath it, the category-intro-section forms a hero-style header block aligned to the left within the central column. Below the hero, the filter stack (service-filter-bar, active-filters-summary, results-meta-bar) forms a compact control band before the main service-package-list. Pagination-controls sit at the bottom center of this central column. The empty-state-panel replaces the list and pagination region when no results are present.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On initial load, the page-controller and category-intro-section together fill roughly the top 40–50% of the viewport height, with generous negative space above, below, and to the sides, mirroring the hero composition. The filter band occupies a relatively shallow horizontal strip just below the hero (about 10–15% of viewport height), while the service-package-list takes over the rest of the vertical space and extends beyond the fold. Left and right margins for the content column remain wide (about 15–20% viewport width each), with interactive components spanning 60–70% of the viewport width. Section breaks (intro-to-filters, filters-to-list, list-to-pagination) use 48–64px vertical gaps to preserve a cinematic, airy feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is intentionally low, especially in the hero/intro and filter band. Each major component has clear breathing room around it, with 24–32px internal padding and 32–64px external spacing. The service-package-list is more populated but still uses generous gaps between cards and rows to maintain the premium, editorial feel rather than a crowded catalog. Controls (filters, meta, pagination) use spacious pill and chip treatments rather than tight clusters, keeping the interface calm despite the functional complexity.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the page-controller pill at the top center, then naturally drops into the left-aligned category-intro headline and description, echoing the mockup’s vertical progression. From there, attention moves down into the neon-accented filter bar, then to the active-filters-summary just below it, and then to the results-meta-bar acting as a bridge into the main list. The user’s gaze continues down through the vertically stacked service-package cards, finally reaching the centered pagination-controls at the bottom. When present, the empty-state-panel becomes the focal area in the middle of the page where the list would normally sit, framed by the filters above and the surrounding negative space.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a matte black canvas with a wide, invisible central column framed by generous left and right margins. At the very top, a wide, pill-shaped page controller stretches across most of this central column, floating slightly away from the viewport edges and establishing the futuristic, premium tone. Below it, in the upper-middle of the viewport, a hero-like category intro anchors the page: a bold, large-scale category title and supporting text aligned to the left within the central column, with ample vertical breathing room. Beneath this hero band, a slender, neon-accented filter strip locks in as the primary interaction zone, followed by a concise summary of active filters and a subtle results meta bar. The main body of the page is a tall, vertically scrolling stack of service package cards arranged in a single-column grid within the same left-aligned content column, each card surrounded by generous negative space. At the bottom of this column, centered horizontally, sit the pagination controls, visually isolated with extra vertical padding, acting as a soft stopping point for each page of results. If no results are found, the middle of the screen is taken over by a minimal, centered empty state panel, floating against the dark background and framed by the hero and filter band above, preserving the cinematic, editorial feel of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the very top of the page, the page controller appears as a wide, pill-shaped bar centered horizontally within the viewport. It spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving 10–15% margin on each side, and sits within the same central column used by the rest of the content. Vertically, it occupies a shallow band (about 10–12% of viewport height), with 24–32px padding inside the pill and approximately 32–48px of negative space above it before the top edge of the viewport, giving it a floating, hero-nav-like presence. The component contents (e.g., category selector, global view toggles) are laid out along a horizontal axis inside the pill, aligned slightly left of center to echo the left-aligned text column below while still feeling centered overall.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page, giving way to the filter bar as the main persistent control band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the page controller, the category intro section forms a hero block occupying roughly the next 30–40% of the viewport height on initial load. It is constrained to the central content column (about 60–70% of the viewport width) and aligned flush to the left edge of that column, leaving 15–20% side margins. The main category headline is large and bold, placed in the upper half of this block, with a generous vertical gap of about 24–32px between the headline and any supporting subheading. A thin vertical neon bar sits to the immediate left of the subheading or description line, inset from the content column’s left edge by about 8–16px, visually tying back to the neon line from the original hero mockup. There is substantial negative space below the intro text (48–64px) before the filter bar begins, preserving the feeling of a spacious, cinematic hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down, serving as an initial orientation band rather than a persistent control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service filter bar sits immediately below the category intro section, separated by a clear vertical gap of about 48px. It spans the same central content column width (60–70% of the viewport) and is horizontally centered, with controls aligned primarily to the left within this column. The bar itself reads as a low, horizontal strip with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, containing key filter dropdowns, toggles, or chips laid out in a single row that can wrap gracefully to a second line if needed. The top edge of this bar visually lines up with the left edge of the hero text above, maintaining a strong vertical column. The area above and below the bar is relatively open (24–32px to the intro above, 16–24px to the active-filters-summary below), making the filter band feel like a distinct, neon-accented control layer rather than crowded utility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Becomes sticky once scrolled to the top of the viewport, remaining fixed at the top within the page’s central column area so that filtering controls are always available while the user scrolls the results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filters summary is positioned directly beneath the service filter bar, aligned to the same left edge within the central content column. It occupies a short horizontal band, about the height of one or two lines of text plus any filter chips, and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width like the filter bar above. There is a modest vertical gap of 16–24px separating it from the filter bar, keeping them visually related but distinct. Active filter chips or labels are laid out left-aligned on a single line that can wrap, with ample horizontal and vertical spacing between chips (8–16px), preventing visual clutter. A subtle neon-accent line or small icon to the left of the first chip can echo the vertical neon bar from the hero, reinforcing continuity without overwhelming the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page and sits just below the sticky filter bar when the user scrolls; it does not stick itself, allowing it to move out of view as results take focus.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results meta bar sits just below the active filters summary, separated by a slim vertical gap of about 16px. It is aligned to the same left edge and spans the same central column width (60–70% viewport width). The bar is visually lightweight: a single horizontal row approximately 40–56px tall that may show result count, current sort mode, and view options. The primary meta text (e.g., “24 service packages”) is left-aligned, while any secondary controls (sort, view) sit to the far right within the same row, creating a subtle horizontal tension. Above the meta bar is the more expressive filter area, and below it begins the more content-dense list; this bar visually and functionally bridges those zones while maintaining generous breathing room (24–32px) before the first row of service cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll; it moves with the rest of the content and sits immediately above the scrolling results list, acting as a contextual caption for the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service package list occupies the main body of the page beneath the results meta bar, starting after a vertical spacing of about 24–32px. It remains confined to the same central content column, left-aligned, and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width with consistent 15–20% side margins. On desktop, service packages appear as large, card-like rows stacked vertically in a single column to maintain the editorial, hero-inspired feel, with 24–32px gaps between cards. Each card has generous internal padding (24–32px all around) and stretches nearly the full width of the content column, allowing large typography and imagery to breathe. The list extends downward beyond the fold, with the generous spacing ensuring that even long lists feel intentional and curated rather than dense or grid-like.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Forms the primary scrollable content area of the page. It scrolls beneath the sticky filter bar so that filters remain accessible while browsing, but the list itself is not fixed or paged inside a separate scroll container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls are positioned at the bottom of the service package list, separated from the last card by a clear vertical gap of about 48–64px to create a visual pause. The controls are centered horizontally within the central content column and occupy roughly 40–50% of the viewport width, making them feel compact and focused compared to the full-width cards above. Individual pagination elements (previous/next buttons, page numbers) are spaced generously (8–16px apart) and may appear as pill-shaped buttons, further reflecting the nav-pill language from the hero. There is ample negative space both above and below (32–48px) so that this band reads as a distinct, calm endpoint for the page rather than an appended utility strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the main content flow; it appears only after the user reaches the bottom of the current page of results. It does not stick or float, reinforcing the idea of discrete, cinematic 'pages' of content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no service packages match the current filters, the empty state panel appears in place of the service package list and pagination. It is vertically positioned so that its center roughly aligns with the middle of the viewport after the user scrolls the filter area into view, creating a balanced, floating experience. Horizontally, it is centered within the same central content column and spans about 40–50% of the viewport width, making it narrower than the list cards to emphasize its special state. The panel has generous internal padding (32–48px) and substantial external negative space (48–64px) above and below, with minimal but clear messaging and a primary action. It feels like a quiet, illuminated island in the dark canvas framed by the sticky filter bar above and the wide margins around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in the same vertical slot as the service-package-list and pagination-controls; when active, those components are hidden. It scrolls normally beneath the sticky filter bar and remains centered within the content column.

name: Service Package Detail
title: Service Package
description: Displays full details of a selected XR service package or campaign, and lets users submit a quote request or project brief for that package.

core_features: Retrieve and display full details for a specific ServicePackage using the servicePackageId URL parameter and getServicePackageDetail.
Show key metadata such as package type, technology, participant capacity, expected reach, duration, location constraints, and formatted pricing.
Display descriptive content including short summary, description, objectives, and inclusions in an organized, scannable structure.
Present context-specific primary CTAs (e.g., quote request or project brief) based on the ServicePackage.package_type and/or experience_type.
Provide an inline or modal quote request form bound to submitServicePackageQuoteRequest, pre-linked to the current ServicePackage.
Provide an inline or modal project brief form bound to submitProjectBriefForCampaign, pre-linked to the current ServicePackage for campaigns.
Surface related ServicePackages from getServicePackageDetail.relatedPackages, allowing users to navigate between packages via URL parameters.
Offer breadcrumb navigation back to the parent ServiceCategory using serviceCategory.slug in the URL.
user_workflows:
View service package details:
1. User navigates to service_package.html with a servicePackageId URL parameter from another page.
2. On load, the page reads servicePackageId from the URL.
3. The page calls getServicePackageDetail(servicePackageId).
4. While loading, the main content area shows a loading state specific to this request.
5. On success, the page displays package name, summary, technology, package_type, metadata (participantsLabel, expectedReachLabel, duration_days, location_constraints, displayPrice), and descriptive content (description, objectives, inclusions).
6. Industry tags and relatedPackages from the response are also displayed.
7. If the API returns an error or no active package, the page shows an error state with a non-data-dependent message (e.g., that the package could not be loaded) and no package-specific content.

Submit a quote request for a package:
1. User views a package whose configuration supports quote requests (e.g., based on package_type or price_type).
2. User clicks a quote-related CTA such as 'Request Quote' or 'Add to Quote' in the CTA panel.
3. A quote request form appears (inline or in a modal), pre-bound to the current servicePackage.id.
4. User fills in required fields (such as contact name and email) and optional fields (such as company, phone, number of participants, preferred start date, budget cap, and message).
5. On form submit, client-side validation ensures required fields are present and values are of the correct type (e.g., numeric fields for participants and budget).
6. If validation passes, the component calls submitServicePackageQuoteRequest with the current servicePackage.id and the form values.
7. While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a submission-in-progress state is shown.
8. On success (response.success is true), the form shows a success message using the response.message and may clear the form or close the modal.
9. On failure (response.success is false or network error), an error message is displayed and the user can adjust the form and retry.

Submit a project brief for a marketing or launch campaign:
1. User views a package that is considered a campaign (e.g., package_type == 'marketing_campaign' or experience_type == 'product_launch' or 'brand_experience').
2. The CTA panel shows a 'Start Project Brief' button.
3. User clicks the 'Start Project Brief' CTA, opening a project brief form (inline or in a modal), pre-bound to the current servicePackage.id as relatedServicePackageId.
4. User fills in required fields (such as contact name, brand name, campaign budget, desired launch month) and optional fields (company, email, phone, goals, notes).
5. The component validates that required fields are present and that campaign budget is numeric and desired launch month is a valid date/month selection.
6. On submit, the component calls submitProjectBriefForCampaign with the current relatedServicePackageId and form values.
7. While the request is being processed, submission controls are disabled and a progress indicator is shown.
8. On success, the component displays response.message, may reset the form, and can optionally close the modal.
9. On failure, an error message is shown and the user can correct inputs or retry.

Navigate to related service packages:
1. User scrolls to the 'Related packages' section populated from getServicePackageDetail.relatedPackages.
2. The list shows each related ServicePackage name and key metadata drawn dynamically from the API response.
3. User clicks a related package entry.
4. The browser navigates to service_package.html with a new servicePackageId URL parameter corresponding to the selected related package.
5. The page re-runs the getServicePackageDetail call for the new servicePackageId, showing a loading state.
6. When the new data loads, all bound components update to the newly selected package's information.

Navigate back to service category:
1. User sees breadcrumb navigation built from getServicePackageDetail.serviceCategory.
2. User clicks the ServiceCategory breadcrumb item.
3. The browser navigates to service_category.html with serviceId set to serviceCategory.slug.
4. The Service Category page (outside the scope of this spec) uses this slug to display the relevant listing.
