interactions: On page load, read servicePackageId from the URL; if present, trigger getServicePackageDetail and manage loading/error states.
On change of servicePackageId in the URL (e.g., via navigation to another related package), re-fetch package details using getServicePackageDetail.
On click of breadcrumb category item, navigate to service_category.html?serviceId={serviceCategory.slug}.
On click of the primary quote-related CTA, open the quote request form, pre-bound to the current ServicePackage.
On submit of the quote request form, validate inputs and call submitServicePackageQuoteRequest; then display success or error feedback.
On click of the 'Start Project Brief' CTA, open the project brief form modal, pre-bound to the current ServicePackage.
On submit of the project brief form, validate inputs and call submitProjectBriefForCampaign; then display success or error feedback.
On click of a related package card or list item, navigate to service_package.html?servicePackageId={relatedServicePackage.id} and refresh the page data.
On dismiss of modals (quote request or project brief), close the modal and keep the underlying package state unchanged.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the servicePackageId URL parameter. When service_package.html loads, it reads servicePackageId from the query string. If servicePackageId exists, the page calls getServicePackageDetail(servicePackageId) and enters a 'loading' state until the API resolves. Once getServicePackageDetail returns, the following state is populated: ServicePackage (core details), ServiceCategory (for breadcrumbs and context), industryTags (for informational tags), displayPrice (for price display), participantsLabel and expectedReachLabel (for human-readable participation/reach), and relatedPackages (for navigation). If the API call fails or returns no active ServicePackage, the page enters an 'error' state where package-specific components show a generic error message and no package-dependent data.

When a user clicks a related package, the navigation changes the URL to include a new servicePackageId. The page can either fully reload or detect the URL change via a routing layer; in both cases, the new servicePackageId triggers a fresh call to getServicePackageDetail and resets loading/error states. No state is stored in localStorage or any persistent client storage; all state is kept in memory within components and is re-derived from the URL and API responses.

For forms, each modal maintains its own ephemeral state: form field values, validation errors, and submission states. On submit, each form transitions into a 'submitting' state until submitServicePackageQuoteRequest or submitProjectBriefForCampaign resolves. The result updates form-specific success/error states using response.success and response.message. Closing a modal can optionally reset its internal state. These form states do not affect the global page state and do not change the URL, keeping navigation and data loading strictly tied to the servicePackageId parameter.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the servicePackageId URL parameter, calling getServicePackageDetail, and distributing the resulting data and loading/error states to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read servicePackageId from URL and call getServicePackageDetail(servicePackageId).
onServicePackageIdChange: When the URL's servicePackageId changes, re-call getServicePackageDetail and reset loading and error states.
onLoadSuccess: Store the returned data in shared state for other components.
onLoadError: Set an error flag and message to be consumed by error-aware components.

components.functionality: Displays breadcrumb navigation linking back to the parent ServiceCategory and indicating the current ServicePackage.
event_handlers: onCurrentPackageClick: Optionally no-op or reload current servicePackage.html with the same servicePackageId.

components.functionality: Shows the main identifying information for the service package such as name, short summary, technology, package_type, experience_type, and industryTags.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Update displayed content whenever new ServicePackage data is loaded.

components.functionality: Displays key metadata relevant to scoping and feasibility, such as participant capacity, expected reach, duration, location constraints, and pricing details.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Recalculate which metadata elements to show/hide based on presence of fields (e.g., hide expected reach if no expected_reach).
onStatusChange: If ServicePackage.status is not 'active', display a non-data-specific unavailable state.

components.functionality: Organizes descriptive fields (description, objectives, inclusions) into sections or tabs to make content easier to scan.
event_handlers: onTabChange: Switch between description, objectives, and inclusions without reloading data.
onDataChange: Update tab contents when a new ServicePackage is loaded.

components.functionality: Determines which primary action buttons to display ('Request Quote', 'Add to Quote', 'Start Project Brief') based on the ServicePackage properties and opens the appropriate forms.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Evaluate ServicePackage fields to decide which CTAs are available (e.g., show project brief for marketing campaigns, show quote for training packages or custom_quote pricing).
onRequestQuoteClick: Trigger opening of the quote-request-modal component, passing the current ServicePackage.id.
onAddToQuoteClick: Trigger the same behavior as request quote or a lighter variant of the quote form, still bound to submitServicePackageQuoteRequest.
onStartProjectBriefClick: Trigger opening of the project-brief-modal component, passing the current ServicePackage.id as relatedServicePackageId.

components.functionality: Collects and submits quote requests tied to the current ServicePackage using submitServicePackageQuoteRequest.
event_handlers: onOpen: Initialize form state; bind the current ServicePackage.id internally as servicePackageId.
onInputChange: Update local form state for each field as the user types or selects values.

onSubmit: Validate required fields (e.g., contactName, email) and types (e.g., numeric fields for participants and budget). If valid, call submitServicePackageQuoteRequest with { servicePackageId: ServicePackage.id, contactName, company, email, phone, numberOfParticipants, preferredStartDate, budgetCap, message }.
onSubmitSuccess: When response.success is true, show a success confirmation using response.message and optionally reset or close the form.
onSubmitError: Show an error message if response.success is false or if the API call fails.
onClose: Close the modal and optionally clear local form state.

components.functionality: Collects and submits project briefs for campaigns using submitProjectBriefForCampaign, pre-linked to the current ServicePackage as relatedServicePackageId.
event_handlers: onOpen: Initialize form state and bind relatedServicePackageId = ServicePackage.id.
onInputChange: Update local project brief field values as the user interacts with the form.

onSubmit: Validate required fields (contactName, brandName, campaignBudget, desiredLaunchMonth) and that campaignBudget is numeric. If valid, call submitProjectBriefForCampaign with { relatedServicePackageId: ServicePackage.id, contactName, brandName, company, email, phone, campaignBudget, desiredLaunchMonth, goals, notes }.
onSubmitSuccess: Display response.message, set a success state, and optionally reset/close the modal.
onSubmitError: Display a generic error state using response.message or fallback messaging.
onClose: Close the modal and clear internal form state.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related ServicePackages returned by getServicePackageDetail.relatedPackages and allows navigation to their detail pages.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Update the list whenever relatedPackages changes due to a new parent package.

components.functionality: Shows user-friendly error messages when getServicePackageDetail fails or returns an inactive/invalid ServicePackage.
event_handlers: onRetryClick: Re-call getServicePackageDetail with the current servicePackageId.
onDataChange: Hide error state when fresh valid data is loaded.

components.functionality: Provides consistent feedback for form submissions (both quote request and project brief), showing loading, success, or error status.
event_handlers: onQuoteRequestSubmit: Show a submitting indicator for the quote request until a response is received.
onQuoteRequestResponse: Display success or error status specific to the quote request flow.
onProjectBriefSubmit: Show a submitting indicator for the project brief until a response is received.
onProjectBriefResponse: Display success or error status specific to the project brief flow.
onDismiss: Allow users to dismiss transient feedback messages without affecting underlying data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a vertically stacked, single primary content column inside a centered, full-width hero-like section. At the very top of the viewport, a pill-shaped breadcrumb/navigation element echoes the hero nav pill from the mockup. Below it, still within the same central column, the service package name and key summary form a bold, left-aligned header block occupying the upper-middle of the viewport. Immediately beneath, a compact metadata strip and then a larger description/tabbed-content area continue down the same left-aligned column. A visually distinct CTA panel is positioned in the lower-right portion of the central content area, partially overlapping the mid and lower portions of the page content on desktop, but always visually detached from the main text column. Related packages sit further down in their own spacious section. Overlays (loader, modals, feedback) sit above this structure, maintaining the centered, cinematic feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a vertically stacked, single primary content column inside a centered, full-width hero-like section. At the very top of the viewport, a pill-shaped breadcrumb/navigation element echoes the hero nav pill from the mockup. Below it, still within the same central column, the service package name and key summary form a bold, left-aligned header block occupying the upper-middle of the viewport. Immediately beneath, a compact metadata strip and then a larger description/tabbed-content area continue down the same left-aligned column. A visually distinct CTA panel is positioned in the lower-right portion of the central content area, partially overlapping the mid and lower portions of the page content on desktop, but always visually detached from the main text column. Related packages sit further down in their own spacious section. Overlays (loader, modals, feedback) sit above this structure, maintaining the centered, cinematic feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content container spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered horizontally, leaving 15–20% dark margins on the left and right. The pill-shaped breadcrumb/nav at the top occupies around 10–15% of the vertical viewport height. The main package header and immediate metadata block occupy roughly the next 30–40% of the viewport, concentrated in the left half of the central container. The lower part of the first viewport is mostly occupied by the beginning of the description tabs, with generous vertical spacing above and below each block. The CTA panel occupies about 25–30% of the central container’s width on desktop and sits toward the right side, starting around the mid-height of the page and extending into the lower half of the viewport as the user scrolls. The related-packages section occupies its own band below the fold with a generous 64–96px margin separating it from the main content. Overlays (loader, modals, feedback) generally span 100% width and height for dimming, with their content constrained around 40–50% width and centered.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is intentionally low-density and cinematic. Each component—breadcrumbs pill, header, metadata strip, tabs, CTA, related list—has ample breathing room, with 32–64px vertical spacing between major sections and at least 24–40px internal padding for pill- or card-shaped surfaces. The central text column rarely spans the full container width; instead it uses roughly two-thirds of the content width, leaving soft margins within the container and strong outer margins against the page edges. Non-critical text (metadata, related items) appears lighter and more compact but is never crowded. The overall feel prioritizes clarity and visual impact over information density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the pill-shaped breadcrumb/navigation at the top center, which anchors the sense of location within the site. From there, the visual flow drops directly down to the bold, left-aligned service package title in the upper-middle of the viewport. The viewer’s gaze continues downward to the supporting summary and metadata panel, subtly guided by a neon vertical accent line at the left edge of key text blocks. Below that, the tabbed description area invites deeper exploration, while the CTA panel is intentionally offset to the right and slightly lower, acting as a secondary but persistent call to action in peripheral vision. As the user scrolls, attention eventually shifts to the related packages grid at the bottom. During interaction, modals and feedback overlays temporarily override this flow by taking center stage, but remain minimal and aligned with the existing vertical axis.

layout.overall_description: The Service Package Detail page reads like a dark, editorial hero extended into a detail view. At the very top of the viewport, a wide, pill-shaped breadcrumb bar floats in the center, echoing the nav pill from the original mockup and establishing context with minimal text. Below this, content snaps into a left-aligned column within a centered container. The service package title dominates the upper-middle of the screen in large, high-contrast type, occupying only about two-thirds of the container width and leaving rich, matte-black margins at both sides. Directly beneath, a short, refined summary paragraph and a compact metadata strip line up to the same left edge, accentuated by a thin neon vertical bar that acts as a visual spine. As the user scrolls, the description area unfolds in tabbed sections below the metadata, maintaining the same alignment and spacious rhythm, while a neon-edged CTA panel hovers toward the right side of the content, slightly offset and often partially overlapping the mid-to-lower viewport as a sticky element. The bottom of the page opens into a roomy related-packages grid framed by the same left and right margins, each card breathing within dark negative space. When loaders, modals, or feedback appear, they do so as centered, glowing surfaces suspended over the matte-black background, preserving the premium, immersive atmosphere while briefly reordering the visual hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader covers the entire viewport as a fixed, full-bleed overlay on top of the dark background and all page content. A semi-opaque black layer (60–70% opacity) mutes everything beneath. At the center of the screen—both vertically and horizontally—a minimal, neon-accented loading indicator (spinner or subtle XR-themed glyph) appears, roughly 10–15% of the viewport width on desktop. The indicator is surrounded by generous empty space, with any loading text set directly below it in small, light typography. No other page elements are visually competing; the loader is the only focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport and full-screen; appears above all other components as a blocking overlay until data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs sit at the very top of the content hierarchy, inside a horizontally centered, pill-shaped bar that spans approximately 60–70% of the viewport width. This pill is positioned with its top edge about 32–48px below the browser chrome, occupying roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport height. Inside the pill, the breadcrumb text is left-aligned, starting about 24–32px from the left inner edge, while the right side of the pill remains mostly empty, reinforcing the minimalist feel. The pill’s vertical height is about 56–72px, with 16–24px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding. On larger screens, the pill appears to float over the matte-black background, casting a soft shadow or glow, and acts as the entry point into the page’s context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal page flow on scroll; it scrolls away with the rest of the content, maintaining initial prominence only at the top of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service package header occupies the upper-middle segment of the first viewport, directly beneath the breadcrumbs pill. There is a generous 48–64px vertical gap between the bottom of the breadcrumbs pill and the top of the header text, reinforcing the sense of a distinct hero band. The header content is placed inside the central content container, which is centered on the page and spans about 60–70% of the viewport width. Within this container, the text is left-aligned, with the main package title set in large, bold type spanning roughly 60–70% of the container’s width (approximately 40–50% of the total page width), leaving a substantial right-side open margin. If a short label or category is part of the header, it appears above the title in smaller type with a 8–16px gap, aligned to the same left edge. Below the title, an optional single-sentence summary sits with 16–24px vertical separation, forming a tight but airy block. The entire header block occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport height on initial load, anchoring the user visually before they scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main flow; acts as the primary hero content, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metadata panel sits immediately below the header block, sharing the same left alignment within the central content container. It begins about 24–32px beneath the header summary text, creating a clear but tight connection. A thin neon vertical bar, 2–3px wide, runs along the left edge of the metadata panel, starting in line with the first metadata row and extending down the full height of the panel; the metadata text is indented 16–24px from this bar. The panel itself uses roughly 60–70% of the container width, mirroring the width of the header above. Metadata items (e.g., duration, delivery format, budget range) are arranged in 1–2 compact rows, either as a single line of separated segments or as two stacked lines depending on available width. Vertical spacing inside the panel is modest but not cramped, with 8–16px between rows. The overall height of the metadata panel is relatively low—around 15–20% of the header height—ensuring it reads as a secondary info strip rather than a large block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, tightly coupled to the header and preceding the tabbed content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description tabs section begins approximately 40–56px below the bottom edge of the metadata panel, marking a transition from overview to detailed content. It occupies the same central content container, again spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width and aligned to the left. The tab labels form a horizontal row aligned with the left edge of the header and metadata text. Each tab label has generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and vertical padding (12–16px), with 24–32px spacing between tabs, and is underlined or highlighted using the neon accent when active. The tab strip itself consumes about 48–64px in height. Below the tab row, a large content area fills the remainder of the visible section, occupying the full width of the container under the tabs but still honoring the right-side inner margin. This content area has 24–32px padding from the top of the tab strip and 32–48px padding at the bottom, allowing paragraphs, bullet lists, and media to sit in an airy, editorial-style column. The initial portion of this tab content typically occupies the lower part of the first viewport and continues below the fold as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the main scrollable body of the detail page; tab switching changes content within the same spatial footprint without moving surrounding elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The CTA panel is a visually distinct, card- or pill-shaped block that sits offset from the main text column, occupying the right side of the central content area on desktop screens. Horizontally, it aligns so that its left edge starts approximately at the midpoint of the central content container, covering about 25–30% of the container’s width and leaving a slim margin to the right frame of the page. Vertically, the top of the CTA panel is roughly aligned with the upper portion of the description tabs content—starting either just below the tabs or overlapping the first fold of tab content—so that it appears in view shortly after the user begins to scroll but remains clearly detached from the main text. Inside, the panel uses generous padding (24–32px on all sides) and contains a concise headline, a short supporting line, and two primary buttons (‘Request a Quote’, ‘Submit Project Brief’) stacked or side-by-side with 16–24px spacing. The panel is outlined or edge-lit with the neon accent, giving it a tech-forward glow while maintaining the dark background. On smaller screens, this panel collapses to full-width and is placed inline below the description tabs content instead of floating to the side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the CTA panel behaves as a sticky element within its section: it remains pinned within the right side of the viewport as the user scrolls through the tabbed content, until the bottom of its containing section is reached. On mobile/tablet, it becomes a normal inline block with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When triggered, the quote request modal appears as a centered, floating panel above a dimmed full-screen backdrop. The backdrop is a matte-black overlay with 60–70% opacity over the page content. The modal itself is horizontally centered and vertically positioned slightly above the true center of the viewport (by about 5–10% of viewport height) to feel intentional and energetic. Its width spans roughly 40–50% of the viewport on desktop, with a max width matching the central content container. The modal has 32–40px padding on all sides, with the main title aligned left at the top, followed by a brief instruction line and the form fields stacked in a single column. Individual fields are spaced 16–24px apart, maintaining the luxurious negative space. Primary and secondary actions sit at the bottom right of the modal content. The edges of the modal are crisp, with a subtle neon border or glow that connects it to the rest of the design language.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport as an overlay; it disables interaction with underlying content while open and scrolls internally if the form height exceeds the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project brief modal uses the same overlay structure as the quote request modal: a full-screen, semi-opaque dark backdrop with a centered panel. It is positioned similarly—centered horizontally and slightly above vertical center—and uses roughly 50–60% of the viewport width to accommodate potentially longer form content. Within the modal, a left-aligned title and brief description appear at the top, followed by a vertically stacked form emphasizing larger text areas for project description, goals, and requirements. Each field is generously spaced (16–24px apart) and maintains a comfortable margin from the edges of the modal. The submit and cancel actions sit at the bottom right, with extra padding (24–32px) below them for breathing room. The modal’s edges and focus states for form fields incorporate the neon accent, but the overall layout remains minimal and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full-viewport overlay with internal scroll when necessary, identical backdrop behavior to the quote-request modal.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related packages list occupies a dedicated section near the bottom of the page, clearly separated from the main package content by a large vertical gap of 64–96px. The section shares the same central content container width (60–70% of the viewport) and left alignment as the main content. At the top of this section, a small, left-aligned section label or heading appears with 16–24px spacing above the first row of cards. The related packages themselves are displayed as 2–3 large, card-like tiles per row (depending on viewport width), each card occupying roughly 30–45% of the container width with 24–32px horizontal gaps between them and 32–40px vertical spacing between rows. Cards maintain the dark, matte background with neon-accented overlays or edges and have 24–32px internal padding. The entire section may occupy 40–60% of a typical screen height, but with enough negative space above and below to feel like a distinct, optional exploration zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing after the main content and CTA; it scrolls into view as a natural conclusion to the detail page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state replaces the main hero and detail content area when a service package cannot be loaded or retrieved. It appears within the same central content container, centered both horizontally and vertically within the first viewport (or within the central column if other global elements remain). The error message block is left-aligned within the container but positioned so that its overall block is visually centered vertically. A short, clear error headline appears in bold type, followed by a single line or two of explanatory copy beneath, separated by 12–16px. A slim, neon-accented button or link to retry or go back to the package list sits 24–32px below the text. The surrounding space is almost entirely empty, reinforcing the sense of interruption and maintaining the cinematic aesthetic. The breadcrumbs pill may still appear above, slightly dimmed in importance, or can be hidden depending on implementation, but no metadata, tabs, or CTA panel are shown.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the primary content area, occupying the space where header and tabs would normally render; it scrolls normally if additional global content exists above or below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Form submission feedback appears as a slim, pill-shaped notification that floats above the lower center of the viewport, echoing the scroll indicator’s alignment from the original mockup. It is horizontally centered and positioned about 40–56px above the bottom edge of the screen. The pill spans roughly 30–40% of the viewport width on desktop, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Text is centered within the pill and kept to a single line where possible, using high-contrast type. Success feedback uses a subtle neon glow reinforcing the positive state, while error feedback may use a more intense neon hue. The rest of the page remains visible and slightly dimmed only if necessary; otherwise, the pill floats non-intrusively over active content. On smaller screens, it still appears in the bottom center, but may extend to 60–80% width for readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport, overlaying other components without blocking interaction; appears transiently after form submission and fades out or can be dismissed by the user.

name: Pop-up Installation Configurator
title: Pop-up Installation Configurator
description: Configure an immersive pop-up installation by entering event details, selecting experience modules, and requesting a quote based on a dynamically calculated cost estimate.

core_features: Initialize the configurator by loading the pop-up installations service category, available experience modules, and default audience/date hints using getPopupConfiguratorOptions.
Pre-fill configuration fields (location, audience range, event date) from incoming URL parameters when present.
Allow users to input and update core event details such as location, event date, and expected audience range.
Allow users to select one or more experience modules, adjust quantities, and optionally add custom labels per module using the experienceModules data.
Build a configuration object from current form state and call calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate to dynamically calculate an estimated total cost and receive a module-level price breakdown.
Display the current estimated total, currency, and per-module line items, updating in response to configuration changes.
Allow users to optionally enter a target budget range and visually indicate whether the current estimate is below, within, or above the target range (purely client-side guidance).
Provide a 'Save & Request Quote' action that reveals a short contact form (name, company, email, optional phone).
On contact form submission, call savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote with the current configuration and contact details to create an ExperienceConfiguration and a related QuoteRequest.

Display a confirmation state that surfaces key details returned from savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote (such as estimated total and identifiers) and confirms that the configuration has been saved and submitted.
Expose a supplemental CTA to navigate to contact.html with inquiryType=project_quote for users who want to further discuss the saved configuration.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time configuration without pre-filled URL parameters
1. User lands on popup_configurator.html without URL parameters.
2. On page initialization, the configurator calls getPopupConfiguratorOptions.
3. The page stores the returned serviceCategory, experienceModules, audienceRangeDefaults, and dateRangeHint in memory state.
4. The event basics form displays empty inputs for location and event date and suggests audience defaults based on audienceRangeDefaults.
5. The user enters a location and selects an event date within the guidance from dateRangeHint, then enters minimum and maximum expected audience values.
6. The user opens the module selection component, browses the experienceModules list, and selects one or more modules, specifying quantities and optional custom labels.
7. Each time the user updates event basics or module selections, the page rebuilds the configuration object and calls calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate (with debouncing or only after required fields are present).
8. The estimate summary updates with the latest estimatedTotal, currency, and moduleBreakdown line items.
9. The user optionally enters a target budget range to see if the estimate falls below, within, or above that range (client-side comparison only).
10. When satisfied, the user clicks 'Save & Request Quote', revealing the contact form.
11. The user enters contact name, company, email, and optionally phone, then submits.
12. The page calls savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote with the current configuration and contact details.
13. On success, the page displays a confirmation panel summarizing key ExperienceConfiguration and QuoteRequest information and disables further editing of the submitted configuration (or clearly indicates it is now submitted).

Workflow 2: Configuration with pre-filled URL parameters
1. User arrives from service_category.html or index.html via a link that includes one or more of the URL parameters: location, audienceMin, audienceMax, eventDate.
2. On page initialization, the configurator parses the URL parameters and stores them in initial state.
3. The page then calls getPopupConfiguratorOptions and merges the returned defaults with the URL-provided values: pre-filling location, audience_min, audience_max, and event_date inputs if those parameters are present and valid.
4. The user reviews the pre-filled values and can modify them as needed.
5. The user selects modules and quantities in the module selection component.
6. As the user updates inputs, the configurator builds a configuration object from the merged state and calls calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate to get the current estimatedTotal and moduleBreakdown.
7. The estimate summary shows the updated totals, and the user adjusts modules or audience inputs until satisfied.
8. The user proceeds to 'Save & Request Quote', fills in contact details, and submits.
9. The configurator calls savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote, then shows confirmation with the ExperienceConfiguration and QuoteRequest details.

Workflow 3: Adjusting configuration to fit a target budget
1. User has already entered location, date, and audience information and selected some modules.
2. After an estimate is calculated via calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate, the user enters a desired minimum and maximum budget range into the budget guidance fields.
3. The UI compares estimatedTotal to this budget range on the client side and displays a status message (e.g., under budget, near target, over target) without calling any additional interfaces.
4. If the estimate is above the user's target range, the user returns to the module selection component to lower module quantities or remove modules.
5. Each change triggers a re-calculation via calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate.
6. The user iterates until the estimate falls within or near the desired budget range.
7. Once satisfied, the user continues with 'Save & Request Quote' and submits contact details to save the configuration and create a QuoteRequest.

Workflow 4: Navigating to contact page after saving a quote
1. After a successful save, the confirmation panel includes a supplemental CTA like 'Discuss this configuration with us'.
2. The user clicks this CTA.
3. The configurator navigates the browser to contact.html with the URL parameter inquiryType set to project_quote.
4. The user can then continue the conversation from the contact page, referencing the confirmation details shown on the configurator page (such as configuration or quote identifiers).
interactions: On page load: Parse URL parameters (location, audienceMin, audienceMax, eventDate) and store them in initial state, then call getPopupConfiguratorOptions. After the API response arrives, merge defaults (audienceRangeDefaults, dateRangeHint) with URL-based pre-fill values and populate the form fields.

On location input change: Update the in-memory configuration state for location_display and any derived fields (e.g., city/country if parsed client-side). Optionally update the URL parameter 'location' to match the current value, then trigger recalculation via calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate if other required fields and at least one module selection are present.

On event date change: Validate the selected date against dateRangeHint, update configuration state for event_date, optionally update the 'eventDate' URL parameter, and trigger calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate under the same conditions.

On audience minimum or maximum change: Enforce simple client-side validation (e.g., min ≤ max, positive values). Update audience_min and audience_max configuration state, optionally synchronize 'audienceMin' and 'audienceMax' URL parameters, and trigger calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate when valid.

On module selection (add module): When a user selects a module from the list, create a module selection entry in local configuration state including experience_module_id, initial quantity, and optional custom_label. Trigger calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate with the updated configuration.

On module quantity change: Validate the quantity (e.g., not negative, optionally checking against capacity_min and capacity_max from the selected ExperienceModule if available). Update the module entry in configuration state and call calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate with the new configuration.

On module removal: Remove the module selection entry from configuration state. If no modules remain, clear the current estimate and skip calling calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate until modules are added again.

On budget range input change: Update a local 'budgetRange' state object (not persisted via interfaces) and re-evaluate the comparison between estimatedTotal and the user's budget. Update visual indicators accordingly without calling any API.

On configuration state change that warrants recalculation: Build a lightweight configuration object containing current location, event date, audience_min, audience_max, and selected modules (with experience_module_id, quantity, and any custom labels). Call calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate with this object and update estimate-related state (estimatedTotal, currency, moduleBreakdown) when the response is received.

On 'Save & Request Quote' primary button click: If the contact form is currently hidden, expand it and scroll into view. If it is visible and all required contact fields are valid, trigger form submission behavior instead.

On contact form submit: Validate required fields (contactName, email) and ensure that a current estimate is available. Build the configuration object from the latest state (the same structure used for calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate, but including any additional notes if present). Call savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote with configuration, contactName, company, email, and phone.

On successful savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote response: Store the returned experienceConfiguration and quoteRequest objects in page state. Show a confirmation panel that references these objects (for example, displaying the configuration location, event date, estimated_total, and any identifiers). Optionally disable further edits or clearly label that any subsequent changes would require a new quote submission.

On failed API response (for any interface): Display an inline error message in the relevant component (e.g., loader, estimator, or save form). Provide a retry action that re-invokes the failed interface with the last known parameters.
On 'Discuss this configuration with us' CTA click: Navigate to contact.html, appending the query parameter inquiryType=project_quote to the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters solely for initial state and optional state reflection, not for persistent storage. On initial load, the configurator reads location, audienceMin, audienceMax, and eventDate from the URL query string. If present and valid, these values are used to pre-populate the corresponding configuration fields (location, audience_min, audience_max, event_date) before or immediately after getPopupConfiguratorOptions returns. The page may also update the browser's URL to reflect current form state, such as when the user edits location or audience ranges, by replacing or adding these same parameters in the query string. This allows users to share a link that re-creates the basic configuration inputs. No additional IDs (such as ExperienceConfiguration.id or QuoteRequest.id) are loaded from URL parameters, because re-loading an existing configuration is not supported by the assigned interfaces. The detailed configuration, selection of modules, and estimate results are stored only in in-memory page state during the session. When navigating away from the page or refreshing without URL parameters, this in-memory state is lost and must be re-entered. All calls to getPopupConfiguratorOptions, calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate, and savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote are based on the current in-memory representation of the configuration, not on any value retrieved from localStorage or other persistent client storage.

components.functionality: Handles initial page setup: parses URL parameters, calls getPopupConfiguratorOptions, and populates the root configuration state with serviceCategory, available experienceModules, and default audience/date hints merged with any pre-filled URL values.
event_handlers: onInit: parse URL parameters (location, audienceMin, audienceMax, eventDate) and store them in temporary initial state.
onInit: call getPopupConfiguratorOptions and, when resolved, set page-level state for serviceCategory, experienceModules, audienceRangeDefaults, and dateRangeHint.
onInit: after merging defaults with URL params, initialize configuration state fields (location_display, event_date, audience_min, audience_max) used by other components.
onError: if getPopupConfiguratorOptions fails, display an error state and expose a retry action that re-runs onInit.

components.functionality: Collects and manages core event details for the configuration, including location and event date, using defaults from getPopupConfiguratorOptions and optional URL pre-fill.
event_handlers: onLocationChange: update configuration.location_display (and optional derived fields like location_city and location_country) in page state; optionally update the 'location' URL parameter; notify the estimator component that configuration state has changed.
onEventDateChange: validate against dateRangeHint constraints, update configuration.event_date in page state; optionally update 'eventDate' URL parameter; notify the estimator component.
onNotesChange: update configuration.notes in page state for inclusion in the saved configuration (not used by calculation unless backend chooses to).

components.functionality: Captures minimum and maximum expected audience/visitor counts and applies default ranges when appropriate.
event_handlers: onMount: if audienceRangeDefaults is available and URL parameters audienceMin/audienceMax are absent, pre-fill audience_min and audience_max fields using audienceRangeDefaults.

onAudienceMinChange: validate that the value is non-negative and not greater than audience_max; update configuration.audience_min; optionally update 'audienceMin' URL parameter; trigger a recalculation request to the estimator component.

onAudienceMaxChange: validate that the value is ≥ audience_min; update configuration.audience_max; optionally update 'audienceMax' URL parameter; trigger a recalculation request to the estimator component.

components.functionality: Displays all available experience modules and lets users add, remove, and adjust module selections (including quantities and optional custom labels) for the configuration.
event_handlers: onModulesLoad: receive the experienceModules array from config-page-initializer and render selectable items.

onAddModule: when a user selects a module, append an entry to a configuration.modules array in page state with experience_module_id, default quantity, and optional custom_label; notify the estimator component.

onQuantityChange: for a selected module, validate and update the quantity; update the corresponding ExperienceConfigurationModule-like state entry used when calling calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate.
onRemoveModule: remove the module entry from configuration.modules; notify the estimator component; if this results in no modules selected, request that the estimator clear the current estimate.
onCustomLabelChange: update the custom_label for the corresponding module selection in configuration.modules.

components.functionality: Allows users to input their own desired minimum and maximum budget to compare against the dynamically calculated estimate, providing non-binding guidance only.
event_handlers: onBudgetMinChange: update local budgetRange.min state and re-evaluate the comparison with the current estimate (if available).
onBudgetMaxChange: update local budgetRange.max state and re-evaluate the comparison with the current estimate (if available).
onEstimateUpdate: whenever the estimator component provides a new estimatedTotal, recompute the guidance state (e.g., under/within/over target) using the local budgetRange.

components.functionality: Consumes the current configuration state, calls calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate when needed, and displays the resulting estimated total cost, currency, and per-module price breakdown.
event_handlers: onConfigurationChange: when notified by forms or module selector that relevant configuration fields have changed and at least one module is selected, build a configuration object and invoke calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate.
onCalculatePopupConfigurationEstimate: send the current configuration (location, event_date, audience_min, audience_max, and selected modules with IDs and quantities) to the interface.
onEstimateResponse: store estimatedTotal, currency, and moduleBreakdown in local state and display them; notify the budget-guidance-panel of the new estimate.
onNoModulesSelected: clear any existing estimate display and avoid calling calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate until modules are added.
onError: if calculatePopupConfigurationEstimate fails, show an inline error with a retry action that re-uses the last configuration object.

components.functionality: Collects contact details and, when submitted, saves the current configuration and creates a quote request via savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote.
event_handlers: onPrimaryButtonClick: if the contact form is collapsed, expand it; if already expanded and the form is valid, trigger onSubmit.
onContactNameChange: update local contactName state and run validation.
onEmailChange: update local email state and run validation.

onSubmit: verify that required contact fields are valid and that a current estimate exists; construct a configuration payload from the latest configuration state and call savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote(configuration, contactName, company, email, phone).

onSaveResponseSuccess: store the returned experienceConfiguration and quoteRequest in state; emit an event to show the confirmation panel; optionally lock further edits or mark the configuration as submitted_for_quote in UI.
onSaveResponseError: display an error message and enable retry, which re-invokes savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote with the same parameters.

components.functionality: Displays a confirmation message and key data from the successfully saved ExperienceConfiguration and QuoteRequest, signaling to the user that their configuration and quote request are recorded.
event_handlers: onShow: render after a successful savePopupConfigurationAndRequestQuote call, using experienceConfiguration and quoteRequest data to display location, event date, audience range, estimated_total, and any identifiers provided.
onEditNewConfiguration: optionally allow the user to start a new configuration by resetting mutable configuration state while keeping the confirmation visible or archived in the session.

components.functionality: Provides a supplemental call-to-action link for users who want to further discuss their configuration, navigating them to the contact page configured for project quote inquiries.
event_handlers: onClick: navigate the browser to contact.html?inquiryType=project_quote.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: All configurator components are arranged in a single, vertical narrative inside a centered content container that echoes the hero: a wide matte-black canvas with a left-aligned column of content. The core forms (event basics, audience, modules, budget, quote request) stack one after another, each treated as a distinct 'scene' with its own vertical breathing room. A few elements (loading, summary, final CTA) intentionally detach slightly from this column (overlay, sticky, or centered at the bottom) to echo the mockup’s detached scroll indicator behavior while keeping the main interaction column consistent.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All configurator components are arranged in a single, vertical narrative inside a centered content container that echoes the hero: a wide matte-black canvas with a left-aligned column of content. The core forms (event basics, audience, modules, budget, quote request) stack one after another, each treated as a distinct 'scene' with its own vertical breathing room. A few elements (loading, summary, final CTA) intentionally detach slightly from this column (overlay, sticky, or centered at the bottom) to echo the mockup’s detached scroll indicator behavior while keeping the main interaction column consistent.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the central content container spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with 15–20% dark margins on either side to create a framed, cinematic feel. Within that container, most forms occupy about 55–65% of the total page width, anchored to the left. Where a secondary panel appears (e.g., estimate summary), the left column holds the interactive forms (~60% of container) and the right sub-column holds the summary (~40%), but both still sit inside the same central container. Vertically, each major step takes up about 50–70% of the viewport height, with generous gaps (64–96px) between major components. The bottom of the page preserves a relatively empty band of 15–20% viewport height, visually echoing the hero’s empty lower area and foregrounding the final CTA.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains intentionally low: each component is given ample top and bottom margin, and internal padding is generous (around 16–24px vertical and 24–40px horizontal) to feel premium and uncluttered. Only one or two primary interactive elements are clearly emphasized within each visible viewport segment. Section titles are bold and oversized relative to body copy, with plenty of surrounding negative space. The module selection area is the densest part, but even there modules are arranged with consistent gaps and breathing room, avoiding a cramped, dashboard-like feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is a guided, top-to-bottom journey. The user first sees the global nav/brand bar (outside this spec), then the loader overlay (briefly), then progresses through the left-aligned steps: event basics → audience → modules → budget guidance → estimate summary (persisting) → save/request quote → confirmation → final contact CTA. Neon accents—primarily vertical bars to the left of section titles and subtle highlights on key controls—act as visual rails, guiding the eye down the column similar to the hero’s neon vertical line. A sticky estimate summary panel remains within sight as the user scrolls through mid-to-late steps, providing a stable anchor analogous to the hero’s bottom-center scroll indicator, gently pulling attention toward the evolving quote without overwhelming the main forms.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a dark, matte-black canvas, framed by wide left and right margins that make the central content feel like a focused editorial spread. At load, a minimal, full-viewport loader overlays everything; once dismissed, the user is left with a tall, airy configuration flow that reads like a sequence of immersive story beats rather than a utilitarian form. A single central container, centered horizontally, holds a left-aligned content column where each step appears as a spacious block: a bold, large-type section title marked by a thin neon vertical bar at its left edge, followed by clean, minimal inputs or options. Between each block is a substantial band of negative space so that only one major decision area dominates the screen at a time. In the middle of the flow, as modules and budgets come into play, a compact but prominent estimate summary docks to the right side of this central container and becomes sticky, subtly hovering as the user scrolls the left column of options beneath it. Near the end, the quote request form returns to a single-column focus, followed by a confirmation panel that takes over the same space when complete. Finally, at the lower-center of the page, slightly separated by a generous vertical gap, a minimalist contact CTA sits almost alone against the dark background, echoing the hero’s bottom-center scroll indicator and inviting deeper discussion. The overall effect is cinematic and deliberate: a calm, guided progression through configuration decisions in a high-end XR studio aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On initial page load, this component appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above the entire layout. It covers 100% of the width and height of the viewport, with a solid matte-black background that blends into the site’s dark theme but slightly dims any underlying content. At the very center of the viewport (both horizontally and vertically) is a compact cluster: a small neon-accented loader graphic (e.g., a thin ring or bar) and a single line of status text set in medium-sized, high-contrast type. There is generous negative space around this central cluster, with no additional UI near the edges, preserving the minimalist, premium feel. Once initialization is complete, this overlay fades out, revealing the main configurator flow positioned beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay: positioned fixed at top-left (0,0), covering the entire viewport; temporarily blocks interaction with underlying content and then disappears completely once loading is done.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This form is the first visible step in the configurator flow and sits within the main central container. The container itself is centered horizontally, spanning about 65% of the viewport width, leaving roughly 17.5% dark margin on both left and right sides. The event-basics-form aligns to the left edge of this container, occupying roughly 60% of the total page width. At its top is a bold, large-scale section heading, left-aligned, with a thin vertical neon bar running from just above the baseline of the heading down through the first line of descriptive text, positioned about 24px to the left of the heading—mirroring the mockup’s neon line + subheading treatment. Below the heading, with about 16–24px vertical gap, is a short, single-paragraph description that spans no more than two to three lines for a clean, editorial look. The actual form fields (event name, dates, location, etc.) stack vertically beneath this copy with consistent 16–24px vertical spacing between fields, all left-aligned with the heading, and with comfortable internal padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal) around the entire form block. The total vertical footprint of this component is roughly 50–60% of the viewport height, leaving visible negative space both above (under the nav) and below, so the form feels isolated and important.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow within the central column; scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the event-basics-form, after a generous vertical gap of about 64–80px, the audience-range-form continues the left-aligned column. It shares the same left edge as the event basics section, sitting inside the same central container width (~65% of viewport). The section begins with a slightly smaller but still bold heading, also marked by a thin vertical neon bar aligned with the heading’s left edge, reinforcing the visual rhythm. A compact explanatory line or two of body copy sits 16–24px beneath the heading, with the actual range controls (sliders, stepper inputs, or segmented controls) laid out beneath. The interactive controls are arranged in a vertical stack or simple two-column arrangement, but constrained so they never exceed roughly 60% of the page width, keeping them comfortably within the left part of the container. Generous vertical spacing (16–24px) between controls prevents visual clutter. Overall, this block occupies about 40–50% of the viewport height, allowing both this section and the tail of the previous one (or the beginning of the next one) to be visible at once, supporting a sense of continuous progression.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow; scrolls with the page and remains part of the main left-aligned content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component appears below the audience-range-form, separated by approximately 80–96px of vertical space to signify a major step change. The module-selection-list is the visually largest section of the configurator, designed to feel like a curated gallery rather than a dense catalog. Within the same central container, the section heading is left-aligned and prominent, with the signature vertical neon bar at its left, matching the alignment of all previous headings. Beneath a short, single-line description (spaced 16–24px below the heading), the modules are presented in a grid or stacked cards that occupy roughly the left 60% of the central container’s width—effectively around 40–45% of the entire viewport, maintaining large side margins. Each module card is given substantial padding (24–32px) and 24–32px gaps to its neighbors, with a maximum of two cards per row on desktop to avoid clutter. The right side of the central container, initially, remains mostly empty above the fold, which creates a feeling of wide negative space that will later be anchored by the estimate summary. Vertically, this section can extend beyond a single viewport height; as the user scrolls, they move down through multiple rows of modules, but at any given moment, only a few modules and the heading are visible, thanks to the generous spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow within the central container; its content scrolls vertically. On larger screens, it visually pairs with the sticky estimate-summary-panel that appears to its right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Following the last row of modules, there is a vertical gap of about 64px before the budget-guidance-panel appears, still within the same central container and aligned to the left. This panel is intentionally more compact, occupying roughly 45–50% of the viewport width (i.e., somewhat narrower than the main module list) so it feels like a focused advisory card rather than another large form. Its heading, again, uses the neon vertical bar motif and sits left-aligned at the same column start line as previous sections. Below the heading, a concise block of text—perhaps a few lines summarizing budget recommendations—is followed by one or two lightweight input or selector elements (e.g., a preferred budget range or flexibility toggle). The entire panel is boxed visually by ample internal padding (24–32px all around) and perhaps a subtle border or glow, but in terms of spatial layout it leaves a notable area of empty space to its right within the central container, where the estimate summary will visually align. The panel’s vertical dimension is moderate—around 35–45% of viewport height—so that it can appear entirely on screen while still showing the presence of the estimate summary panel beside it on larger screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow; it sits in sequence under the module-selection-list, but on larger screens it visually aligns horizontally with the sticky estimate-summary-panel to form a soft two-column relationship.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-summary-panel is positioned within the same central container as the forms but is anchored to the right side of that container to create a dual-column feeling for the mid-to-late steps. Horizontally, it occupies roughly 30–35% of the viewport width (about 40% of the central container), with its left edge aligned to the midline of the container and its right edge stopping well before the overall page edge, preserving the 15–20% outer margin. Vertically, the panel begins near the top of the module-selection-list section—its top edge roughly level with the module section heading—and extends downward just enough (about 40–50% of the viewport height) to show the key estimate details: title, subtotal, breakdown lines, and a dynamic total, all with generous internal padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal). The panel uses a clean, stacked layout with a clear hierarchy: a bold title at the top, then the estimate number in a large, high-contrast type, followed by a concise breakdown. As the user scrolls through the module-selection-list and budget-guidance-panel on the left, this summary panel remains in view, subtly hovering within the right side of the central frame. On smaller viewports (e.g., mobile), the summary panel collapses back into the main column and is placed directly below each relevant section, spanning the full width of the central container to maintain readability and avoid cramped side-by-side content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the central container on larger screens: it is positioned so that its top aligns near the top of the viewport once the module-selection-list scrolls into view and remains partially fixed as the left column scrolls beneath it, only scrolling out of view when reaching the end of the configurator. On small screens, it reverts to standard document flow and stacks beneath the module and budget sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After the budget-guidance-panel and the main interaction with the module list, the layout returns to a more singular focus. The save-request-quote-form appears below these sections with a clear vertical separation of around 80–96px, signaling the final action state of the configurator. It re-centers the user's attention on the left-aligned main column: the form’s heading (with the familiar vertical neon bar) and short explanatory text are positioned at the same left margin as all previous headings. The form itself spans most of the central container’s width—about 70–80% of it—to give the key actions room and visual weight, but still leaves a comfortable dark buffer to the sides. Fields for name, email, company, and any additional details stack vertically with 16–24px gaps, and the primary CTA button (e.g., 'Request Quote') is treated as a wide, pill-shaped element that aligns with the left edge of the fields but may extend slightly toward the center, giving it a strong but not overly intrusive presence. The estimate-summary-panel remains visible to the right as long as vertical space allows, visually tying the action of requesting a quote to the visible estimate numbers. The full block of this form takes up roughly 60–70% of the viewport height, ensuring it feels like the concluding focal scene of the configuration process.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow; it appears after the budget-guidance-panel. On larger screens, it shares the viewport with the sticky estimate-summary-panel to its right; on smaller screens, it simply follows beneath the summary as a stacked block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Once the save-request-quote-form is successfully submitted, the quote-confirmation-panel appears in the same spatial region, effectively replacing the form to maintain continuity. It occupies the identical left-aligned area within the central container, spanning about 70–80% of the container’s width and inheriting the same outer margins. At the top, a confident, large-format confirmation headline (e.g., 'Quote Requested') appears, with the vertical neon bar still marking the left edge and reinforcing the brand’s visual signature. Beneath this, a concise confirmation message and any essential next-step details are presented in a compact block of text, with 16–24px line spacing and ample breathing room above and below. Optional secondary actions (such as downloading a summary or sharing details) are arranged as discreet inline links or small pill buttons aligned below the main text but kept minimal to avoid clutter. Vertically, the panel is shorter than the preceding form—roughly 40–50% of the viewport height—leaving a noticeable band of empty space below. This open lower area mirrors the empty lower band of the hero layout and sets the stage for the final, bottom-centered contact CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow; it conditionally replaces the save-request-quote-form in the layout (e.g., via transition), occupying the same position and width. It scrolls normally but is typically near the bottom of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-discussion-cta is the final element in the configurator flow and is deliberately detached from the left-aligned column to echo the hero’s bottom-centered scroll indicator. It is positioned near the bottom of the page content, with a generous vertical gap of about 80–96px below the quote-confirmation-panel (or, if the form is still visible, below the save-request-quote-form). Horizontally, it is centered within the viewport rather than aligned to the left column. The CTA itself is a minimal, neon-accented text link or slim pill-shaped button—no wider than needed to fit the text—floating against the dark background at the visual center of the lower portion of the screen. On tall viewports, this CTA often sits within the bottom 15–20% of the viewport height, surrounded by ample negative space above and below, making it immediately noticeable yet calm and unobtrusive. This bottom-centered positioning creates a visual echo of the double-chevron scroll indicator in the hero, functioning here as a subtle invitation to go beyond the configurator and initiate a deeper conversation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow but visually centered: it is placed as the last content block on the page and uses auto margins or equivalent to center horizontally. On shorter viewports where content height exceeds screen height, it may require scrolling to reach, preserving the intentional empty band at the very bottom.

name: Case Studies Listing
title: Case Studies
description: Browse, filter, and shortlist XR case studies by technology and industry, with navigation to detailed case study pages and saved collections.

core_features: Load available technology and industry filters dynamically using getCaseStudyFilterOptions and render them as selectable controls.
Apply filters (technology and industry) to the case study listing using searchCaseStudies, with results displayed in a paginated grid of case study cards.
Respect incoming URL parameters for technology and industry to pre-select filters and immediately query matching case studies.
Provide pagination controls that update the page state and URL (e.g., page) and re-query searchCaseStudies with the correct page and pageSize.
Allow users to click a case study card to navigate to case_study_detail.html with the selected caseStudy.id passed as a URL parameter.
Enable users to quickly save a case study into a named shortlist collection via saveItemToNamedCollection, using itemType="case_study" and collectionType="case_study_shortlist".
Provide a lightweight UI for entering or confirming the collection name when saving a case study, and show feedback using the saveItemToNamedCollection response (success, message).
Offer a clear navigation action to saved_items.html so users can review all their saved case study collections.
Keep filter and pagination state in sync with URL parameters so that the listing is shareable and back/forward browser navigation restores the same view.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Browse pre-filtered case studies from another page
- Step 1: User clicks a link on index.html or resources.html that points to case_studies.html with technology and/or industry URL parameters.
- Step 2: On page load, the state manager reads the technology and industry parameters from the URL.
- Step 3: The page calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions to fetch the available filter values.
- Step 4: The filter toolbar initializes its controls, marking any options matching the URL parameters as selected (when present in the returned options).
- Step 5: The state manager calls searchCaseStudies with a filters object that includes the selected technology and industry values, along with the current page and pageSize.
- Step 6: The case study grid renders the returned CaseStudy results.
- Step 7: The user can refine filters; each change updates the URL parameters, resets the page to the first page, and triggers a new searchCaseStudies call.

Workflow 2: Build a case study shortlist for internal training ideas
- Step 1: User lands on case_studies.html (optionally pre-filtered) and uses the filter toolbar to select a relevant technology and industry.
- Step 2: The state manager calls searchCaseStudies with the current filters and pagination parameters; the grid displays matching case studies.
- Step 3: For a promising case study, the user clicks a 'Save to collection' action on the card.
- Step 4: A shortlist dialog opens, pre-filled with the last used collection name (if held in in-memory state) or a blank field; the user can type any collection name (for example, a name used by their team).
- Step 5: On submit, the dialog calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType set to "case_study", itemId set to the selected CaseStudy.id, collectionName from the user input, and collectionType set to "case_study_shortlist".
- Step 6: On success, the dialog closes, the feedback component shows a success message from the API, and the in-memory state remembers the last used collectionName for subsequent saves during this session.
- Step 7: The user repeats the save action on multiple case studies; when ready, they use a 'View saved collections' link (or button) to navigate to saved_items.html to review the shortlist.

Workflow 3: Navigate to case study detail and back to the same listing state
- Step 1: User applies filters and pagination until they see a relevant case study in the grid.
- Step 2: The user clicks on the case study card (or a 'View details' action) which navigates to case_study_detail.html with a URL parameter caseStudyId equal to the CaseStudy.id.
- Step 3: The user reviews the detailed information and then uses the browser back button to return to case_studies.html.
- Step 4: On return, the state manager re-reads the URL parameters (technology, industry, page).
- Step 5: The filter toolbar re-selects the appropriate filter values based on the URL.
- Step 6: The state manager calls searchCaseStudies with filters and page from the URL, so the same subset of results and the same page is re-rendered.
- Step 7: The user continues browsing or adjusting filters without losing their previous context.
interactions: Filter selection: When a user selects or deselects a technology or industry option in the filter toolbar, the filter toolbar emits a filter-change event. The state manager updates the internal filter state, synchronizes the technology and industry URL parameters, resets the page parameter to the first page, and calls searchCaseStudies with updated filters and pagination.

Clear filters: When a user clicks a 'Clear filters' action, the filter toolbar clears the selected technology and industry in its UI, the state manager removes those parameters from the URL (or sets them to empty values), resets pagination, and calls searchCaseStudies with an empty or reduced filters object.

Pagination click: When a user clicks next/previous or a specific page number, the pagination component updates the current page in state, updates the page parameter in the URL, and triggers searchCaseStudies using the existing filters and the updated page value.

Case study card click: When a user clicks the main interactive area of a case study card (thumbnail, title, or explicit 'Open' action), the page navigates to case_study_detail.html with a caseStudyId URL parameter equal to the clicked CaseStudy.id.

Save to collection (open dialog): When a user clicks 'Save to collection' on a case study card, the shortlist dialog component opens, receiving the target CaseStudy.id and (optionally) a default collectionName from the in-memory state.

Save to collection (submit): When the user submits the shortlist dialog, the dialog calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType="case_study", itemId set to the selected CaseStudy.id, collectionName as entered by the user, and collectionType="case_study_shortlist". On success, it emits a save-success event with the returned collection and collectionItem; on failure, it shows the message returned and emits a save-failed event.

Save feedback and navigation: The save feedback component listens for save-success events and shows a transient confirmation message, optionally including a 'View saved collections' action. If the user clicks this action, the page navigates to saved_items.html (no additional parameters required by this page).

URL-driven state changes: When the browser’s back/forward buttons are used, any changes in the technology, industry, or page URL parameters are detected by the state manager, which then updates the filter toolbar selection and triggers searchCaseStudies so that the UI reflects the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters and in-memory state (not localStorage) to manage filters and pagination.

On initial load:
- The state manager reads URL parameters technology and industry. If they exist, they are treated as the initial filter values; if they do not match any of the options returned by getCaseStudyFilterOptions, they are ignored or treated as unselected.
- The state manager also reads a page parameter (if present) to determine the current page; if absent or invalid, a default page index in state (such as the first page) is used but not hard-coded in the URL.
- The page first calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions to retrieve allowed technologyOptions and industryOptions. These results are stored in state and passed to the filter toolbar for rendering.
- Once filter options are loaded and initial selections are established, the state manager calls searchCaseStudies with an object passed into the filters parameter. This object includes keys corresponding to the selected technology and industry values (if any) and may omit them entirely when no filter is applied. The page parameter is passed directly from the current state; pageSize is taken from a configurable state value or application configuration and passed to the pageSize parameter.

On filter changes:
- When the filter toolbar reports a change (technology and/or industry), the state manager updates the internal filter state, rewrites the URL query string to include or remove technology and industry parameters accordingly, and resets the page state to the first page. It then calls searchCaseStudies with the updated filters and pagination.

On pagination changes:
- When the user changes the page via the pagination component, the state manager updates the page state and page URL parameter, keeping technology and industry parameters unchanged. It then calls searchCaseStudies with the current filters and the new page value.

On navigation via browser history:
- When the URL changes due to browser back/forward actions, the state manager re-parses the URL parameters for technology, industry, and page. It updates the filter toolbar selections and the pagination component accordingly, then calls searchCaseStudies with filters constructed from the updated parameters.

Saving to collections:
- Saving items does not change the URL. The saveItemToNamedCollection interface is called with values derived from the current UI interaction (CaseStudy.id and user-entered collectionName). The success or failure response is handled in-memory to show feedback. Optionally, the most recently used collectionName can be kept in in-memory state during the session to pre-fill the dialog but is not written to or read from URL parameters.

components.functionality: Central controller for the Case Studies page. Reads URL parameters (technology, industry, page), orchestrates calls to getCaseStudyFilterOptions and searchCaseStudies, stores results and pagination metadata in state, and passes derived state down to other components. Listens for filter and pagination events and updates URL and data accordingly.

Displays dynamic technology and industry filter controls using the options provided by getCaseStudyFilterOptions and the currently selected values from the state manager. Allows users to select/deselect a single technology and a single industry (or more, depending on requirements) and emits filter change events. Provides a 'Clear filters' action to reset to an unfiltered state.

Shows a compact summary of the currently applied filters as derived from state (selected technology and industry). Provides quick-remove actions for each active filter that clear that filter and trigger a search update via the state manager.

Renders the list of CaseStudy results returned from searchCaseStudies as a grid of interactive cards. Each card displays key fields (such as title, summary, technology, and industry from the CaseStudy entity) and exposes actions to open the detail page and to save the item to a collection.

Displays pagination controls based on totalCount and the current page and pageSize provided by the state manager. When the user changes the page, it emits page-change events which cause the state manager to update the page parameter in the URL and re-call searchCaseStudies.

A modal or inline dialog that opens when a user chooses to save a case study to a collection. Accepts the target CaseStudy.id, optionally pre-fills the collection name from in-memory state, and lets the user confirm or change the collectionName. On submit, calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType="case_study", itemId set to the CaseStudy.id, collectionName from the input, and collectionType="case_study_shortlist". Emits success or failure events based on the API response.

Listens for save-success and save-failure events from the shortlist dialog. On success, shows a confirmation message derived from the saveItemToNamedCollection response (e.g., response.message) and may store the most recently used collectionName in in-memory state for reuse. Provides a 'View saved collections' action that navigates to saved_items.html. On failure, shows the error message without navigation.

Displays loading indicators while getCaseStudyFilterOptions or searchCaseStudies requests are in progress, and shows a generic 'no results' state when searchCaseStudies returns a totalCount of zero for the current filters. Provides an optional action to clear filters in the empty state by triggering the filter toolbar’s clear function.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, tall hero-like section for the case study listing. At the top of the content column, a wide pill-shaped filter toolbar plays the role of the hero/nav pill. Directly beneath it, aligned to the same left column, sits the active filter summary with a neon vertical bar, echoing the hero subheading treatment.
