Below this, the case studies grid fills the middle of the viewport in a left-aligned content column, while the pagination control sits detached and centered near the bottom of the viewport, echoing the bottom scroll chevron. Non-primary components (notifications, dialogs, empty/loading) appear layered on top of this structure without breaking the vertical column flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, tall hero-like section for the case study listing. At the top of the content column, a wide pill-shaped filter toolbar plays the role of the hero/nav pill. Directly beneath it, aligned to the same left column, sits the active filter summary with a neon vertical bar, echoing the hero subheading treatment. Below this, the case studies grid fills the middle of the viewport in a left-aligned content column, while the pagination control sits detached and centered near the bottom of the viewport, echoing the bottom scroll chevron. Non-primary components (notifications, dialogs, empty/loading) appear layered on top of this structure without breaking the vertical column flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered content container (roughly 60–70% of the viewport width) is framed by wide left and right margins (about 15–20% of width each). The top 15–20% of the viewport is reserved mostly for the filter toolbar pill and generous top padding. The mid 50–60% is dedicated to the case studies grid. The bottom 15–20% is kept relatively light, with more negative space around the centered pagination component. Vertical spacing between primary stacked elements (toolbar → summary → grid → pagination) is generous, typically 32–64px, with at least 64–96px of breathing room at the top and bottom of the main listing section.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is intentionally low-density and cinematic: each component (filter toolbar, active filter summary, grid, pagination) has ample surrounding negative space. The filter toolbar has generous internal padding, like a navigation pill. The grid itself is relatively spacious, with larger-than-average gutters between cards and top/bottom padding, prioritizing visual impact over quantity per fold. No element hugs the edges; every block feels inset and carefully placed within the dark field.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is guided in a vertical sequence: first to the bold pill-shaped filter toolbar near the top center, then down to the active filter summary line on the left, then into the case studies grid as the main content area. After scanning the grid, attention drops to the bottom-centered pagination control, echoing a scroll cue. Transient UI (notification banner, dialogs) appears above this flow but uses alignment (centered or right-aligned within the content container) that respects the same grid and margins.

layout.overall_description: The case studies listing page feels like a dark, cinematic control surface for exploring XR work. All content sits within a centered, wide but contained column floating in a matte black background, with generous emptiness on both sides. At the very top, after a band of negative space, a large pill-shaped filter toolbar stretches across most of the content width and is horizontally centered, acting like a sleek control bar. Directly below, aligned to the left edge of the internal grid, a single-line active filter summary with a thin neon vertical rule quietly annotates the current selection, mirroring the hero’s subheading treatment. Beneath this, the case study cards form a grid that occupies the midsection of the viewport—wide but not full-bleed—leaving dark margins around it so the work feels curated rather than crowded. At the bottom of the screen, detached from the grid and centered horizontally, a minimal pagination control floats in open space, echoing the double-chevron scroll indicator from the mockup. Notifications slide in as sleek, narrow strips within the same central column, and the shortlist dialog appears as a centered overlay with generous padding and dimmed background, preserving the premium, futuristic aesthetic even in functional moments like saving or filtering. Empty and loading states subtly take over the grid area with a centered indicator while leaving the top filter controls in place, maintaining the page’s strong vertical hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, structural component that does not occupy explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it governs the state of filters, grid data, pagination, and shortlist interactions for all visible components within the centered content container. It should not render any visible UI or alter the vertical rhythm; instead, it coordinates component behavior behind the scenes while all visual components align to the same central column and margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic layer; no spatial footprint in the UI.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter toolbar is positioned near the top of the main content area and is the first prominent element users see after any global site navigation. It appears as a wide, pill-shaped bar horizontally centered within the viewport, sitting inside the central content container. The pill spans roughly 70–80% of the overall page width (or the full width of the max-width container) with 15–20% margins left and right, mirroring the nav pill from the mockup. There is substantial top padding—approximately 64–96px—from the top of the viewport (or from the global nav) to the top of this pill, creating a distinct atmospheric band of negative space. Inside the pill, controls (technology, industry filters, search, etc.) are arranged in a horizontal row with generous internal padding (around 16–24px vertically and 24–40px horizontally). The pill’s left edge aligns with the left edge of the underlying content column (used by the grid and filter summary), but its shape and subtle glow give it elevated prominence. There is a 32–48px gap below the pill before the next component (the active filter summary), preserving the clear vertical stepping of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Default is static within the flow, but it may optionally become sticky within the central column on scroll (sticking near the top with a small 16–24px offset) while keeping its horizontal centering and margins, so that filter controls remain accessible without feeling cramped.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filter summary sits directly below the filter toolbar, aligned to the left edge of the main content column, similar to the subheading in the hero mockup. It occupies approximately 50–60% of the content container’s width, leaving the right side mostly empty, which enhances the editorial feel. The block begins about 32px below the bottom of the pill-shaped toolbar. At the very left of this block, a thin, vertical neon line (about 2–3px wide, 24–40px tall) acts as a visual anchor. Immediately to the right of this line, with an 8–16px horizontal gap, a single or double line of concise text describes the active filters and result count. The text is set in a smaller, high-contrast type than the main headlines but still clear and refined. Vertical spacing above and below this summary is generous (24–32px above the grid), so it feels like a lightweight annotation rather than a congested control area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the vertical flow that expands or contracts in height depending on the number of active filters listed, but it always maintains its left alignment and relationship to the filter toolbar and grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case studies grid occupies the central band of the page, sitting directly beneath the active filter summary with roughly 32–48px of separation. It is left-aligned within the same central content column, using an implicit 12-column grid; cards typically span 4–6 columns each depending on breakpoint (two or three cards per row on desktop). The grid width is about 60–70% of the viewport, matching the content container, with wide gutters (24–32px) between cards horizontally and 32–48px vertically to preserve a premium, gallery-like feeling. The top of the grid should land somewhere around 30–40% of the viewport height from the very top, ensuring the toolbar and summary are clearly established before content begins. The grid extends downward as many rows as needed, but the first fold still leaves visible negative space beneath it to highlight the pagination control. Cards themselves should have generous internal padding and may feature subtle neon or soft glow accents on hover to maintain the futuristic XR aesthetic against the matte black background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive grid within the content column. On narrower viewports, the grid collapses to fewer columns but maintains left alignment and comfortable breathing room at the sides. When there are no results, this area is taken over by the empty/loading state component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned visually separate from the grid, echoing the scroll indicator from the mockup. They sit below the last row of cards with a substantial gap—approximately 48–64px of vertical space—creating a sense that they float in the lower part of the viewport. Horizontally, the pagination strip is centered within the page, not left-aligned with the grid. It occupies a compact width, roughly 20–30% of the viewport, with controls (page numbers, next/prev) arranged in a tight, minimal row or a small pill-shaped cluster. There is generous empty space above and below the pagination (at least 48px below before the end of the content section), and it ideally rests around the bottom 15–20% of the viewport on the initial load so it functions as a subtle cue to continue exploring more pages, visually analogous to the mockup’s neon double-chevron.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the document flow. As content grows vertically, it scrolls naturally with the page; it does not stick. On shorter result sets where the grid height is small, the pagination can be vertically centered between the bottom of the grid and the viewport bottom to maintain a visually balanced lower region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist dialog appears as a centered modal overlay when invoked, floating above the dark background and dimming the underlying page content with a translucent black scrim. It is placed at the visual center of the viewport, spanning roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (never exceeding the width of the main content container) and up to 50–60% of the viewport height, depending on content. The dialog is rectangular with subtly rounded corners and generous internal padding (32–40px on all sides), echoing the pill and card treatments. It is aligned to the same vertical axis as the main content column and pagination, reinforcing the central hierarchy. The area around the dialog remains empty except for the darkened underlying page, making the dialog feel like a focused, premium control surface for saving or organizing case studies.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal overlay centered in the viewport with a full-screen, non-scrolling scrim behind it. The page content behind can scroll, but interaction is locked to the dialog until dismissed. On smaller viewports, the dialog scales down to about 80–90% of viewport width and height while staying centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save feedback notification appears as a slim, sleek banner aligned within the main content column, near the top-right area of the viewport. It is anchored horizontally to the right edge of the content container (not the full viewport) and sits approximately 24–32px below the top of the viewport (or just below any global site header), visually distinct but not overpowering the filter toolbar. The banner spans about 30–40% of the container width and has a low-profile height with 12–16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, forming a subtle rounded rectangle or pill. A neon accent line or icon on the left edge can cue success while maintaining the futuristic aesthetic. The surrounding space remains open, so the banner feels like a light overlay hovering above the dark interface rather than a full-width alert bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed or sticky within the viewport, positioned relative to the content container’s right edge. It slides in (e.g., from slight opacity/translation) and auto-dismisses after a short interval. It does not push other content down; instead, it overlays the existing layout without disrupting the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty or loading state occupies the same spatial footprint as the case studies grid, preserving the overall layout structure. When active, it replaces the grid cards within the central column while leaving the filter toolbar and active filter summary visible above. The loading/empty indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within the grid area: its central point aligns with the grid’s horizontal center and sits roughly in the middle of the remaining viewport height below the filter summary. This might be expressed as a single, softly glowing geometric loader or a minimal, neon-accented icon plus a short line of explanatory text. Generous whitespace surrounds this indicator, with at least 48–64px clearance from the filter summary above and ample space to the container edges, making the state feel intentional and calm rather than error-like.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual overlay within the grid region: it replaces grid contents while active but does not cover the filter toolbar, filter summary, or pagination area. If the grid area is taller than the viewport, the indicator remains centered in the visible grid region and scrolls naturally with the page.

name: Case Study Detail
title: Case Study
description: Displays full details for a selected XR case study, allows users to save it into a named collection, and provides navigation to related case studies and service categories.

core_features: Load and display a specific case study based on the caseStudyId URL parameter using getCaseStudyDetail.
Show full case study content including title, summary, client, technology, industry, challenge, solution, results, metrics, and media gallery.
Provide a prominent control to save the current case study into a named collection via saveItemToNamedCollection.
Allow users to specify a collection name and collection type when saving; the backend will either create a new collection or append to an existing one.
Display feedback on whether the case study was added to a new collection or an existing collection based on the saveItemToNamedCollection response.
Offer breadcrumb navigation back to the Case Studies listing page.
Display related case studies returned by getCaseStudyDetail and allow navigation to their detail pages via URL parameters.
Highlight the related service category returned by getCaseStudyDetail and link to the corresponding service_category.html page using its slug.
user_workflows:
View a case study:
1. User arrives on case_study_detail.html with a caseStudyId URL parameter.
2. The page calls getCaseStudyDetail(caseStudyId).
3. While loading, the page shows a loading state instead of case study content.
4. On success, the page displays the case study’s title, metadata (technology, industry, client, published date), summary, media gallery, narrative sections (challenge, solution, results), and metrics.
5. User can scroll through the content and interact with media items (e.g., view images or play videos).

Save the case study into a named collection:
1. On the loaded case study, the user clicks the primary "Save" or "Add to collection" control.
2. A save modal or panel opens with a form containing:
   - A required text field for collectionName.
   - A required selection for collectionType (values constrained to the SavedCollection.collection_type enum).
   - The itemType is fixed to "case_study" and itemId is bound to the current caseStudy.id.
3. User enters a collection name (which may correspond to an existing collection or a new one) and confirms the collection type.
4. User submits the form.
5. The page calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType="case_study", itemId=current caseStudy.id, collectionName, and collectionType.
6. While the request is pending, the form shows a saving state and disables further submissions.
7. On success, the UI reads the response:
   - If success is true and createdNewCollection is true, the UI shows a message indicating a new collection was created and the item was added.
   - If success is true and createdNewCollection is false, the UI shows a message indicating the case study was added to an existing collection.
8. The modal may close automatically after success or remain open with a success message, depending on implementation.
9. On error (success is false), the UI shows the message from the response and allows the user to retry.

Navigate to related case studies:
1. After getCaseStudyDetail returns, the relatedCaseStudies array is displayed in a related items component.
2. User clicks a related case study entry.
3. The browser navigates to case_study_detail.html with caseStudyId set to the selected relatedCaseStudy.id.
4. The page re-runs the load workflow using the new caseStudyId, replacing the visible case study content and related items accordingly.

Navigate to related service category:
1. After getCaseStudyDetail returns, if relatedServiceCategory is present, the page shows a call-to-action panel referencing that service category.
2. User clicks the service category link or button.
3. The browser navigates to service_category.html with serviceId set to relatedServiceCategory.slug.
4. The service category page handles its own loading based on the serviceId URL parameter.

Return to the Case Studies listing:
1. The breadcrumb component renders a link back to the case_studies.html listing.
2. User clicks the breadcrumb link.
3. The browser navigates to case_studies.html (no additional parameters required for this action).
interactions: On page load or when caseStudyId in the URL changes, the page triggers getCaseStudyDetail with the current caseStudyId.
If caseStudyId is missing or invalid, the page shows an error state instead of attempting to render case study details.
Clicking the main Save/Add to collection button opens the save-to-collection modal or panel.
Typing into the collectionName field updates the pending values that will be sent to saveItemToNamedCollection.
Selecting a collectionType option updates the pending values that will be sent to saveItemToNamedCollection.
Submitting the save form triggers a call to saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType="case_study", itemId bound to the current caseStudy.id, and the user-entered collectionName and collectionType.
Receiving a successful saveItemToNamedCollection response updates local UI state to show success and optionally reflect that the item is saved.
Receiving an unsuccessful saveItemToNamedCollection response displays the returned message and keeps the form open for corrections.
Clicking a breadcrumb link navigates the browser to case_studies.html.
Clicking a related case study navigates to case_study_detail.html with caseStudyId set to that related case study’s id.
Clicking the related service category call-to-action navigates to service_category.html with serviceId set to relatedServiceCategory.slug.
Interacting with media items in the media gallery (e.g., selecting an item, starting or stopping playback) updates the displayed media state without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is determined by the caseStudyId URL parameter. On initial load, the page reads window.location (or equivalent) to extract caseStudyId. If caseStudyId exists, the page calls getCaseStudyDetail(caseStudyId) and enters a loading state. When the response resolves, the page stores the returned caseStudy object, relatedCaseStudies array, and relatedServiceCategory object in view state and transitions to a loaded state. If the request fails (for example, if caseStudyId does not correspond to an existing CaseStudy), the page enters an error state where no case study content is rendered and an error message is shown. When a user clicks a related case study, the browser navigates to the same page with a new caseStudyId value in the URL (e.g., case_study_detail.html?caseStudyId=...), which resets the component state: loading state is shown, getCaseStudyDetail is called again with the new id, and the caseStudy, relatedCaseStudies, and relatedServiceCategory bindings are updated when the response arrives. Navigations to other pages (case_studies.html or service_category.html) are handled purely by changing the URL; this page does not manage their internal state. Saving a case study to a collection does not change the URL; instead it updates local UI state based on the saveItemToNamedCollection response. The page may also optionally read additional non-required URL parameters (such as a preselected collectionType or collectionName) if defined by upstream navigation, but these do not affect data loading and are used only to pre-fill the save form fields. All persistent data related to collections is managed through the saveItemToNamedCollection interface; no localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: Reads the caseStudyId URL parameter, calls getCaseStudyDetail with that value, and exposes loading, error, and loaded data states to child components.
Displays a clickable breadcrumb link back to the case_studies.html listing and optionally the current case study title as a non-clickable label.
Shows the case study title, summary, client name, technology, industry, and published date, and includes the primary Save/Add to collection button that opens the save modal.
Renders the media_gallery array from the case study, allowing users to select or play individual media items such as images or videos.
Displays the narrative sections of the case study, including challenge, solution, and results fields, in structured sections.
Iterates over the caseStudy.metrics array and presents each metric item. If the array is empty or undefined, the component hides itself or shows an appropriate placeholder.

Opens when the user chooses to save the case study. Provides a form to capture collectionName and collectionType, then calls saveItemToNamedCollection to add the current case study to that collection and shows success or error feedback.

Shows inline feedback about the last save operation for this case study (for example, indicating that the case study has been added to a specific collection or that an error occurred). It reacts to the result of saveItemToNamedCollection.
Displays the relatedServiceCategory information if available and provides a link to service_category.html using serviceId=relatedServiceCategory.slug.
Lists relatedCaseStudies returned from getCaseStudyDetail. Each item links to case_study_detail.html with its own caseStudyId, enabling quick navigation between related case studies.

Manages synchronization between the URL caseStudyId parameter and the page’s internal state. Listens for navigation events, triggers data reloads when caseStudyId changes, and ensures that the correct case study is shown for the current URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, vertically stacked flow that mirrors the mockup’s hero structure, then extends it into a cinematic case study narrative. At the very top, a wide, pill-shaped bar hosts the breadcrumbs and lightweight case-level actions, centered horizontally. Below this, in the upper-middle of the first viewport, the case study header forms the hero: a bold, left-aligned title and short intro confined to a central content column. A subtle scroll cue sits near the bottom-center of the hero. Below the hero, content continues in a linear stack: media gallery, metrics band, rich body sections, then related service CTA and related case studies. Overlays (loader, save modal, status indicator) float above this stack without disturbing the underlying column structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, vertically stacked flow that mirrors the mockup’s hero structure, then extends it into a cinematic case study narrative. At the very top, a wide, pill-shaped bar hosts the breadcrumbs and lightweight case-level actions, centered horizontally. Below this, in the upper-middle of the first viewport, the case study header forms the hero: a bold, left-aligned title and short intro confined to a central content column. A subtle scroll cue sits near the bottom-center of the hero. Below the hero, content continues in a linear stack: media gallery, metrics band, rich body sections, then related service CTA and related case studies. Overlays (loader, save modal, status indicator) float above this stack without disturbing the underlying column structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the first full viewport height on desktop: the top 10–15% is the pill-shaped breadcrumb bar, the middle 40–50% is the hero header column, and the bottom 15–20% is mostly empty, emphasizing the scroll cue and hint of the next section. Horizontally, all main content lives in a centered container that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving 15–20% dark margin on each side for a framed, premium feel. Below the hero, sections maintain generous vertical separation (64–96px) with some sections like the media gallery and related case studies allowed to span slightly wider (up to ~80% width) to feel immersive while still visually anchored to the central grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a consistently low content density: each major component (breadcrumbs, header, gallery, metrics, body blocks, related content) is visually isolated with substantial margins and padding. The header text is large and bold with few lines. Metrics and related lists are compact but spaced, avoiding tight clustering. Padding for panels and cards follows the 16–24px vertical and 24–40px horizontal rhythm, ensuring every block feels deliberate and high-end rather than information-dense.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is guided in a strong top-to-bottom sequence: first the pill-shaped breadcrumb bar at the top center, then down to the bold case study title in the mid-upper viewport. From there, attention shifts to the short introductory copy aligned with a neon accent, then down toward the bottom-center scroll cue. Once the user scrolls, the flow continues into the media gallery as the first content reveal, then into a concise metrics band that summarizes impact, followed by deeper narrative body sections. Finally, the gaze lands on the related service category CTA and the related case studies list near the bottom. Overlays like the save modal and status indicator appear centrally or at the edges of this flow without stealing focus from the hero when not active.

layout.overall_description: The Case Study Detail page feels like a dark, cinematic canvas with content suspended in a central column, surrounded by generous matte-black margins. At the very top, a long, pill-shaped bar floats near the page edge, centered horizontally and containing the breadcrumbs—minimal, typographic, and sharply lit by a subtle neon accent. Scrolling your gaze downward, a bold, oversized case study title appears in the upper-middle of the viewport, aligned to the left of an invisible grid column that spans roughly two-thirds of the page width. Directly beneath it, a short line or two of introductory copy is anchored by a thin neon vertical rule at the far left, echoing the mockup’s hero subheading treatment. The lower portion of the hero is intentionally sparse, with a small neon double-chevron scroll cue hovering at the horizontal center near the bottom, inviting you to move down. As you scroll, the main canvas gently reveals a wide media gallery band and then a compact metrics strip, still tethered to the same left-aligned column, before unfolding into a sequence of generously spaced body sections. Each section breathes, with large dark gaps between them, so the content feels curated rather than crowded. Near the bottom, a sleek, understated panel points you toward the broader service category, followed by a minimalist, card-based list of related case studies. Throughout, any transient UI—like the save modal, status indicators, or loading state—appears as floating, neon-tinged layers on top of this central narrative, never overcrowding the primary reading path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the data loader occupies the full viewport as a dark, matte-black overlay that completely covers the underlying page. It is fixed to the screen, anchored to all four edges (top, bottom, left, right at 0), so it doesn’t scroll. At the exact center of the viewport, a minimal neon-accented loader (such as a ring or subtle XR-inspired glyph) sits within an invisible 200–300px square zone, with ample empty space around it, mirroring the low-density, premium feel. No other UI is visible or interactive until loading is complete, reinforcing focus on the central loader and maintaining the cinematic introduction to the case study.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-screen fixed overlay that sits above all other components while loading, blocking interaction and remaining centered regardless of scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs are placed inside a wide, pill-shaped container that floats at the very top of the case study hero. This pill is centered horizontally on the page and spans roughly 70% of the viewport width, leaving about 15% margin of dark space on each side. Vertically, it sits with a top offset of about 32–48px from the top viewport edge, occupying approximately 10–12% of the hero height. The pill has generous horizontal padding (24–40px) and moderate vertical padding (16–20px), giving the breadcrumb text room to breathe. Inside the pill, the breadcrumb text is left-aligned within the pill itself, following the overall left-aligned aesthetic, while any lightweight actions (like a subtle “Back to work” link or a compact save icon) can be right-aligned within the same pill, maintaining symmetry. There is a sizable vertical gap (64–96px) between the bottom of this pill and the start of the case-study-header content, emphasizing hierarchy and keeping the header visually isolated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the hero that scrolls away with the page; no sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case study header forms the core of the hero. It is positioned in the upper-middle of the initial viewport, starting roughly 25–30% down from the top edge. It sits within a centered content container that spans about 60–65% of the viewport width, leaving generous dark margins on the left and right. Within this container, all text is left-aligned to an invisible vertical grid line. The headline is large and bold, occupying up to 2–3 lines and visually taking up the central 40–50% of the viewport height. Directly below the headline, with a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px, is a short introductory statement (one to three lines). This intro is preceded by a thin, neon vertical bar aligned to the same left margin: the bar runs from slightly above the first baseline of the intro copy down to its last line, about 32–48px tall, visually echoing the mockup’s neon subheading marker. Below the intro, any secondary meta (client name, year, platform tags, etc.) is arranged in a low-density, small-type row or two, aligned to the same left edge and separated from the intro by about 16–24px. The total header block (headline, neon-marked intro, meta) is visually centered within the mid-section of the hero, with substantial empty space above and below to maintain the cinematic, premium feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level hero content that scrolls normally; locked into the central column with no fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The media gallery sits immediately below the hero header section, becoming the first substantial content reveal as the user scrolls past the scroll cue. On desktop, it begins approximately where the hero’s bottom 15–20% empty zone ends. The gallery spans a slightly wider width than the text-only header—about 75–80% of the viewport—while still being centered, so its left edge aligns roughly with the header’s text column and its right edge extends a bit further to feel more immersive. Vertically, the gallery occupies about 40–60% of the viewport height depending on aspect ratios, and it appears as a single, prominent band rather than a dense grid. Individual media items (hero image, video, or XR mockups) are arranged in a linear, horizontally scrollable strip or a small number of stacked frames, with generous 24–32px gaps between items. Above the gallery, a subtle section label (e.g., “Experience Preview”) may sit aligned to the same left grid line as the header, separated from the header content by 64–96px of vertical space. Below the gallery, another generous gap (64–80px) separates it from the metrics or body content, preserving the airy, cinematic rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section within the main scroll; media inside may enable horizontal scrolling or lightbox overlays but the gallery block itself is vertically stacked.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The body sections follow the gallery in a tall, vertically stacked column of narrative content. This column sits inside the same central container width as the header—roughly 60–65% of viewport width—centered with 15–20% margins on either side. Each body section (e.g., Challenge, Approach, Solution, Outcome) is treated like an editorial block: a left-aligned section heading followed by concise paragraphs, possibly with occasional inline images or pull quotes. Each section is separated from the next by 64–96px of vertical space, ensuring each feels distinct and easy to scan. The first body section starts about 64px below the bottom edge of the metrics panel (if metrics are placed above) or 80–96px below the media gallery if metrics are integrated inside the body. Within each section, paragraphs have comfortable spacing (16–24px between paragraphs), and any inline media is kept to a single column width to avoid clutter. The overall effect is a long, flowing column of narrative content that maintains the same left alignment as the hero text, with the surrounding dark space framing the story.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertical content that scrolls naturally, maintaining a single-column reading experience on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics panel is positioned as a concise summary band that appears after the media gallery but before the deepest narrative sections, acting as a bridge between visuals and detailed storytelling. It sits within the same central container as the body text, aligned to the familiar left grid line, and spans about 60–65% of the viewport width. Vertically, it is relatively shallow compared to other sections—roughly 20–30% of the viewport height—presenting 3–4 key metrics in a single row or in two staggered rows. Each metric (e.g., ‘+42% engagement’, ‘3M users’, ‘6 months to launch’) appears as a minimal card or typographic block with generous internal padding (16–24px vertical, 24–32px horizontal) and clear spacing between metrics (24–32px horizontally). A thin neon vertical rule on the far left of the panel, aligning with the header’s neon bar, visually anchors the panel to the hero style; the metrics themselves sit to the right of this rule. The panel is separated from the media gallery above by 64–80px and from the first body section below by another 64–80px, preserving the low-density, editorial pacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level band that scrolls with the page, integrated smoothly between gallery and narrative sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection save modal appears as a focused overlay centered within the viewport, above a dimmed version of the page. When triggered, a semi-transparent, matte-black scrim covers the entire viewport. The modal itself is a clean, rectangular card with very subtly rounded corners (to complement the pill nav), centered both horizontally and vertically. On desktop, it occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport width (max width about 520–640px) and about 30–40% of the viewport height depending on content. Inside, content aligns left within a comfortable padding zone (24–32px on all sides). The title (e.g., ‘Save to Collection’) sits at the top left, followed by form fields (existing collections list, new collection name input) arranged in a simple vertical stack with 16–24px separation. Primary and secondary actions (e.g., Save, Cancel) are aligned to the right at the bottom of the modal with 16–24px spacing. The surrounding scrim keeps ample negative space around the modal, so it appears as a floating, luminous object in the dark, consistent with the futuristic aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, above all other content, with a full-screen scrim that blocks interaction with the page while open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save status indicator appears as a small, pill-shaped toast that floats near the upper-right region of the viewport, clear of the central content column. On desktop, it is offset approximately 32px from the right edge and 96px from the top edge, ensuring it does not overlap the breadcrumb pill or hero headline. The pill spans about 240–320px in width with 12–16px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding, containing a short line of text such as ‘Saved to Collection’ and possibly a subtle check icon. It uses a neon accent for the border or background to stand out briefly against the dark backdrop. The indicator does not push any layout around it; instead, it overlays above the existing content and disappears automatically after a short duration. Its size and position keep it noticeable but peripheral to the main reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lightweight, fixed-position overlay that floats above the layout without affecting document flow; appears and auto-dismisses, anchored to the top-right viewport corner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related service category CTA forms a distinct section near the lower quarter of the page, following the main body sections and metrics. It sits within the central content container but may be slightly wider—about 70–75% of viewport width—to differentiate it as a panel. Vertically, it appears after a generous gap of 80–96px from the final body section, so it feels like a new chapter rather than an extension of the narrative. The CTA appears as a low, wide card with generous internal padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal). Inside, the copy is left-aligned: a short label (e.g., ‘Service Category’) above a bold line with the category name and a concise sentence describing what that category covers. A primary link or button (e.g., ‘Explore this Service’) is aligned to the right or at the lower right within the card, respecting the same horizontal padding. The card floats against the dark background with a subtle outline or neon accent, but still respects the spacious, editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level panel in the vertical stack that scrolls with the page, occupying its own clearly separated section near the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related case studies list appears as the final major content section on the page, directly beneath the related service category CTA. It is separated from the CTA by 48–64px of vertical space. This section is anchored to the same central column, spanning about 75–80% of the viewport width and centered. A simple section heading (e.g., ‘More Work Like This’) is left-aligned at the top, followed by a horizontally arranged row of case study cards or a clean vertical stack, depending on the number of items. On desktop, 3–4 cards may appear in a single row, each card occupying roughly 20–25% of the width with 24–32px gaps between them. Cards are tall, minimal tiles with a small image or gradient top, a concise title, and maybe a short tag line, all aligned left with generous internal padding (16–24px). The row of cards sits with ample breathing room above and below, and the entire section has a bottom margin of about 96–120px before the page footer, so the page ends with a sense of openness rather than visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in the main scroll, forming the last content block before the footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component operates behind the scenes with no visible footprint in the UI. It attaches to the case study detail page to synchronize the current case, selected media item, scroll position, or collection state with the URL and browser history. It does not occupy any visual space, does not add padding or margins, and does not alter the layout of any other component. Its presence is purely functional, ensuring deep-linking and shareable states while preserving the clean, minimalist presentation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-layout-affecting functionality that is effectively invisible in the spatial hierarchy of the page.

name: Saved Collections & Reading Lists
title: Saved Collections
description: Page for viewing and managing all saved collections of case studies and articles, including renaming or deleting collections, removing individual items, and navigating back to related case study and resource pages.

core_features: Load and display all saved collections and their items for the current user using getSavedCollectionsOverview.
Group collections logically (e.g., by collection_type) and allow selecting or expanding a collection to inspect its items.
Allow inline renaming of a collection via renameSavedCollection.
Allow deletion of an entire collection (and its items) via deleteSavedCollection with confirmation.
List all items within a collection and provide direct navigation to the corresponding case_study_detail.html or article_detail.html pages.
Allow removal of a single saved item from a collection via removeItemFromCollection.
Optionally handle an incoming item (via URL parameters) to be added to a named collection using saveItemToNamedCollection, creating the collection if needed.
Provide contextual navigation back to case_studies.html and resources.html to continue discovery from the collections hub.
user_workflows: View all saved collections: 1) User opens saved_items.html. 2) Page-state manager reads URL parameters (e.g., selected collection, filters). 3) collections-overview-loader calls getSavedCollectionsOverview. 4) collections-accordion-list displays each collection and its items (or item counts). 5) User can expand a collection to see items or click a collection header to make it the active collection (updating the URL state).

Rename a collection: 1) User clicks a rename action on a collection header. 2) collection-rename-inline-form switches that collection into edit mode and focuses the name input. 3) User submits the new name. 4) The form calls renameSavedCollection with collectionId and newName. 5) On success, the component updates the displayed name and the updated_at timestamp (if returned) without reloading the page; optional success message may be reflected in URL (e.g., ?message=collectionRenamed).

Delete a collection: 1) User clicks delete on a collection. 2) delete-collection-confirmation-dialog appears, referencing the selected collectionId from state. 3) User confirms. 4) The dialog calls deleteSavedCollection. 5) On success, the collection is removed from the in-memory list, the selected collectionId URL parameter is cleared if it referred to the deleted collection, and an optional feedback state is reflected via URL (e.g., ?message=collectionDeleted).

Open a saved case study or article: 1) User expands a collection and sees its items in collection-items-list. 2) User clicks on an item row. 3) collection-item-row checks item_type. 4) For item_type=case_study it navigates to case_study_detail.html?caseStudyId=<item_id>. 5) For item_type=article it navigates to article_detail.html?articleId=<item_id>.

Remove an item from a collection: 1) User expands a collection and hovers or taps on an item. 2) User clicks the remove action on that item. 3) collection-item-row calls removeItemFromCollection with collectionId, itemType, and itemId. 4) On success, the item is removed from the collection’s item list in memory; no full page reload is required, and URL parameters remain unchanged.

Add a newly saved item to a named collection (optional, via URL): 1) User saves an item from case_study_detail.html or article_detail.html and is sent to saved_items.html with URL parameters such as ?addItemType=<case_study|article>&addItemId=<id>. 2) page-state-manager detects addItemType and addItemId and activates add-item-to-collection-dialog. 3) On load, collections-overview-loader has already called getSavedCollectionsOverview to provide the list of existing collections. 4) User selects an existing collection from a dropdown or types a new collection name. 5) User chooses or confirms a collectionType (e.g., inferred from itemType or chosen from the allowed enum values). 6) add-item-to-collection-dialog calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType, itemId, collectionName, and collectionType. 7) On success, if createdNewCollection is true, the new collection is appended to the list; if false, the existing collection is updated to include the new item. 8) The dialog then updates the URL to remove the addItemType/addItemId parameters and optionally set selected collectionId to the affected collection so the user can review it.

Continue discovery from saved items: 1) User reaches the bottom or an empty state of the collections overview. 2) discovery-links-footer displays contextual links. 3) Clicking the 'Find more VR case studies' link navigates to case_studies.html?technology=vr. 4) Clicking 'Browse more resources' navigates to resources.html (no additional parameters).
interactions: On page load, the page-state-manager component parses URL parameters (e.g., collectionId, view, addItemType, addItemId, message) and stores them in in-memory state to drive which collection is selected, which filters are applied, and whether the add-item dialog is displayed.
On page load or refresh, collections-overview-loader triggers getSavedCollectionsOverview and binds the returned collections and items to collections-accordion-list and add-item-to-collection-dialog.

When a user changes a filter (e.g., collection_type or sort order) in collections-filter-toolbar, the component updates the URL (e.g., ?view=reading_list) and updates the in-memory filtered view without calling additional APIs.

When a user clicks on a collection header in collections-accordion-list, the component toggles expansion, marks it as the active collection, and updates the URL parameter collectionId to the corresponding SavedCollection.id.
When a user clicks the rename control for a collection, collection-rename-inline-form activates edit mode for that collection row (no API call yet).

When a user submits the rename form, collection-rename-inline-form calls renameSavedCollection with the provided newName and the collection’s id; on success it updates the collection name in the bound data and exits edit mode; on error it shows an inline error and does not change the name.

When a user clicks delete on a collection, delete-collection-confirmation-dialog is displayed; if the user confirms, it calls deleteSavedCollection; on success it removes the collection from the bound list and updates the URL to remove the deleted collectionId if present.
When a user clicks a saved case study item row, collection-item-row reads item_id and navigates to case_study_detail.html?caseStudyId=<item_id>.
When a user clicks a saved article item row, collection-item-row reads item_id and navigates to article_detail.html?articleId=<item_id>.

When a user clicks remove on an item, collection-item-row calls removeItemFromCollection with collectionId, itemType, and itemId; on success the item is removed from the collection’s item list in memory.

When the page is opened with addItemType and addItemId in the URL, add-item-to-collection-dialog is shown automatically; when the user submits the dialog, it calls saveItemToNamedCollection, and on success it updates the list of collections and cleans up the URL by removing addItemType/addItemId while optionally setting collectionId to the affected collection.
When a user clicks the 'Find more VR case studies' contextual link, the browser navigates to case_studies.html?technology=vr.
When a user clicks the 'Browse more resources' contextual link, the browser navigates to resources.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The saved_items.html page uses URL parameters to manage state instead of localStorage. Example parameters: 1) collectionId: The id of the currently selected or expanded SavedCollection. When present, collections-accordion-list ensures that collection is expanded and marked as active; when it is removed from the URL (e.g., after deletion), the UI falls back to a default state (such as no active collection or the first available collection). 2) view: A logical filter indicating which collections to show (for example, values mapped to SavedCollection.collection_type). collections-filter-toolbar reads this parameter on load to set its initial filter, and when the user changes the filter it updates this URL parameter; collections-accordion-list uses it to filter collections client-side using the already loaded data from getSavedCollectionsOverview. 3) addItemType and addItemId: When present, they indicate that the page should show add-item-to-collection-dialog preconfigured for adding a specific item to a collection. page-state-manager reads these values on load; add-item-to-collection-dialog uses them as the itemType and itemId for saveItemToNamedCollection. After a successful save or when the user cancels, the dialog triggers a URL update to remove these parameters so the dialog does not reappear on refresh. 4) message (optional): Used to reflect high-level outcomes such as collection renamed or deleted. On load, page-state-manager can use this to display a one-time status message; when dismissed, it updates the URL to remove message. All state transitions that should persist across refreshes (selected collection, active filters, pending add-item flow) are reflected via URL parameter updates; transient UI-only states (inline edit mode, open confirmation dialogs) are held only in memory and do not alter the URL.

components.functionality: Initializes page-level state based on URL parameters and orchestrates when other components should load data or display dialogs. It parses parameters such as collectionId, view, addItemType, addItemId, and message on load and exposes them to other components via in-memory state. It is also responsible for updating URL parameters when state changes (e.g., when a user selects a different collection, changes filters, or completes an add-item flow).

Calls getSavedCollectionsOverview once when the page is initialized, then stores and exposes the resulting list of SavedCollection objects and their SavedCollectionItem children to presentation components. It handles loading and error states and triggers re-rendering of dependent components when the data is received.

Provides controls to filter and optionally sort how collections are displayed (for example, by collection_type or by updated_at). It reads the initial filter state from the view URL parameter via page-state-manager and updates that parameter when the user changes filters. It does not call any interfaces; instead, it updates in-memory filter state that collections-accordion-list uses to determine which collections to show.

Displays all SavedCollection entries (filtered according to collections-filter-toolbar) in an expandable list or grouped by collection_type. Each collection row shows its name, optional description and metadata (e.g., item count, timestamps from the data), and exposes actions to select, expand, rename, or delete the collection. It uses the collectionId state from page-state-manager to decide which collection is currently active/expanded.

Handles the inline renaming of a single SavedCollection. When activated for a specific collection row by collections-accordion-list, it replaces the read-only name with an input field. On submission, it calls renameSavedCollection with the collectionId and the newName, and on success updates the bound SavedCollection.name and exits edit mode.

Displays a confirmation prompt when the user attempts to delete a collection. It receives the target collectionId from collections-accordion-list and, upon user confirmation, calls deleteSavedCollection. If the deletion is successful, it removes that collection from the in-memory list in collections-overview-loader and, if necessary, clears or updates the collectionId URL parameter via page-state-manager.

Shows the SavedCollectionItem entries belonging to the currently active or expanded SavedCollection. For each item, it displays available metadata from the SavedCollectionItem (and any embedded case study or article metadata returned by getSavedCollectionsOverview) and allows navigation to the detail page or removal from the collection. The component uses the currently active collectionId from page-state-manager to decide which collection’s items to render.

Represents a single SavedCollectionItem within collection-items-list. It determines its navigation behavior based on item_type: for case_study items it links to case_study_detail.html with the caseStudyId URL parameter set to item_id; for article items it links to article_detail.html with the articleId parameter set to item_id. It also exposes a remove action that calls removeItemFromCollection with the collectionId, itemType, and itemId.

Activated when the URL contains addItemType and addItemId. It allows the user to add the specified item into an existing or new named collection. The dialog uses the list of existing SavedCollection objects from collections-overview-loader to populate a selectable list and also accepts a free-text collectionName field. It determines or allows the user to select a collectionType compatible with SavedCollection.collection_type. On submit, it calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType (from URL or state), itemId (from URL or state), collectionName, and collectionType. On success, it updates the collections data (adding the new item and/or collection as indicated by the response) and updates URL parameters to remove addItemType/addItemId and optionally set collectionId to the affected collection.

Displays transient success or error messages to the user, such as confirmation that a collection was renamed, deleted, or that an item was added or removed. It can read an initial message code from the message URL parameter and also receive messages from API responses. When dismissed, it clears any message URL parameter through page-state-manager so the banner does not reappear on refresh.

Provides contextual links to continue exploring case studies and resources when the user wants to add more items to collections. It renders navigation actions that send the user to case_studies.html with the technology URL parameter set to vr and to resources.html with no additional parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: All interactive content lives inside a single, full-height hero-style section on a matte black background. A centered content column (about 60–70% of the viewport width) anchors everything, with generous left and right margins (~15–20% each). Within this column, elements flow in a vertical stack from top to bottom: status banner (when present), then the saved collections toolbar (acting as the hero headline area), followed by the accordion list of collections. Dialogs appear above this main stack as centered overlays.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All interactive content lives inside a single, full-height hero-style section on a matte black background. A centered content column (about 60–70% of the viewport width) anchors everything, with generous left and right margins (~15–20% each). Within this column, elements flow in a vertical stack from top to bottom: status banner (when present), then the saved collections toolbar (acting as the hero headline area), followed by the accordion list of collections. Dialogs appear above this main stack as centered overlays.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper 15–20% of the viewport is reserved for the status banner and the collections toolbar, mirroring the nav pill band from the mockup. The mid 55–65% of the viewport is dominated by the collections accordion list and its item rows, but these still only occupy the central column, leaving dark negative space on both sides. The lower 15–20% remains visually lighter, providing breathing room before the discovery links footer and maintaining the cinematic feel. When loaders or dialogs appear, they occupy the central portion of the viewport and visually override the underlying layout while preserving the dark, minimal background.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is deliberately low-density: each major block (banner, toolbar, accordion sections, dialogs) has generous vertical margins (32–64px) and internal padding (16–24px). Collection headers and item rows use ample line height and horizontal spacing, with neon accents and typography doing the visual work rather than crowded content. The result feels editorial and premium, with plenty of negative space around each component so no area feels visually noisy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first encounters any status message banner at the top of the content column, then naturally drops to the collections toolbar which acts as the primary focal area (page title and high-level controls). From there, the gaze moves down into the accordion list of collections, scanning left-aligned collection titles and expansion chevrons. Within an expanded collection, the eye is guided by a subtle vertical neon accent at the far left and follows the rows of saved items to the right. The page concludes with the discovery-links footer at the bottom of the column, subtly inviting navigation elsewhere. When dialogs are open, they become the immediate focal point, centered in the viewport with the rest of the page dimmed behind them.

layout.overall_description: The page reads as a dark, futuristic, full-height canvas with a single, disciplined content column floating in the center of the screen. At the very top of this column, a slim, full-width status banner appears only when needed, its left edge marked by a neon accent that echoes the studio’s XR aesthetic. Below, separated by a generous band of negative space, a pill-like collections toolbar sits in the upper-middle of the viewport, left-aligned within the column and housing the page title and filters—this area visually replaces the hero headline zone from the mockup. Beneath it, occupying the central vertical band, a series of wide accordion panels list each saved collection, each header stretching the width of the column and expanding downward to reveal their items. Inside expanded panels, item rows stack vertically with ample breathing room, forming a clean, readable list that still feels cinematic due to the surrounding darkness and bold typography. The lower part of the viewport remains intentionally sparse, with only a refined discovery-links footer resting at the bottom of the content column, leaving wide bands of empty black above and below to preserve the sense of calm and focus. Any loader or dialog momentarily takes over the center of this stage as a floating, high-contrast panel, while the rest of the layout recedes into a dim background, maintaining the premium, immersive studio feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. It sits conceptually behind the entire page, governing which visual states are shown (loading, loaded, error) and orchestrating whether loaders, banners, or dialogs are visible. From a layout perspective, it influences which components render within the central content column and when full-screen overlays appear, but it has no direct position, size, or visual footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, purely logical; it affects the visibility and states of other components but is not rendered in the visual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay centered on the viewport, spanning the full width and height of the screen. The background remains the matte black of the page but dimmed slightly to about 70–80% opacity, with the central content column still faintly visible beneath. In the exact vertical and horizontal center, a compact loading construct (skeleton blocks or a futuristic spinner with subtle neon detailing) occupies roughly 20–25% of the viewport width and auto height. The overlay respects the same central column width for its skeleton lines, so any placeholder content aligns with the left edge and width of the underlying collections list, reinforcing the grid even in the loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed full-screen overlay that sits above all other components; scroll on the underlying page is disabled while it is visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collections filter toolbar occupies the upper-middle portion of the central content column, visually playing the role of the hero headline block. It is horizontally centered with the page but left-aligned within its own width, spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width with 15–20% dark margins on each side. The toolbar itself appears as a wide, pill-shaped or softly rounded rectangle with a subtly elevated, dark charcoal background. It sits approximately 64px below any status banner (or the top of the column if no banner is present), creating a calm band of negative space above. Inside, the left side is anchored by the page title and/or primary label for the saved collections, using large, bold typography. To the right, on the same baseline but with ample horizontal separation, sit filter chips, a sort control, or view toggles. Vertical padding inside the pill uses 16–24px, and horizontal padding is 24–40px, matching the premium spacing system. The entire toolbar feels like a structured, editorial hero header rather than a dense control bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow of the content column; it scrolls away with the page rather than sticking, maintaining the cinematic, hero-like entrance experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collections accordion list sits directly below the filter toolbar, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 32–48px. It spans the full width of the central content column, aligning its left edge perfectly with the toolbar’s left alignment. Each accordion section (each saved collection) is a full-width block with a low-profile card treatment: a near-black background slightly lighter than the page, with subtle dividers or shadows. The headers use a tall row height (56–72px) with generous horizontal padding (24–32px), and the collection title is left-aligned, while a minimal icon or chevron sits at the far right edge, indicating expand/collapse. Accordion sections are stacked vertically with 16–24px of space between them, allowing each collection to feel isolated and important. When a section is expanded, its content slides open underneath the header, occupying the same column width and pushing subsequent sections downward, maintaining the linear, top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static stack within the central scroll area; sections expand and collapse in place, increasing or decreasing the total page height without affecting other components’ positions above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rename inline form appears directly within a collection header row, replacing the static collection title text but preserving the overall layout of that row. It is anchored to the same left alignment as the original title inside the accordion header, beginning approximately 24–32px from the left edge of the section. The input field spans roughly 40–60% of the header width, leaving space on the right for confirmation and cancel icons or buttons, which align vertically to the center of the header. Vertical height of the header does not dramatically increase; instead, the form fits within the existing 56–72px row, maintaining the clean, minimal structure. The surrounding collections above and below remain in place, emphasizing that renaming is a focused, inline edit rather than a separate screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement within a specific accordion header; it does not overlay other content and scrolls with the list as part of the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This dialog appears centered both vertically and horizontally in the viewport, floating above the dark page as a compact, high-contrast panel. Its width is about 420–480px on desktop, with a slightly rounded rectangle shape and a subtly lighter, matte charcoal background. The dialog title and explanatory text are left-aligned inside, with 24px top padding and 24–32px side padding, creating an airy, considered composition. Action buttons (e.g., Confirm/Delete and Cancel) are right-aligned along the bottom edge, separated from the content by ~24px of vertical space and framed by a neon-accented primary button. The rest of the page, including the central column and accordion list, is dimmed with a semi-transparent black overlay, ensuring the dialog becomes the clear focus in the visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal overlay that blocks interaction with underlying content; it remains anchored to the center of the viewport even if the page beneath is scrollable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within an expanded accordion section, the collection items list appears directly beneath the header, occupying the full internal width of that section. It starts with a small vertical offset (about 8–16px) below the header to visually separate the two. The list arranges individual saved items in a single column, left-aligned with the header text, and extends downward as needed. A thin vertical neon bar can run along the far left edge of the expanded content area, extending the full height of the visible items, subtly echoing the vertical neon line from the mockup’s body copy and guiding the eye downward. Vertical spacing between rows (collection-item-row components) is generous—8–16px between each row—to keep the content airy and legible. The list never expands beyond the central content column, preserving the wide dark margins on either side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content within an expanded accordion; it grows vertically inside its parent section and scrolls as part of the main page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each collection item row is a horizontally oriented strip inside the collection-items-list, aligned to the central content column. The row height is comfortable and generous (56–72px), with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. The left side of the row begins slightly to the right of any vertical neon accent, with the item title and meta information stacked or aligned horizontally depending on available space, all left-aligned for quick scanning. On the far right of the row, aligned to the same baseline, are subtle interactive controls such as a remove icon and a link or button to ‘View case study’/‘Open article’. Each row has a subtle hover state—slight lift or brightness increase—while maintaining a low-key, premium look (no heavy outlines). Rows are separated by 8–12px vertical gaps or fine dividers, continuing the sparse, editorial density rather than a compressed table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list items that flow vertically within the collection-items-list; they scroll naturally with the page and do not overlap or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This dialog appears as a larger, centered overlay similar in positioning to the delete confirmation dialog but with a slightly wider footprint (approximately 520–640px wide) and greater height to accommodate a list or form. It is horizontally centered in the viewport and vertically placed near the center, with enough surrounding breathing room to preserve the sense of a floating panel in a dark void. The top portion houses a clear title and short description, left-aligned with 24–32px padding, while the middle portion contains either a list of collections or selection controls, arranged in a single column that aligns with the broader page’s central column grid. At the bottom, actions (Confirm/Add and Cancel) are right-aligned with clear neon-accented primary action. The background page is dimmed and blurred very slightly, reinforcing focus on the dialog without losing the XR, futuristic feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal overlay, centered on screen, blocking interaction with underlying content; it remains stable relative to the viewport during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status message banner sits at the very top of the central content column, just below any global navigation and above the collections filter toolbar. It spans the full width of the content column (matching the toolbar’s width), with 16–20px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. The banner’s left edge aligns precisely with the toolbar’s left edge, and a thin vertical neon strip or glowing accent mark runs along the far left inside edge, visually tying it to the mockup’s neon vertical bar motif. Text is left-aligned and concise, with a subtle icon at the far left to indicate success, warning, or error. A close/dismiss icon is right-aligned and vertically centered. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the banner and the top of the toolbar below, ensuring the banner feels separate and ephemeral rather than fused to the main layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal flow at the top of the content column; when present, it pushes content below downward. It may appear and disappear dynamically but does not overlay other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discovery-links footer anchors the bottom of the central content column, appearing after the final accordion section. It is separated from the last piece of interactive content by a generous vertical gap of 64–96px, preserving a sense of completion and open space. The footer spans the same width as the content column, centered on the page with wide dark margins on either side. Within the footer, links to related case studies, resources, or navigation destinations are arranged either in a single row (if few) or in a compact grid, but always left-aligned to the column’s grid. Typography is slightly smaller than the main content but maintains strong contrast. The vertical padding inside the footer block is 32–48px, giving it a grounded, stable presence that gently closes the page while still feeling light and non-marketing-heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main content flow; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the collections list and does not stick or float.

name: Resources & Blog Listing
title: Resources & Blog
description: Searchable and filterable listing of XR-related articles and resources, with topic/tag filters, pagination, and quick saving of articles into named reading lists.

core_features: Load available topic and tag filter options via getArticleFilterOptions and render them as selectable filters.
Initialize search state from URL parameters (topic, search) and execute an initial article search via searchArticles.
Provide a search form that updates the URL and re-queries searchArticles when submitted.
Provide topic and tag filters that update the URL query parameters and re-queries searchArticles when changed.
Display article results as cards (using data from searchArticles) with title, summary, tags, topic, publish date, and reading time if available.
Allow users to click an article card to navigate to article_detail.html with the articleId in the URL.
Provide pagination that calls searchArticles with the correct page and pageSize and keeps page state in the URL.
Allow users to save an article into a named reading list using saveItemToNamedCollection, creating the collection if needed.
Provide navigation to saved_items.html so users can review and manage their saved reading lists and collections.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Land on Resources & Blog from another page with a pre-selected topic
1. User arrives at resources.html with a topic URL parameter set by index.html or case_study_detail.html.
2. On page load, the page reads the topic parameter from the URL and sets the initial topic filter state.
3. The page calls getArticleFilterOptions to populate topic and tag filters.
4. The page calls searchArticles with filters including the topic from the URL and default values for other filters (e.g., no tags, first page).
5. The article results list displays only articles matching the topic.
6. User can refine further by adding tags or typing into the search field, which updates the URL and triggers new searchArticles calls.

Workflow: Use the search bar to find specific XR content
1. User lands on resources.html, optionally with a search parameter in the URL.
2. On load, the search parameter (if present) populates the search input field.
3. The page calls getArticleFilterOptions and then searchArticles with filters based on the URL (including search text if present).
4. User edits the search text and submits the search form.
5. The submit handler updates the search parameter in the URL, resets the page parameter to the first page, and calls searchArticles with the updated filters.
6. The results list is refreshed to show articles that match the keyword search.

Workflow: Filter articles by topic and tags
1. User arrives at resources.html (with or without existing URL parameters).
2. The page calls getArticleFilterOptions to retrieve topicOptions and tagOptions.
3. The topic filter component renders available topics; the tag filter component renders available tags.
4. User clicks a topic option; the topic change handler updates the topic parameter in the URL and clears the page parameter.
5. The handler calls searchArticles with updated filters including the selected topic and any currently selected tags or search text.
6. User toggles one or more tags; each change updates a tags parameter (e.g., comma-separated list) in the URL, resets the page parameter, and triggers another searchArticles call.
7. The results list updates to reflect the chosen topic and tag combination.

Workflow: Paginate through article results
1. After a search or filter change, the page shows article results along with pagination controls.
2. The current page number is stored in a page URL parameter.
3. User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or specific page number).
4. The pagination handler updates the page parameter in the URL without changing other parameters (topic, search, tags).
5. The handler calls searchArticles with the existing filters and the new page value.
6. The results list updates to show articles for the selected page.

Workflow: Open an article from the listing
1. User browses the article cards shown in the results list.
2. User clicks on an article card (or its title/read-more action).
3. The click handler reads the article.id from the card data and constructs a navigation URL to article_detail.html with articleId as a query parameter.
4. Browser navigates to article_detail.html?articleId={id}, where article_detail.html can load and display the selected article.

Workflow: Save an article to a named reading list
1. User finds an article in the results list that they want to save.
2. User clicks the save/add-to-reading-list action on that article card.
3. The save handler opens a collection selection/creation UI (e.g., inline form or modal) that lets the user enter or confirm a collection name and collection type (e.g., reading_list).
4. User confirms the save action, which calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType set to article, itemId set to the selected article's id, and the chosen collectionName and collectionType values.
5. On success, the component may show a non-static confirmation message and update any in-memory state (e.g., marking the article as saved) without hardcoded labels for specific collections.
6. User can optionally click a link to navigate to saved_items.html to review their saved lists.

Workflow: Review saved collections from the Resources page
1. User is on resources.html and wants to see previously saved reading lists.
2. User clicks a navigation element labeled generically (e.g., a link or button) that leads to the saved collections area.
3. The click handler navigates the browser to saved_items.html (without relying on local storage), where saved_items.html handles loading and displaying SavedCollection and SavedCollectionItem data.
4. User can then return to resources.html using browser navigation or site navigation to continue browsing articles.
interactions: Submitting the search form updates the search URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and calls searchArticles with updated filters.
Changing the topic filter updates the topic URL parameter, clears the page parameter, and calls searchArticles with the new topic and existing search/tags filters.
Toggling tag filters updates a tags URL parameter representation, clears the page parameter, and calls searchArticles.
Clicking pagination controls updates the page URL parameter and calls searchArticles while preserving other filter parameters.
Clicking an article card builds a URL to article_detail.html with articleId set to the selected article's id and navigates to it.
Clicking the save/add-to-reading-list control on an article opens a collection configuration interaction and, on confirmation, calls saveItemToNamedCollection.
Clicking the link to view saved collections navigates to saved_items.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter and pagination state.
