On initial load and on every URL change, the page reads parameters such as topic, search, tags, and page. The topic parameter (string) is used to pre-select a topic filter and is passed into searchArticles as part of the filters object if present. The search parameter (string) populates the search input and is also passed into searchArticles filters. A tags parameter (string) may store a delimited list of tags; this is parsed into an array for the filters.tags field passed to searchArticles. A page parameter (number) determines the current page sent to searchArticles; if absent or invalid, the page defaults to the first page. The pageSize value is managed by the page logic (e.g., a configurable constant or control) and is always passed explicitly into searchArticles along with page. Whenever the user changes search text, topic, tags, or pagination, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (using navigation or history APIs) and then invokes searchArticles with filters derived from the current URL state. The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; all shareable state is encoded in the URL so that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same filters and results.

components.functionality: On page load, this component reads topic, search, tags, and page from the URL, calls getArticleFilterOptions to fetch available topicOptions and tagOptions, initializes the filter UI with these options and any pre-selected values from the URL, and then triggers the initial searchArticles call with filters derived from the URL state.

Provides a text input for article search and a submit action. The input is initialized from the search URL parameter if present. On submit, it updates the search parameter in the URL, resets the page parameter to represent the first page, and calls searchArticles with filters composed from the updated search text plus any current topic and tag selections.

Displays selectable topic filters using the topicOptions returned from getArticleFilterOptions. The initially selected topic is based on the topic URL parameter. When the user selects or changes a topic, it updates the topic parameter in the URL (or removes it if cleared), resets the page parameter, and triggers a searchArticles call with the new topic value and other active filters.

Displays tag filters using the tagOptions returned from getArticleFilterOptions. Selected tags are derived from the tags URL parameter. When a tag is toggled on or off, the component computes the new tag selection list, encodes it into the tags URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and calls searchArticles with updated tag filters while preserving the current topic and search text filters.

Renders the list of articles returned from searchArticles as interactive cards. Each card displays fields from the Article entity (such as title, summary, topic, tags, published_at, reading_time_minutes, and hero_image if available). It also includes clickable regions that navigate to article_detail.html with the articleId in the URL and a save/add-to-reading-list action that delegates to the save-to-collection components.

Displays pagination controls based on the totalCount and the current page and pageSize used in the most recent searchArticles call. When the user navigates to a different page, the component updates the page URL parameter and triggers a new searchArticles call with the same filters (topic, search, tags) but the new page value.

Handles the save/add-to-reading-list workflow for article cards. When an article's save control is activated, this component captures the article.id and presents a minimal UI state for choosing or entering a collectionName and collectionType (with collectionType suitable for reading lists). On confirmation, it calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType set to article, itemId set to the selected article id, and the provided collectionName and collectionType. It then processes the response, updating internal state about whether the article is considered saved and optionally showing a confirmation, without relying on any hardcoded collection names or static data.

Provides a navigation element that takes the user from resources.html to saved_items.html to review their saved collections and reading lists. It does not load or manage SavedCollection data directly on this page; instead, it relies on navigation so that saved_items.html can handle data retrieval.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: Adopt a single, vertically stacked content column centered in the viewport, echoing the hero composition from the mockup. All interactive elements (search, filters, list, pagination) live within one central column that is left-aligned internally but framed by wide left/right margins. Above-the-fold, this column is dominated by the search form (taking the role of the hero headline), supported by topic and tag filters beneath it. The listing of articles flows directly below as a linear stack of cards, culminating in centered pagination at the bottom. Supporting controls like saved collections navigation and the save-to-collection controller are attached to the edges of this main column rather than becoming separate sidebars, preserving the monolithic, editorial column feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Adopt a single, vertically stacked content column centered in the viewport, echoing the hero composition from the mockup. All interactive elements (search, filters, list, pagination) live within one central column that is left-aligned internally but framed by wide left/right margins. Above-the-fold, this column is dominated by the search form (taking the role of the hero headline), supported by topic and tag filters beneath it. The listing of articles flows directly below as a linear stack of cards, culminating in centered pagination at the bottom. Supporting controls like saved collections navigation and the save-to-collection controller are attached to the edges of this main column rather than becoming separate sidebars, preserving the monolithic, editorial column feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use approximately 60–70% of the page width for the main content column, centered horizontally, leaving about 15–20% of the width as dark margins on each side. Vertically, the top 25–30% of the viewport is reserved for the search-and-filters band (mirroring a hero section), with generous spacing between saved collections navigation, search, and filters. The article results list occupies the central vertical band (about 50–60% of the scrollable area), with large gaps between cards. The bottom 10–15% of the viewport is intentionally sparse, emphasizing the pagination control centered horizontally with ample breathing room above and below. Floating controls (like the save-to-collection controller) occupy small, non-intrusive regions at the lower right of the content column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, cinematic layout throughout. Each functional block (search, topic filters, tag filters, list, pagination) is visually isolated with generous vertical gaps (typically 32–64px) and ample internal padding (16–32px) inside pill shapes and cards. No component runs edge-to-edge; all are inset within the central column and spaced apart so that individual elements can be scanned at a glance. The listing uses one article per row with clear separation, avoiding cluttered grids. Accent elements (neon lines, pill boundaries) are used sparingly to guide attention without visually overloading the page.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Replicate the mockup’s vertical eye path: the user first encounters a subtle saved collections navigation near the top-right of the main column, then immediately focuses on the large, hero-like search form as the primary focal point. From there, the eye drops to the horizontal topic filter bar, then to the more detailed tag filter panel beneath it. After filters, the gaze flows into the vertical stack of article cards, naturally following the column downward. The path ends on the centered pagination control at the bottom, occupying a similar role to the hero scroll chevron by signaling continuation. A floating save-to-collection controller at the lower-right of the column adds a secondary visual anchor that becomes prominent only in relevant interaction moments.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a matte-black, full-bleed background with a single, tall content column floating in the center, framed by wide dark margins on the left and right. At the top of this column, a discreet ‘saved collections’ link tucks into the upper-right corner, while the main search form stretches out beneath it as a bold, pill-shaped, neon-accented field that visually reads like a hero headline. Directly below, a row of pill-shaped topic filters runs horizontally from the left edge of the column, followed by a collapsible tag filter panel that drops down into view when needed, still aligned to the same left edge. The central portion of the page is a vertical, editorial-like stack of article cards, each full-width within the column but well separated by deep negative space. Toward the bottom, this stack tapers into an airy gap leading to a compact, centered pagination control, echoing the bottom-centered scroll cue of the original hero. As users scroll, a subtle, glowing save-to-collection controller floats near the bottom-right of the content column, reinforcing the futuristic, premium feel while leaving the core reading flow clean and uninterrupted.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and occupies no visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the content hierarchy, initializing data and filter state before any of the visible filter-related components (search form, topic filter bar, tag filter panel, article results list) render. It does not affect the layout of other components; they behave as if it were not present in the visual tree.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual initializer; no spatial footprint and no positioning in the rendered layout. Executes on load and remains invisible during user interaction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is the hero element near the top of the main content column. It is positioned roughly 96px–120px below the global site nav (or top of the viewport) to create a large band of negative space above it, mirroring the mockup’s hero spacing. Horizontally, it sits inside the central content container, which spans about 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered. Within that container, the search form is left-aligned and stretches to about 80–90% of the container’s width, feeling wide but not full-bleed. The form itself is a large pill-shaped bar with 24–32px vertical padding and 24–40px horizontal padding, emphasizing its primary role. Any label or helper text is aligned to the left edge of the input, maintaining a clean vertical alignment line with the filters and article titles below. There is a generous vertical gap (about 32–48px) between the bottom of the search form and the topic-filter-bar, reinforcing its hero-like prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the page, scrolling away naturally as the user moves down. On smaller screens, it expands to nearly 100% of the available width with 16–24px side padding from the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic filter bar sits directly below the search form as a secondary horizontal band of controls. It is left-aligned with the search field, sharing the same left margin inside the central content container and spanning roughly the same usable width (80–90% of the container). The bar is visually realized as a row of pill-shaped filter chips or toggles, each with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, creating a pill-like nav reminiscent of the mockup’s top nav element. The overall vertical height of this band is modest, about 56–72px depending on chip content, with ample negative space above (32–48px from the search form) and below (24–32px to the tag-filter-panel). Chips align in a single row on desktop, wrapping to a second row only when necessary, but always keeping a clean left alignment and leaving generous empty space on the right side of the container to maintain the minimalist feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the content. On smaller screens, chips may wrap to multiple rows while preserving the top-left alignment with the search form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tag filter panel is positioned immediately below the topic filter bar, aligned to the same left edge of the central content column. It occupies the full usable width of that column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport, centered), but its visual density is kept low through generous padding and spacing. When collapsed, it appears as a slim, neon-accented horizontal bar—perhaps a label like 'Refine by tags' with an icon—about 40–56px tall and separated from the topic filter bar by 24–32px of vertical space. When expanded, it drops down into a vertical panel that pushes the article-results-list downward rather than sliding over it, preserving the clean linear flow. Inside the expanded panel, tags may be arranged in one or two columns, but they remain left-aligned within the same internal grid, with 24–32px top and bottom padding and 16–24px side padding. The bottom of the panel sits 40–56px above the first article card, ensuring a clear separation between filtering and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Collapsible and expandable within the normal document flow (no overlay). It scrolls with the page and is not sticky; when expanded, its height increases and pushes subsequent content downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article results list begins just below the tag filter panel, forming the primary reading area of the page. It is a vertical stack of article cards arranged in a single column inside the central content container. The list is left-aligned with the search and filters, taking up approximately 100% of the container’s width (about 60–70% of the viewport), and each card is separated by generous vertical gaps of 32–48px. Each article card has ample internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and sits on a subtly elevated or bordered panel that distinguishes it from the matte-black background while still feeling minimal. Titles, metadata, and actions inside each card align to the same left vertical axis established by the search field, maintaining a precise editorial column. The list extends downward through the middle 50–60% of the page’s scrollable area, leaving notable negative space above the first card (40–56px from the tag panel) and below the final card (64–80px to the pagination) to preserve the airy, cinematic rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrollable content. The number of visible cards adjusts with viewport height, but the single-column, left-aligned structure remains consistent across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed at the very bottom of the article-results-list, acting as a visual and functional terminus for the page section similar to the mockup’s bottom-centered scroll indicator. There is a generous vertical gap of about 64–80px between the last article card and the pagination, creating a calm buffer that draws attention downward. The pagination control itself is horizontally centered within the main content column but appears visually centered in the page by virtue of the symmetric left/right margins. It spans roughly 30–40% of the container’s width, with individual page numbers or arrows arranged in a compact row. The controls are low-profile yet legible: small pill-shaped buttons or minimalist icons with neon accents that glow slightly against the black background. Below the pagination, an additional 48–64px of empty vertical space leads to the bottom of the viewport, reinforcing the sense of an intentional, airy endpoint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline with the flow of the document. It appears after the last results card and scrolls normally; it is not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-to-collection controller manifests as a floating pill-shaped control that becomes visible only when relevant (e.g., when the user selects or saves an article). Spatially, it is pinned to the lower-right region of the central content column rather than the full viewport edge: on desktop, it sits approximately 32px above the bottom of the viewport and aligns horizontally with the right edge of the article cards (about 15–20% in from the overall viewport right edge). The controller is moderately sized—around 260–320px wide—with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, making it feel substantial but not overpowering. It floats over the matte-black background and may overlap the lower portions of article cards slightly, but careful placement ensures it does not obscure key text. Its neon accent and subtle glow give it a noticeable but secondary presence compared to the main content. On mobile, it can span nearly the full width of the content column and be centered horizontally, still offset 24–32px from the bottom of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the viewport while visible, anchored to the lower-right corner of the main content column region. It appears contextually (e.g., when items are added) and persists during scrolling until dismissed or no longer needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved collections navigation is a subtle, tertiary control positioned at the top of the content column, slightly above or aligned with the top edge of the search form. It is anchored to the right side of the central container, forming a small text-and-icon link such as 'My reading lists' with a chevron or folder icon. Horizontally, it sits flush with the right edge of the search form and article cards, creating a clean right alignment that balances the dominant left alignment of the main text. Vertically, it is separated from the search form by a modest gap of 16–24px (if above) or 0–8px (if aligned on the same baseline), with approximately 64–96px of empty space above it to the top of the viewport, echoing the airy band above the hero nav in the mockup. Its visual styling is understated—smaller type and lighter weight than the main search label—so it is discoverable without competing with the search form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the page. It scrolls away naturally as the user moves down and does not remain sticky or fixed.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays the full content for a specific article, allows saving the article into named reading lists, and enables navigation to related articles and the main Resources listing.

core_features: Load and display a single article’s full details using getArticleDetail, based on the articleId URL parameter.
Show article metadata including author, publication date, topic, tags, and estimated reading time.
Display the article’s hero media and full content body.
Provide a Save/Bookmark control that uses saveItemToNamedCollection to add the current article into a named collection.
Offer a workflow to save the article into a new or existing reading list by entering a collection name and choosing a collection type.
Show feedback about the success or failure of the save operation using the response from saveItemToNamedCollection.
Display related articles (from getArticleDetail) and allow navigation to another Article Detail page via articleId in the URL.
Provide navigation back to the Resources listing (resources.html) via a breadcrumb or back link.
user_workflows: View Article Content: 1) User opens article_detail.html with ?articleId=... in the URL from resources.html, index.html, or saved_items.html. 2) On page load, the article-detail-loader component reads articleId from the URL and calls getArticleDetail(articleId). 3) While loading, a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the page populates the header, metadata, hero media, and content components with the returned article data. 5) If getArticleDetail fails or returns no article, an error message is shown and a link back to resources.html is provided.

Save Article to Reading List: 1) While viewing an article, the user clicks the Save or Bookmark button in the article-save-control component. 2) The reading-list-modal opens, showing an input for Collection name and a selector for Collection type (for example, a reading list type). 3) The user enters a collection name (for a new or existing list) and selects a collection type. 4) The user confirms the action. 5) The article-save-control calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType set to 'article', itemId set to the current article’s id, collectionName set to the entered collection name, and collectionType set to the selected type. 6) On success, the modal closes and the save-feedback-banner displays a confirmation using the message from the API. 7) On failure, the modal keeps open and displays the error message so the user can adjust and retry.

Navigate to Related Article: 1) While viewing an article, the related-articles-list component shows a list of relatedArticles returned by getArticleDetail. 2) The user clicks on a related article entry. 3) The browser navigates to article_detail.html with ?articleId=<related article id>. 4) The article-detail-loader detects the changed articleId, calls getArticleDetail for the new article, and updates all article components accordingly.

Return to Resources Listing: 1) The breadcrumb/back link component is visible on the Article Detail page. 2) The user clicks the 'Back to Resources' link. 3) The browser navigates to resources.html without additional parameters, returning the user to the Resources & Blog listing.
interactions: onInit (page load): article-detail-loader reads the articleId query parameter from the URL. If present, it calls getArticleDetail(articleId) and sets loading=true. If not present, it sets an error state indicating that no article was specified.

onGetArticleDetailSuccess: When getArticleDetail resolves successfully, the returned article object is stored in page state (e.g., currentArticle) and the relatedArticles array is stored (e.g., relatedArticles). Loading is set to false. Any existing error state is cleared.

onGetArticleDetailError: If getArticleDetail fails (network error, invalid articleId, or empty result), loading=false and an error state is set. The article-header, article-body-content, and related-articles-list components read this state and either hide content or show fallback error messaging.
onClickBackToResources: The article-breadcrumb-nav component triggers navigation to resources.html (no parameters) when the user clicks the back link.

onClickSaveArticle: The article-save-control component opens the reading-list-modal when the user clicks the Save/Bookmark button, but only if currentArticle is loaded and no article load error is present.

onChangeCollectionName: Within reading-list-modal, as the user types into the collection name input, the local modal state is updated (e.g., draftCollectionName) to be later sent to saveItemToNamedCollection.

onChangeCollectionType: Within reading-list-modal, when the user changes the collection type selector, the local modal state (e.g., draftCollectionType) is updated to that enum value to be passed to saveItemToNamedCollection.

onSubmitSaveToCollection: When the user confirms saving in reading-list-modal, validation ensures that a non-empty collection name and a valid collectionType enum value are present. Then article-save-control calls saveItemToNamedCollection with itemType='article', itemId=currentArticle.id, collectionName=draftCollectionName, and collectionType=draftCollectionType. While awaiting the response, the modal may show a saving state to prevent duplicate submissions.

onSaveItemToNamedCollectionSuccess: When saveItemToNamedCollection returns success=true, the modal closes, and save-feedback-banner is updated with the message from the response and information about whether a new collection was created (createdNewCollection) or an existing one was used. Any error indicators in the modal are cleared.

onSaveItemToNamedCollectionErrorOrFailure: If the call fails technically or returns success=false, the modal remains open and displays the returned message as an error. The user can adjust the collection name/type and resubmit.

onClickRelatedArticle: In the related-articles-list, clicking a related article triggers navigation to article_detail.html?articleId=<selected related article’s id>. This updates the URL and causes a full article reload sequence through article-detail-loader.

onUrlChangeArticleId: If the articleId query parameter changes (for example, via in-page navigation to a related article or manual URL edit), article-detail-loader re-runs getArticleDetail with the new articleId and resets its loading and error states accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of page state is the articleId URL parameter. On initial load and on any subsequent URL change, article-detail-loader reads window.location.search to extract articleId. If articleId is defined and non-empty, it calls getArticleDetail(articleId). If articleId is missing, the page enters an error state that prevents article content from rendering and instead shows an error message and a back link to resources.html. The loaded article and relatedArticles from getArticleDetail are stored in in-memory page state (e.g., currentArticle and relatedArticles) and are not persisted to localStorage or other long-term storage; they are lost when the page is reloaded. The Save workflow does not modify URL parameters; calls to saveItemToNamedCollection only affect backend SavedCollection and SavedCollectionItem records and return transient feedback for this view. Navigation to another article always updates the articleId query parameter (e.g., article_detail.html?articleId=<id>); this URL parameter is the single source of truth determining which article is displayed. No state is stored in localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms; all durable changes go through the provided interfaces.

components.functionality: Reads the articleId query parameter from the URL, calls getArticleDetail to retrieve the article and its related articles, and exposes loading, error, and data states to other components on the page.

Displays a simple breadcrumb or back link that lets the user navigate back to the Resources & Blog listing page (resources.html). Optionally reflects the current article’s topic or title, if available, but functions even when article loading fails by still providing a back link.

Shows the article’s title and high-level metadata such as author, published_at, topic, tags, and reading_time_minutes when available. If the article is still loading, this component reads the loading state from article-detail-loader and displays a loading state instead of data.

Displays the article’s hero_image if present. When no hero_image exists or the article failed to load, it either remains empty or follows a generic fallback behavior informed by the article-detail-loader’s state.

Renders the full article content field. When loading, it may show a placeholder based on the loader’s state. When an error is present (e.g., invalid articleId), it hides content and defers to a centralized error display.
Shows error messages when getArticleDetail fails or when articleId is missing from the URL. Provides a clear message and a link back to the Resources listing so the user can recover.

Provides a Save/Bookmark button for the current article. When clicked, it opens the reading-list-modal for the user to specify a collection name and type, and then calls saveItemToNamedCollection to persist the selection.

Appears when the user chooses to save an article. Captures the collectionName via a text input and collectionType via a selector bound to valid SavedCollection.collection_type enum values. On submit, it passes this data back to article-save-control, which triggers saveItemToNamedCollection. Handles basic validation and displays any error messages returned from the API while keeping the modal open for corrections.

Displays the outcome of the most recent saveItemToNamedCollection call, showing success or error messages and whether the article was added to a new or existing collection, based on the collection and createdNewCollection fields returned from the API.

Displays the list of relatedArticles returned by getArticleDetail. Each item is clickable; clicking navigates to article_detail.html with that related article’s id in the articleId query parameter, causing a reload of the article content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: All article content sits in a single, vertically stacked column that is horizontally centered on the page but left-aligned within that column. At the top of the article view, a pill-shaped breadcrumb bar echoes the hero nav from the mockup. Below it, the article header behaves like a hero: large title as the main focal point, followed by a neon-accented subheading/meta strip. Beneath this, the feature media, then the long-form body, and finally a spacious related-articles section. Functional UI (save control, status banner, loader, error, modal) is layered on top of or beside this main column, without breaking the clean vertical flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All article content sits in a single, vertically stacked column that is horizontally centered on the page but left-aligned within that column. At the top of the article view, a pill-shaped breadcrumb bar echoes the hero nav from the mockup. Below it, the article header behaves like a hero: large title as the main focal point, followed by a neon-accented subheading/meta strip. Beneath this, the feature media, then the long-form body, and finally a spacious related-articles section. Functional UI (save control, status banner, loader, error, modal) is layered on top of or beside this main column, without breaking the clean vertical flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving 15–20% dark margins on each side to create a cinematic frame. Vertically, the top breadcrumb and header collectively occupy about the upper 35–45% of the initial viewport, the media and start of the body take up the mid zone, and the lower 15–20% of the first screen remains relatively open, echoing the empty space around the scroll indicator in the mockup. Major transitions (header to media, media to body, body to related) are separated by 64–96px of vertical spacing, maintaining a premium, airy feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is low-density and deliberately sparse. Each major element (breadcrumb pill, header block, media, body, related list) has generous padding and margin around it. Text lines are kept to a comfortable width within the central column, with ample line-height and paragraph spacing. Actions like the save control and banners are single, minimal elements rather than clusters of controls, avoiding UI noise and preserving the editorial, high-end tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the pill-shaped breadcrumb at the top center of the content column, then drops immediately to the bold article title. From there it follows down to the neon-accented subheading/metadata, continuing downward to the wide hero media. The body content then becomes the main reading track. After the main text, the eye settles on the related-articles section at the bottom. System feedback (save banner) appears briefly near the top-center of the content column, and the save control remains discoverable near the top-right of the header zone, but both defer visually to the title and main reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The Article Detail page presents as a dark, cinematic canvas with a single, centered content column floating within wide matte-black side margins. At the top of this column, a pill-shaped breadcrumb bar stretches across most of its width, acting as a subtle, glowing header strip. Below, the article header occupies the upper-middle of the first viewport like a hero: a large, bold title aligned to the left, with a slim neon accent and succinct subheading/meta beneath it. A discrete, pill-like save control sits aligned to the same horizontal system, slightly offset to the right, reading as a refined utility rather than a dominant CTA. Generous vertical breathing room separates this header block from a wide, high-impact media element that aligns with the same left edge of the text column. The long-form body text flows below in a comfortable, narrow width, with substantial spacing between paragraphs and sections, maintaining the premium, editorial tone. At the bottom of the scroll, a related-articles section appears as a quiet, structured grid under a neon-marked heading, extending the story without overcrowding the page. Overlays (loader, error state, reading list modal, save feedback banner) appear centered over the content column, respecting its width and alignment, and using soft neon details to stay on-brand without disrupting the minimalist, XR-inspired atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader veils the main article content area starting just below the site’s global navigation and spanning the full height of the viewport. It respects the central column structure: a semi-transparent matte-black overlay covers the entire viewport, but the visual focus is a single, minimal loading indicator centered horizontally within the content column and vertically around the middle of the screen. The indicator sits within an invisible 240–320px square area with ample empty space around it, reinforcing the low-density aesthetic. The breadcrumb, header, and other content are visible but subdued underneath, blurred or dimmed slightly to keep focus on the loader.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport, covering all interactive content while loading. The central loading indicator remains vertically and horizontally centered within the viewport regardless of scroll position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation appears at the top of the article view as a wide, pill-shaped bar, echoing the hero nav from the mockup. It is horizontally centered within the page, aligned to the central content column, and spans roughly 70–80% of that column’s width, leaving equal dark margins on its left and right. Vertically, it sits just below the global site navigation, occupying about 10–12% of the first viewport height, with 24–32px of clear space above and below it. The breadcrumb text inside is left-aligned, starting at the same left edge that the article title will use below, reinforcing the column feel. The pill has generous horizontal padding (24–32px) and a low profile height, with subtle neon edges, making it present but not heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element positioned at the top of the article content stack; scrolls away with the page once the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header behaves like a hero block occupying the upper-middle of the first viewport. It sits directly beneath the breadcrumb, separated by 48–64px of vertical space. The header is contained within the same central column (about 60–70% of viewport width), left-aligned. The main title uses large, bold typography spanning 2–3 lines and visually occupying about 40–50% of the column’s height on initial load. It begins flush with the left alignment of the breadcrumb text above. Below the title, with 24–32px of vertical gap, is a compact subheader/meta strip: a thin vertical neon bar (about 2–4px wide, 32–40px tall) sits just to the left of a single line (or two short lines) of subheading text and key metadata (e.g., author, date). This subheader block is no more than 1/4 of the column width, keeping it visually light. Extra negative space (48–64px) below the header ensures the area feels spacious and cinematic, mirroring the mockup’s hero spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; visually anchors the top of the article content and scrolls off-screen as the user reads further.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article media display sits directly below the header block, separated by a generous 64px vertical gap to maintain the airy hero-to-content transition. It aligns with the same left edge as the title and subheading, reinforcing the strong column. On desktop, the media spans approximately 80–90% of the content column’s width, leaving a subtle margin on the right to preserve negative space and avoid feeling full-bleed. The height is responsive but tuned so that the media occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport height on initial view, creating a strong visual anchor without overwhelming the page. The media box sits alone in its band, with 48–64px of clear space above and below before the body text begins, echoing the isolated, cinematic elements of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block within the main scroll. Media scales responsively with viewport width while preserving the overall aspect ratio and central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The body content begins 64px below the bottom edge of the media display, continuing within the same central column. Text is left-aligned and constrained to a comfortable reading width—approximately 50–65 characters per line—which typically matches about 55–65% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving additional breathing room to the right. Paragraphs are separated by 16–24px of vertical space, and section breaks or subheadings use larger 32–48px spacing, creating a clear hierarchy while maintaining low visual density. Any inline images or pull quotes align to the primary text column and maintain the same left edge; they are individually isolated with 32–48px of top and bottom margin. The result is a smooth, uninterrupted vertical reading track that feels premium and editorial.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content forming the core of the page. It grows vertically as needed but always respects the central column width and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs, the error state replaces the main article content within the central column. It is vertically centered in the first viewport, aligning with the same left edge as the article title would. The error message area occupies roughly the middle third of the viewport height. A subtle neon-accented vertical bar on the left leads into a concise error headline and short explanatory body text, all left-aligned and constrained to the same text width as the article body. Surrounding this block is abundant negative space: at least 64px padding above and below the message, with no competing elements, creating a calm, focused state that mirrors the hero’s minimalism.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces or overlays the article content region within the standard scroll flow; if used as an overlay, it’s centered and fixed in the viewport until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-to-reading-list control is positioned within the article header zone as a refined, secondary element. On desktop, it aligns with the top or mid-height of the title block but sits toward the right side of the central column. Imagine the title occupying the left two-thirds of the column and the save control occupying a small area in the rightmost third, with at least 24–32px of clear space from the column’s right edge. The control itself is a pill-shaped button or icon-with-label with 12–16px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding, referencing the nav pill style but scaled down. There is at least 24px of horizontal separation between the save control and the end of the title text, so nothing feels cramped. On narrower viewports, it drops below the title but retains left alignment with the main text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially inline with the header; on scroll, it can become a small sticky element pinned near the top-right of the content column (not the full viewport), remaining visible while reading but staying within the central column’s width and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list modal appears as a central overlay on a dimmed background when the save control is activated. A translucent, matte-black scrim covers the entire viewport, while the modal itself is centered both horizontally and vertically. Its width is about 40–50% of the viewport (capped to the central column width), and it uses softened, pill-like or subtly rounded corners that echo the nav pill, with a thin neon outline or edge accent. Inside, content is vertically stacked: title at the top, list of reading lists in the middle, and actions at the bottom, all left-aligned. The modal has generous internal padding—32–40px on all sides—and plenty of whitespace between list items (16–24px), keeping the interaction light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay centered in the viewport, blocking interaction with underlying content until closed. Remains centered regardless of scroll position, with the background content slightly dimmed or blurred.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save feedback banner appears as a slim, pill-like bar near the top of the central content column, just below the global nav and overlapping slightly above the breadcrumb pill or sitting in the gap between the global nav and the breadcrumb. It is horizontally centered and spans about 40–50% of the column width—wider than the modal but narrower than the breadcrumb nav—so it feels like a lightweight message rather than a full section. The banner’s height is modest, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. It uses a subtle neon glow or edge to denote success or error without being visually aggressive. There is ample empty space (at least 16–24px) above and below the banner so it floats visually, then slides or fades away after a short duration.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed near the top of the viewport, aligned to the central column. Appears above the content on save actions, then auto-dismisses or can be manually dismissed; does not affect layout of underlying components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related articles section sits at the bottom of the article detail page, beginning after a generous 96px vertical gap from the end of the body content. It occupies the same central column width and left alignment. At the top of this section, a small heading is introduced with a thin vertical neon bar on the left, mirroring the subheading style in the hero: the bar is 2–4px wide and 32–40px tall, followed by the section label (e.g., “Related articles”) to its right. Below this heading, related articles are presented in a spacious grid or stacked layout: on desktop, 2–3 cards per row depending on width, each card spanning about 30–45% of the column width with at least 24–32px horizontal and 32–48px vertical spacing between cards. Cards themselves are minimal—thumbnail, title, a small meta line—surrounded by ample padding and dark negative space so the section feels like an elegant extension of the article rather than a cluttered promo block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored at the bottom of the main scroll. Expands vertically based on the number of related articles while maintaining the central column and generous spacing.

name: Events & Workshops Listing
title: Events & Workshops
description: Page for browsing and filtering upcoming XR events and workshops, with dynamic filters for topic, level, date range, and price, and navigation to event detail pages.

core_features: Initialize available filter options (topics, levels, price range bounds) by calling getEventFilterOptions when the page loads.
Read URL parameters (topic, level, maxPrice, startDate, endDate, page, pageSize) on load and use them to set the initial filter state and pagination state.
Execute event search by calling searchEvents with the current filter and pagination state, and render the returned Event results.
Display each event/workshop in a list with key information from the Event model (e.g., title, start date/time, topic, level, price, format, location, remaining spots if available).
Allow users to refine results using topic, level, date range, and price range filters; each filter change updates both the URL parameters and triggers a new searchEvents call.
Provide pagination controls that update the current page (and optionally page size) via URL parameters and refresh search results using searchEvents.
Show the currently applied filters as removable items; removing an applied filter updates the URL parameters, the internal filter state, and refreshes search results via searchEvents.
Provide a clear way to reset all filters to default (unfiltered) state, updating URL parameters accordingly and re-running searchEvents.
Allow users to click an event card to navigate to event_detail.html, passing the selected event.id as the eventId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive from another page with pre-selected filters
1. User clicks an 'Events & Workshops' link or CTA on index.html or resources.html that appends one or more URL parameters (e.g., topic, level, maxPrice, startDate, endDate).
2. events.html loads and reads the URL parameters.
3. The Filter Initialization component calls getEventFilterOptions and sets up the filter UI.
4. The Filters Form component applies the URL parameter values as the initial filter state (selects matching topic/level options when available; sets date and price controls).
5. The Events Results List component calls searchEvents with filters derived from the URL state and with page and pageSize (defaulted if not present).
6. The user sees a list of events that already reflect the pre-selected filters.
7. If the user adjusts filters (e.g., changes topic or level), the filter state updates, the URL parameters are updated, and searchEvents is called again to refresh the list.

Workflow: Find a beginner workshop matching date and budget constraints
1. User lands on events.html (either via navigation or a direct URL) without specific filters or with only some pre-set.
2. On load, the page calls getEventFilterOptions and renders filter controls.
3. User sets level to a desired value using the level filter control.
4. User sets a date range using the start date and end date controls.
5. User sets a maximum price using the price range control.
6. Each change updates the internal filter state, synchronizes the corresponding URL parameters (level, startDate, endDate, maxPrice), and invokes searchEvents with the updated filters and current page reset to the first page.
7. The Events Results List refreshes to show only events matching the selected level, falling within the specified date range, and priced at or below the selected maximum.

Workflow: Explore events by topic and then view details
1. User opens events.html, which initializes filters via getEventFilterOptions and loads initial search results via searchEvents.
2. User selects a topic from the topic filter control.
3. The topic value is applied to the filter state, written to the topic URL parameter, and searchEvents is called with an updated filters object and page reset to the first page.
4. The event list updates to show events for that topic.
5. User scrolls through the results and clicks on an event card.
6. The click handler navigates to event_detail.html with eventId set to the selected Event.id (e.g., event_detail.html?eventId=<Event.id>).
7. The event_detail.html page uses the eventId to show full information and registration options (handled on that page).

Workflow: Paginate through many events
1. User is viewing search results on events.html with some filters applied.
2. Initial results are loaded via searchEvents using filters from the current URL state and page/pageSize (default or from URL).
3. User clicks the pagination control for the next page.
4. The pagination component updates the page URL parameter (and pageSize if changed), keeps the current filters unchanged, and triggers searchEvents with the same filters but with the updated page value.
5. The Events Results List renders the new subset of results.
6. If the user changes a filter after navigating to a later page, the page is reset to 1 in state and in the URL, and searchEvents is called again for page 1 to avoid empty results.

Workflow: Clear filters and start over
1. User has multiple filters applied (topic, level, date range, maxPrice) and is viewing paginated results.
2. User clicks a 'Reset filters' interaction.
3. The filter state is cleared to default values, and all related URL parameters (topic, level, startDate, endDate, maxPrice) are removed or reset; page is set to 1.
4. searchEvents is called with an empty or default filters object and page = 1.
5. Events Results List updates to show the full set of upcoming events (as allowed by the system defaults).
interactions:
Filter option loading
- On page load, a component calls getEventFilterOptions (no parameters).
- On success, the response is bound to the topic and level filter controls and to the initial price range bounds.
- If the URL already specifies values (e.g., topic, level, maxPrice), the component maps those values to the loaded options and sets the filter controls accordingly.

Filter changes (topic, level, date range, price)
- When a user changes the topic control:
  - Update internal filter state: filters.topic.
  - Update the topic URL parameter to match the new value (or remove it if cleared).
  - Reset page to 1 in state and in the page URL parameter.
  - Call searchEvents with the updated filters and page/pageSize.
- When a user changes the level control:
  - Update filters.level.
  - Update the level URL parameter (or remove if cleared).
  - Reset page to 1 and call searchEvents.
- When a user changes the start date or end date controls:
  - Update filters.dateFrom and/or filters.dateTo.
  - Update startDate and endDate URL parameters using ISO date string format (or remove when cleared).
  - Reset page to 1 and call searchEvents.
- When a user changes the maximum price control:
  - Update filters.maxPrice.
  - Update the maxPrice URL parameter (or remove when cleared).
  - Reset page to 1 and call searchEvents.

Search execution via searchEvents
- Any change to filters or pagination triggers a call to searchEvents with:
  - filters: object containing the current topic, level, price, and date constraints (if set).
  - page: current page index derived from state/URL.
  - pageSize: current page size derived from state/URL or a default.
- While the request is in flight, a loading state is shown and previous results are optionally dimmed or kept.
- On success, totalCount and results are used to update the Events Results List and Pagination Controls.
- On error, an error state is displayed with a retry action that re-invokes searchEvents with the same parameters.

Pagination interactions
- When user clicks 'Next page':
  - Increment page by 1 in internal state.
  - Update page URL parameter.
  - Call searchEvents with existing filters and new page.
- When user clicks 'Previous page':
  - Decrement page (not below 1) in state and URL.
  - Call searchEvents.
- When user selects a specific page number or page size (if available):
  - Update page or pageSize in state.
  - Update corresponding URL parameters.
  - Call searchEvents.
- Pagination controls disable or hide navigation beyond available pages determined by totalCount and pageSize.

Applied filter summary chip interactions
- Each applied filter (topic, level, start date, end date, maxPrice) is represented as a removable item.
- When user clicks 'remove' on a filter item:
  - Clear the corresponding value in the filter state.
  - Remove the corresponding URL parameter.
  - Reset page to 1 in state and URL.
  - Call searchEvents with updated filters.
- When user clicks 'Clear all':
  - Clear all filter values in state.
  - Remove all filter-related URL parameters.
  - Reset page to 1 in state and URL.
  - Call searchEvents with default filters.

Event card navigation
- Each event result is rendered as a clickable card or row.
- When a user clicks an event card:
  - The click handler reads the Event.id for that item.
  - The browser navigates to event_detail.html with eventId set as a URL parameter (e.g., event_detail.html?eventId=<Event.id>).
- No data is stored in localStorage; the event detail page is responsible for using eventId to fetch details.

URL parameter synchronization
- On initial load:
  - The page parses existing URL parameters (topic, level, maxPrice, startDate, endDate, page, pageSize) and builds an initial filters object and pagination state.
  - After getEventFilterOptions returns, the filters UI is set to match this state.
  - searchEvents is called immediately with this combined state.
- On subsequent user actions (filter changes, pagination):
  - The page updates URL parameters via the history API or full reload link navigation while keeping the state consistent.
  - The current state can always be reconstructed from the URL alone, ensuring shareable and bookmarkable filtered views.

Empty and edge state handling
- If searchEvents returns totalCount = 0:
  - The Events Results List shows an empty state message indicating no events match the current filters.
  - A control is offered to clear filters or adjust them; activating it resets filters and updates the URL before re-calling searchEvents.
- If filters include invalid or unsupported values from the URL (e.g., unknown topic):
  - The filter initialization logic either ignores the invalid value or clears it.
  - URL parameters may be normalized or cleaned when the user next changes filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for persistent state, with an in-memory state synchronized to them. On page load, the script parses the following URL parameters: topic, level, maxPrice, startDate, endDate, page, pageSize. These values are mapped into an internal state object { filters: { topic, level, maxPrice, dateFrom, dateTo }, pagination: { page, pageSize } }. The Filter Initialization component then calls getEventFilterOptions to populate available selections and clamps or normalizes any incoming values (for example, ensuring page is at least 1). After filters are ready, the page calls searchEvents with the current filters and pagination state. Any user interaction that changes filters or pagination mutates the internal state and simultaneously updates the URL parameters to match (adding, updating, or removing parameters). The page never uses localStorage or other persistent client storage; refreshing or sharing the URL fully reconstructs state from these parameters. The filters passed to searchEvents are always derived from the current URL-synchronized state, ensuring consistency between the visible filters, the URL, and the displayed results.

components.functionality: On page load, fetches available filter options for topic, level, and price range by calling getEventFilterOptions, and merges them with URL-derived state to initialize the filter form and internal filter state.

Displays and manages interactive controls for topic, level, date range (start and end), and maximum price. Synchronizes user selections with URL parameters and triggers searchEvents whenever filters change.
data_binding: URL parameters: topic, level, maxPrice, startDate, endDate

components.functionality: Renders a summary of all currently applied filters based on the synchronized filter state and URL parameters, and allows users to remove individual filters or clear all filters.
data_binding: URL parameters: topic, level, maxPrice, startDate, endDate

components.functionality: Coordinates calling searchEvents whenever filters or pagination change, manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting Event collection and totalCount to the Events Results List and Pagination Controls.

Displays the list of Event items returned by searchEvents, showing key information for each event and rendering an Event Card component for each result. Handles empty states when no events are returned.

Represents a single Event result as a clickable unit that shows basic event details such as title, timing, topic, level, price, format, location, and remaining spots if available. On click, navigates to the event_detail.html page with eventId set to the Event.id in the URL.

Controls navigation between pages of search results. Reads totalCount and current page/pageSize to render page navigation, updates URL parameters for page and pageSize, and notifies the controller when pagination changes so it can call searchEvents.
Displays a loading state while getEventFilterOptions or searchEvents are in progress, based on signals from the Filter Initializer and Events Results Controller.

Shows an error message and retry action when getEventFilterOptions or searchEvents fail, without hardcoding specific error text. The retry action re-invokes the respective interface with the last known parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit inside a single, tall, dark hero-like section that extends into a scrollable listing. At the top, a wide, pill-shaped filters bar replaces the hero nav pill and acts as the primary control surface. Directly beneath it, still in a single vertical column, are the applied filters and result controls, aligned to the left. Below that, the events results list forms a vertical stack of large, spacious event cards. At the very bottom of this central column, pagination controls sit centered horizontally, echoing the neon scroll indicator position from the mockup. Status indicators (loading/error) occupy the results list region without breaking the overarching column structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit inside a single, tall, dark hero-like section that extends into a scrollable listing. At the top, a wide, pill-shaped filters bar replaces the hero nav pill and acts as the primary control surface. Directly beneath it, still in a single vertical column, are the applied filters and result controls, aligned to the left. Below that, the events results list forms a vertical stack of large, spacious event cards. At the very bottom of this central column, pagination controls sit centered horizontally, echoing the neon scroll indicator position from the mockup. Status indicators (loading/error) occupy the results list region without breaking the overarching column structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered horizontally, with 15–20% dark margins on both sides. The filters pill occupies the top 10–15% of the viewport height. The block containing applied filters, result controls, and the start of the events list fills the mid 50–60% of the viewport, with generous vertical spacing between each group. The pagination sits in the lower 10–15% of the viewport (when content height allows), visually floating in space. Vertical spacing between major clusters (filters bar, filter summary/controllers, results list, pagination) is in the 48–64px range to maintain the cinematic feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low and premium. Each functional group is isolated with ample padding and breathing room: the filters bar is a large pill with generous internal padding and clear separation between controls; applied filters and result controls share a light, editorial row with space around them; event cards are large, full-width tiles with substantial internal padding and clear separation through 32–40px gaps. Only the event cards themselves contain denser information, but still with clear hierarchy and neon accents for key metadata.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-center filters pill (primary interactive focus, analogous to the nav pill), then moves downward along the left-aligned column to the applied filters summary and results controller. From there, attention flows directly into the vertical stack of event cards, scanning top-to-bottom. The dark negative space on the sides keeps focus on this central column. Finally, the flow ends at the center-bottom pagination controls, which occupy an isolated, neon-accented zone that echoes the original scroll chevron’s role as an invitation to continue exploring.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a dark, matte, full-bleed viewport with a strong central column framed by wide left and right margins. Near the top, a wide, pill-shaped filters bar floats horizontally, centered in the viewport but with all its contents aligned to the left inside the pill. Beneath it, within the same invisible central column, a single horizontal row houses the applied filters summary on the left and compact results controls on the right, feeling editorial and lightweight. Dropping further down, a vertical stack of large, full-width event cards occupies the core of the page, each card breathing in its own negative space against the black background. The sides of the screen remain mostly empty, enhancing focus on the content column. At the bottom of the list, pagination controls sit centered horizontally with generous spacing above and below, functioning as a grounded, neon-accented anchor similar to a scroll indicator. During loading or error states, the central events region temporarily swaps the cards for a minimal, high-contrast status message that stays within the same column, preserving the calm, cinematic structure of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-initializer operates behind the scenes and does not occupy visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it is attached to the full-page container, initializing filters and results before the visible components appear. From a spatial standpoint, it sits at the very top of the DOM structure but is non-rendering, so it does not affect the alignment, spacing, or proportions of the central column, the filters pill, or the events list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual system component; behaves as a full-page data layer with no rendered footprint and no influence on layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters-form is the visual anchor at the top of the page, replacing the hero nav pill concept with a functional control surface. It appears as a wide, pill-shaped bar positioned near the top center of the viewport, leaving approximately 64–80px of vertical space from the very top edge before the pill begins. Horizontally, the pill spans about 70% of the viewport width and is centered, leaving 15% dark margin on each side. Inside the pill, all filter controls (topic, level, date range, price) are left-aligned along a single baseline, arranged in a horizontal sequence with generous 24–32px gaps. The pill has 16–24px vertical and 24–40px horizontal internal padding, giving the controls plenty of breathing room. The bottom of the pill is separated from the row below (applied filters + controllers) by a generous 32–48px gap of empty dark space to keep it visually distinct and premium.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a sticky control bar on desktop: it pins to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past its initial position, maintaining its width and centered placement while the events list scrolls beneath it. On initial load, it scrolls naturally with the page until reaching the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-summary sits immediately below the filters-form, aligned to the same left edge as the content inside the pill, reinforcing the invisible central column. It shares a horizontal row with the events-results-controller: the applied filters occupy the left two-thirds of the central column, presenting as a loose row (or two rows if wrapped) of compact neon-accented chips or labels. There is approximately 24–32px vertical spacing between the bottom of the filters pill and the top of the first row of chips. Horizontally, the chips begin at the central column’s left edge and flow rightward, with 8–16px gaps between them. The entire applied-filters area is visually light and airy, leaving at least 16–24px of vertical space beneath it before the event cards begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline with the central column content. When filters overflow, the component expands vertically but maintains its alignment and spacing, pushing the events list downward while preserving the overall negative space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-results-controller is positioned on the same horizontal line as the applied-filters-summary but docked to the right within the central content column, creating a balanced, editorial row. It occupies roughly the right one-third of the column width. Controls like sort dropdowns or view toggles are right-aligned within this area, with subtle neon accents for active states. The top edge of this controller aligns vertically with the top of the applied filters chips on the left, forming a clean horizontal rule across the column. There is consistent 24–32px spacing from the bottom of the filters pill above and 24–32px spacing before the first event card below, so this row feels like a slim, refined band dividing filters from results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the central column. On narrower screens it naturally reflows below the applied-filters-summary, still left-aligned but maintaining generous vertical spacing. It scrolls with the page and is not sticky, keeping the sticky behavior reserved for the primary filters-form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-results-list begins directly below the row formed by applied-filters-summary and events-results-controller, separated by a clear 32–40px vertical gap of empty space. It occupies the same central content column as the filters, spanning approximately 60–70% of the viewport width and centered horizontally, with the left edge aligned precisely to the left edge of the filters chips and the contents of the filters pill. Within this column, the list is a simple, vertical stack of event-card components, each full-width within the column. The list extends downward through the middle portion of the page, leaving the side margins in deep shadow, emphasizing the linear, top-to-bottom reading. Between each card there is 32–40px of vertical spacing, so each event feels like its own feature block rather than part of a dense list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically within the page beneath the sticky filters-form. The list expands to fit the number of events; pagination or infinite scroll boundaries appear well below the fold, maintaining the central column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each event-card is a large, horizontally oriented tile that fills the width of the central content column (about 60–70% of viewport width), with its left edge locked to the column’s left alignment and side margins of 15–20% on either side. Vertically, a card is tall enough to accommodate title, date/time, location, level, short description, and a primary call-to-action, typically in the 180–260px height range depending on content. Internally, the card uses generous padding: roughly 24–32px on all sides. The main event title sits in the upper-left of the card in bold, large-scale type, with supporting metadata (dates, tags, price) aligned beneath or to the right, separated by 8–16px gaps. A subtle neon accent—such as a thin vertical bar or small tag—runs near the left edge to echo the hero’s neon line motif. The bottom of each card leaves 24–32px of clear space before the next card begins, reinforcing the spacious, editorial rhythm of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rigidly anchored within the vertical flow of the results list; cards do not overlap or float. On hover or focus, cards may subtly elevate or glow but never break the underlying alignment, maintaining the clean columnar structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are positioned at the bottom of the events-results-list, centered horizontally within the same central column. They are separated from the last event-card by a generous 40–64px vertical gap of empty space, so they appear as a distinct band rather than attached directly to the last card. The controls themselves (page numbers, next/previous, or a ‘load more’ button) form a compact, neon-accented cluster roughly 25–35% of the column width, visually floating in the middle of the page width. When the total page height is relatively short, this cluster tends to sit in the lower 15–20% of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s centered-bottom scroll indicator. Ample dark space surrounds it on all sides, making it an unmistakable end-of-list cue.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the central column content. It scrolls with the page and becomes visible only once the user reaches the bottom of the current results set; no sticky behavior is used here to keep the bottom interaction feeling deliberate and grounded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears within the boundaries of the events-results-list region, taking over the space where event cards would normally render. It is centered horizontally within the central content column and vertically positioned so that, on typical desktop viewports, it appears near the center of the visible area beneath the filters row. Visually, it may be a minimal neon spinner or pulsing double-chevron set, with a short label underneath, both left-aligned or center-aligned depending on the chosen motif but constrained to the width of the column. Surrounding it is generous negative space—no cards, no pagination—so that the loading state feels calm and intentional rather than cramped. The filters-form and applied-filters-summary remain visible above, reinforcing system responsiveness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an overlay state within the results list area, temporarily replacing the event cards and pagination. It does not shift the position of the filters-form or applied-filters-summary and disappears once data is loaded, restoring the normal vertical stack of cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state-message also occupies the events-results-list region when an error occurs, replacing the list of cards. It is placed within the same central content column, with its main text block aligned to the left edge of where event titles would normally begin, maintaining consistency with the overall alignment. The message is located roughly in the middle of the vertical space between the filters row and where the pagination would appear, often near the visual center of the viewport. A concise headline (e.g., ‘Unable to load events’) appears in bold, followed by a short explanatory line and a neon-accented retry action. Surrounding the message is ample dark space on all sides, with at least 32–48px of margin above and below, making the state feel intentional and readable rather than like a cramped alert.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the events list: it appears only when errors occur and occupies the list’s footprint, pushing pagination off-screen or hiding it. It scrolls naturally with the page, but in most cases sits well within the initial viewport given the ample negative space above and below.

name: Event Detail & Registration
title: Event Detail & Registration
description: Displays full details for a selected event or workshop and lets users register by submitting a registration form, using the eventId URL parameter and the available event interfaces.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific event or workshop using the eventId URL parameter and getEventDetail.
Show key event metadata such as topic, level, date and time, format, location, price, currency, capacity, and remaining spots when available from the Event model.
Provide a persistent primary call-to-action button (e.g., 'Register' or 'Reserve Seat') that scrolls or jumps to the registration form on the same page.
Render an event registration form bound to submitEventRegistration, capturing full name, email, number of tickets, and optional notes.
Validate registration input before submission (required fields, positive ticket count, and basic email format).
Prevent or limit registration based on event constraints (e.g., no registration once remaining_spots is zero or the event date has passed).
Submit the registration to submitEventRegistration and display server feedback including success state, messages, and any returned EventRegistration data.

Show an inline confirmation/state banner with next steps after successful registration (e.g., summary of registration and suggested actions such as adding to calendar, if provided by the system or handled elsewhere).
Provide navigation back to the events listing (events.html) via a breadcrumb or 'Back to all events' link.
Maintain and reflect registration status in the URL (using additional URL parameters) so the confirmation state can be restored on reload or when sharing the URL.
user_workflows:
1) Viewing an event detail from the events listing:
- User clicks an event card or title on events.html.
- Browser navigates to event_detail.html with a URL parameter eventId set to the clicked event's identifier.
- On page load, the eventId is read from the URL.
- The page calls getEventDetail(eventId).
- While loading, the page shows a loading state for event content.
- When getEventDetail returns successfully, the Event data is stored in page state and passed to components that display the title, date/time, location/format, price, topic, level, and remaining capacity.
- If getEventDetail fails or returns no event, an error state is shown with an option to navigate back to events.html.

2) Registering for an event from the detail page:
- User lands on event_detail.html with a valid eventId and the event details are loaded as above.
- The user clicks the primary 'Register' / 'Reserve Seat' button in the event summary section.
- The page scrolls or jumps focus to the registration form section.
- The user fills in required fields: full name, email, and number of tickets; optionally adds notes.
- On form submission, client-side validation checks for non-empty full name, valid-looking email syntax, and a positive integer for number of tickets; it may also check requested tickets against remaining_spots when that data is available.
- If validation fails, inline error messages are shown and submission is blocked until corrected.
- If validation passes, the page calls submitEventRegistration with eventId and the form fields.
- While waiting for the response, the submit action is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
- When submitEventRegistration returns, if success is true, the returned EventRegistration data and message are stored, the form section transitions to a confirmation state, and the URL is updated with parameters indicating a successful registration (for example, registrationStatus and the registration id).
- If success is false, an error message is shown and the form remains available for correction and resubmission.

3) Returning to the page with an existing registration state:
- User receives or revisits a URL to event_detail.html that includes eventId and additional parameters indicating registration status (for example, registrationStatus and/or a registration identifier) that were set after a successful submission.
- On load, the page reads eventId (for loading event details) and any additional state parameters that indicate a successful registration.
- The page calls getEventDetail using eventId to populate event information as in other workflows.
- After loading, if the URL indicates a successful prior registration, the registration-form component initializes in a 'confirmation-only' state: it shows a confirmation message instead of an empty form and avoids resubmitting the registration automatically.
- The user can still see event details and use the 'Back to all events' navigation.
- Optionally, the UI may provide a non-submitting button to reveal the registration summary again using the stored URL parameters and any available registration data.

4) Handling events that are full or in the past:
- User lands on event_detail.html with eventId and the page loads Event data via getEventDetail.
- Once Event is loaded, the page checks start_datetime and, if present, end_datetime against the current date/time in the browser.
- If the current time is later than the event’s end_datetime (or start_datetime when end_datetime is unavailable), the registration call-to-action is disabled, and the registration form is either hidden or replaced with a message indicating that registration is closed.
- If remaining_spots is available and is zero or below, the registration button and form are disabled or replaced with a 'Sold out' or equivalent closed state.
- In these closed states, the user can still review event details and navigate back to events.html, but submitEventRegistration is never called.
interactions: On page load, the event detail loader reads the eventId query parameter from the URL and triggers getEventDetail(eventId).
If eventId is missing from the URL, the page skips the API call and directly shows an error state with a link back to events.html.

If getEventDetail returns an error (e.g., network error or event not found), the page displays an error message and a non-destructive action to retry the load and a navigation link back to events.html.
Clicking the 'Back to all events' link navigates to events.html without additional parameters.
Clicking the primary 'Register' button scrolls or focuses the viewport to the registration form component on the same page.
Typing in the registration form inputs updates the local form state in real time (full name, email, number of tickets, notes).
Leaving a required field empty and attempting to submit shows inline validation errors and prevents the submitEventRegistration call.
Entering a non-numeric or non-positive value for number of tickets triggers inline validation errors when blurring the field or on submission.
If Event.remaining_spots is available, changing the number of tickets to a value greater than remaining_spots triggers a client-side validation error and prevents submission.
Submitting a valid registration form triggers submitEventRegistration(eventId, fullName, email, numberOfTickets, notes) and disables the submit button until a response is received.
