On successful submitEventRegistration (success is true), a confirmation banner or panel is shown, using the returned EventRegistration data and message, and the registration form may be hidden or collapsed.
On unsuccessful submitEventRegistration (success is false), the error message from the response is displayed, and the form remains editable so the user can correct inputs and resubmit.

After a successful registration, the page updates the current URL using query parameters (e.g., registrationStatus and the returned EventRegistration.id) so that reloading the page preserves the confirmation state without using localStorage.

If the user reloads the page with a URL that includes registration-related parameters, the registration component initializes in a non-submitting confirmation mode, avoiding duplicate calls to submitEventRegistration and instead simply reflecting that a registration has already been completed.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the eventId URL parameter. On initial load, the page reads eventId from the query string of event_detail.html. If eventId is present and non-empty, it triggers getEventDetail(eventId). The returned Event object becomes the canonical source of truth for all event-dependent UI, including title, timing, format, price, location, capacity, and remaining_spots. If eventId is missing or getEventDetail fails, the page sets an error state and does not attempt to render event-specific details or registration. Additional URL parameters (such as a registrationStatus flag and an optional registrationId derived from EventRegistration.id) may be added or updated by the page using history APIs or redirects after a successful call to submitEventRegistration. These extra URL parameters allow the page to restore the registration confirmation state on reload without using localStorage. On each load, after Event data is available, the page uses the current date/time (from the client) together with Event.start_datetime and Event.end_datetime to decide if the event is upcoming, ongoing, or past; if the event is past, the registration UI is disabled or replaced with a closed state, regardless of any registration-related parameters. Similarly, if Event.remaining_spots is available and is zero or below, the page sets a 'sold out' state that hides or disables the registration form and prevents submitEventRegistration from being called. Within the registration component, transient form state (fullName, email, numberOfTickets, notes) is maintained only in memory and is not persisted to URL parameters until after a successful submission, at which point the page may append registration-specific parameters to the URL to indicate that a registration has been completed for this event.

components.functionality: Reads the eventId from the URL query parameters on page initialization, calls getEventDetail with that identifier, and stores the returned Event data and loading/error states for use by other components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Extract eventId from URL; if present, call getEventDetail(eventId); if missing, set a 'missingId' error state.
onRetryClick: Re-call getEventDetail(eventId) if a previous attempt failed.
onGetEventDetailSuccess: Store the Event object in a shared page state and clear loading/error states.
onGetEventDetailError: Store error information in state and expose it to the event-error-state component.

components.functionality: Displays error messages when the eventId is missing, when the event cannot be loaded using getEventDetail, or when the event is not found; provides navigation back to the events listing and an optional retry action.
event_handlers: onMissingEventId: Show a message that no event was specified and render a 'Back to all events' link.
onLoadError: Show an error message describing that event details could not be loaded and display buttons to retry or go back to events.html.
onBackToEventsClick: Navigate to events.html without additional parameters.
onRetryClick: Trigger event-detail-loader.onRetryClick to attempt getEventDetail again.

components.functionality: Provides a breadcrumb or simple navigation link back to the events.html listing, available whenever the page is viewable regardless of event load state.
event_handlers: onBackLinkClick: Navigate to events.html using a standard link or client-side navigation without adding extra URL parameters.

components.functionality: Shows key event details from the loaded Event object, including title, topic, level, start and end date/time, format, location, price, currency, capacity, and remaining_spots where available. It also exposes the primary 'Register' button used to navigate to the registration form.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When the Event object is updated by event-detail-loader, re-render the summary fields.

onDetermineStatus: Based on Event.start_datetime, Event.end_datetime, and Event.remaining_spots, compute derived states such as 'upcoming', 'ongoing', 'past', or 'soldOut' and expose them to other components.

onRegisterButtonClick: If the event is neither past nor sold out, scroll or focus the viewport to the registration-form component; if the event is closed or sold out, prevent navigation and optionally show a message.

components.functionality: Interprets and displays the Event.start_datetime and Event.end_datetime as user-readable date and time information, including time zone formatting if available, and indicates whether the event is upcoming, ongoing, or has already ended.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: Recompute date and time labels whenever Event data is updated.

onClockTick: Optionally re-evaluate the 'ongoing' or 'past' state against current client time for dynamic status display, without affecting registration eligibility logic already evaluated in other components.

components.functionality: Displays capacity-related information, including Event.capacity and Event.remaining_spots when present, and marks the event as available, limited, or sold out based on these values.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When Event is loaded or updated, calculate capacity display labels (e.g., 'remaining spots') from Event.capacity and Event.remaining_spots.

onDetermineAvailability: Determine internal flags such as 'isSoldOut' or 'hasLimitedSpots' based on Event.remaining_spots and expose them to the registration-form component to enable or disable submission.

components.functionality: Collects registration information for the currently loaded event and submits it via submitEventRegistration. Handles validation, submission, error display, and transitions to a confirmation state when registration succeeds.
event_handlers: onInit: Read registration-related URL parameters (such as registrationStatus and registrationId if present) and decide whether to initialize in 'blank form' mode or 'confirmation-only' mode. Also read Event data to determine if registration is allowed based on date and remaining_spots.
onInputChange: Update local form state for fullName, email, numberOfTickets, and notes as the user types or changes values.

onValidate: Check that fullName and email are non-empty, email appears valid, numberOfTickets is a positive integer, and, if Event.remaining_spots is available, numberOfTickets does not exceed it. If the event is past or sold out, mark the form as non-submittable.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, run onValidate, and if validation passes and Event allows registration, call submitEventRegistration(event.id, fullName, email, numberOfTickets, notes). During this call, disable the submit button and indicate the submitting state.

onSubmitSuccess: When submitEventRegistration returns with success = true, store eventRegistration and message from the response, switch the UI from form mode to confirmation mode, and trigger registration-url-state-updater to update URL parameters with successful registration status and the returned EventRegistration.id.
onSubmitError: When submitEventRegistration returns with success = false, show the returned message as an error and re-enable the form inputs and submit button for correction and retry.
onEventStatusChange: When notified that Event is past or sold out, disable the form fields and submit button or hide the form, and show a non-interactive message that registration is closed.

components.functionality: Shows a confirmation message and summary of the user’s registration after a successful submission, sourced from the returned EventRegistration object and message. It replaces or appears above the registration form content.
event_handlers: onRegistrationCreated: When registration-form receives a successful submitEventRegistration response, display a confirmation state including key details from EventRegistration (such as number_of_tickets) and any message returned by the interface.

onPageLoadWithConfirmedStatus: If URL parameters indicate a previously confirmed registration, show a confirmation message even without re-calling submitEventRegistration, using whatever registration details are available from the last known EventRegistration state or minimal information from the URL.
onDismissConfirmationClick: Optionally collapse or hide the confirmation panel while keeping the page in a non-submitting state (no additional API calls).

components.functionality: Manages URL parameters related to registration status so that successful registration can be reflected in the URL and restored on page reload without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onRegistrationSuccess: When registration-form reports a successful submitEventRegistration response, use the returned EventRegistration.id and status to update the window URL query string (for example, setting registrationStatus to a value derived from EventRegistration.status and adding a registrationId parameter). This is done using browser history APIs or a redirect without reloading the page.
onPageLoad: Read registration-related URL parameters and inform the registration-form and registration-confirmation-panel components whether they should initialize in a confirmation state.

onClearRegistrationState: If the user navigates away or if the registration state needs to be reset (for instance, by a 'register another participant' action implemented in the future), remove or update the registration-related parameters from the URL while preserving eventId.

components.functionality: Displays the Event.format (in_person, online, or hybrid) and location details, adapting the displayed fields based on which values are present in the Event object.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: Render the appropriate labels and values for Event.format and Event.location whenever the Event object is updated.

components.functionality: Shows the Event.price and Event.currency from the Event object and computes derived labels such as ‘per ticket’ pricing for use alongside the registration form.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When the Event is loaded, derive and display price labels using Event.price and Event.currency.

onTicketCountChange: Optionally receive the current numberOfTickets from registration-form and display a non-binding estimated total cost based on Event.price multiplied by numberOfTickets; this is purely informational and does not affect any API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, tall hero-like event masthead at the top that mirrors the mockup: a wide, pill-shaped breadcrumb/nav bar is centered horizontally at the very top. Beneath it, within a centered content column, the event’s hero summary (title and key info) is stacked vertically and left-aligned. Below this masthead band, the registration form and any confirmation state continue in the same left-aligned column, maintaining a linear, top-to-bottom flow with no cluttered sidebars. All event meta panels (date/time, format/location, capacity, pricing) are grouped into a single vertical information stack immediately under the event title, to the right of a neon vertical bar, echoing the subheading treatment from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, tall hero-like event masthead at the top that mirrors the mockup: a wide, pill-shaped breadcrumb/nav bar is centered horizontally at the very top. Beneath it, within a centered content column, the event’s hero summary (title and key info) is stacked vertically and left-aligned. Below this masthead band, the registration form and any confirmation state continue in the same left-aligned column, maintaining a linear, top-to-bottom flow with no cluttered sidebars. All event meta panels (date/time, format/location, capacity, pricing) are grouped into a single vertical information stack immediately under the event title, to the right of a neon vertical bar, echoing the subheading treatment from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero region (breadcrumb + event summary + meta stack) occupies roughly the first 60–65% of the initial viewport height on desktop, leaving the lower part of the screen more open. The breadcrumb pill consumes about the top 10–15% of height, the event title and meta stack fill the mid 40–50%, and a generous gap separates this from the registration form below. Horizontally, all readable content sits in a central column spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width, with 15–20% dark margins on each side, while the breadcrumb pill itself stretches wider (around 80% width) to create a strong framing device. The registration form and confirmation panel use the same central column width, giving them clear prominence but still surrounded by substantial negative space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is deliberately low-density: every major component (breadcrumb, hero summary, meta stack, registration form) is isolated with generous vertical spacing (typically 32–64px between groups) and ample internal padding (around 24–32px vertical and 32–40px horizontal for card-like elements). Event meta details are not packed into tight grids; instead, they are stacked in a simple vertical column next to a slender neon accent bar, keeping each line of information easy to scan. The dark background and padding create a premium, cinematic feel with no visual noise or overly dense text blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves in a controlled vertical sequence: first to the centered breadcrumb pill at the top (establishing context), then down to the large event title in the upper-middle of the viewport (primary focal point). From there, attention slides directly downward into the vertically stacked meta details (date/time, format/location, capacity, pricing), guided by the neon vertical bar on the far-left of that stack. After the meta, a sizeable stretch of negative space leads the eye to the registration form as the primary call-to-action. If the form has been submitted, the confirmation panel occupies the same spatial slot, becoming the new focal point. Loader and error states temporarily intercept this flow by centering themselves within the same visual column when active.

layout.overall_description: The Event Detail & Registration page reads as a dark, cinematic masthead that transitions into a focused registration area. At the very top, a wide, pill-shaped breadcrumb bar floats horizontally at the center, framed by generous dark margins on the left and right. Below it, slightly lower than center in the viewport, a bold event title anchors the eye within a central content column that spans roughly two-thirds of the page width, left-aligned within that column. Immediately beneath the title, a thin neon vertical bar runs down the left edge of a compact information stack: the event’s date and time sit at the top, followed by format and location, capacity status, and pricing, each on its own line with breathing room. This upper block occupies most of the initial view without feeling crowded. Scrolling slightly—or simply looking down—reveals a spacious gap and then a large, card-like registration form aligned to the same left edge of the central column, surrounded by deep negative space above, below, and to the right. When registration completes, a confirmation panel appears in the exact footprint of the form, preserving the calm, linear flow. Throughout, the page keeps a matte black background, neon accents, and bold typography, embracing a minimal, editorial layout that feels like an immersive XR studio rather than a busy marketing page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader sits above all other content as a full-viewport overlay. It covers 100% of the width and height of the browser window with a slightly dimmed, matte-black veil (e.g., 80–90% opacity over the base background) while keeping the underlying layout faintly visible. At the exact center of the viewport—both vertically and horizontally—sits a compact neon-accented loading indicator (such as a spinner or minimal geometric loop), occupying roughly 10–15% of the viewport width. The central loader is visually aligned with the hero’s content column (so it feels like it belongs to the same axis as the event title and form) but is perfectly centered to command attention. No other components are interactive while this overlay is visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay that covers the entire viewport (top: 0, left: 0, width: 100vw, height: 100vh) with a high z-index, independent of scroll, ensuring it always stays centered on screen while loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If the event cannot be loaded, the error state replaces the hero’s main content within the central column. The breadcrumb pill may still appear at the top, but below it, instead of the event summary and form, a single compact error panel is centered in the available viewport. The panel is left-aligned within the same central column (around 60–70% width) but vertically positioned close to the middle of the viewport, with approximately equal dark space above and below. It uses a card-like surface with subtle neon accents along one edge or as a thin underline, and has generous internal padding (around 24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal). The text is minimal—an error headline and a brief explanation—so the component occupies roughly one-third of the viewport height and is surrounded by empty space, maintaining the minimalist, premium feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block-level component that occupies the main content area beneath the breadcrumb; the page height remains relatively short, with the error panel approximately centered in the viewport when first loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is rendered as a wide, pill-shaped bar that sits near the very top of the page, acting as the hero’s navigation band. It spans roughly 80% of the viewport width and is horizontally centered, leaving equal dark margins on both sides. The pill has a low profile in height—about 10–12% of the viewport height, but no taller than necessary to house the text comfortably—with rounded ends and a subtle neon edge or glow. It is positioned with a moderate top margin from the browser chrome (around 32px or two base units), creating a floating, premium feel. Inside the pill, the breadcrumb text and brand mark are aligned along the same central vertical axis as the main content column; the content within the pill is left-aligned, while the pill itself is centered. There is generous padding inside (16–24px vertical, 24–32px horizontal), and the area beneath it is left mostly empty before the event title begins, maintaining a clear hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not fixed); as the user scrolls, it moves off-screen with the rest of the content, preserving the cinematic hero effect rather than acting as a sticky nav.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event summary panel serves as the hero headline block and is positioned directly beneath the breadcrumb pill within the central content column. Its left edge aligns perfectly with the left-aligned content inside the breadcrumb, creating a continuous vertical axis. It begins after a generous vertical gap under the pill—around 64px (one of the larger spacing increments)—placing the event title in the upper-middle area of the viewport. The panel occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width and roughly 25–35% of the viewport height, depending on the title length. The event title itself is large-scale, bold typography with high contrast on the matte black background, dominating the space. Any short description or key tagline within this panel is kept in a compact block directly under the title with 16–24px of spacing, still left-aligned. A large amount of empty space remains to the right and below, keeping the hero feeling airy and premium.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, in-flow block located in the hero region; it sets the primary vertical anchor for the rest of the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date and time information is positioned immediately below the event summary panel as the first line in a vertically stacked meta-information block. Along the far-left of this meta block, a thin neon vertical bar (2–3px wide) runs from just above the date/time line down to the bottom of the last meta item, echoing the mockup’s neon bar before the subheading. The bar sits slightly inset from the central column’s left edge (around 8px), and the date/time text begins 12–16px to the right of the bar. The component itself occupies roughly 40–50% of the central column’s width (enough for a single line on desktop) and is aligned to the same left edge as the hero title. Vertical spacing above this component (from the event title) is modest—about 16–24px—to keep it visually linked to the heading while still distinct. The spacing below it to the next meta items is smaller (8–16px), suggesting they form a coherent stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow block within the meta stack; it anchors the top of the neon vertical bar and sets the indentation for all subsequent meta components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The format and location panel is placed directly beneath the date/time info, forming the second line of the meta stack. It shares the same left alignment and indentation relative to the neon vertical bar: its text begins exactly where the date/time text does, maintaining a clean column of meta text. The vertical spacing between the date/time and this panel is subtle (around 8–12px), so the user reads them as sequential details. The component occupies a similar width—around 40–60% of the central column—allowing format (e.g., ‘In-person’, ‘Virtual’) and location to sit together comfortably on a single line or two short lines. It lives within the same vertical band as the neon bar, which visually ties it to the event title above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow text block in the meta stack, immediately following the date/time component without any structural separation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The capacity status sits below the format/location panel as the next item in the meta-information stack. It is aligned to the same text column to the right of the neon vertical bar, ensuring all meta items visually line up. The spacing between the format/location and capacity status is similar to that between previous meta items (about 8–12px), forming a tight, readable group. Capacity might be presented as a concise label or pill (e.g., ‘Limited spots’, ‘Fully booked’), so the component’s width is modest—approximately 30–40% of the central column—and the pill itself can be slightly accentuated with a fine neon outline to hint at urgency without crowding the layout. There is a slightly larger gap (16–24px) beneath this component if another major detail (like pricing) follows, signifying a subtle sub-grouping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow, compact component within the meta stack; it maintains the vertical rhythm and alignment set by the date/time and format/location items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing information is the final major item in the meta stack and appears directly beneath the capacity status. It shares the same left alignment relative to the neon bar and occupies roughly 40–50% of the central column width, allowing clear, legible pricing details. A slightly larger vertical gap (around 16–24px) separates capacity status from pricing, giving pricing a bit more visual weight as the closing statement of the meta group. The component may use slightly larger or bolder text than the preceding meta lines, but it remains visually subordinate to the main event title. Below this component, a more generous vertical space (48–64px) opens up before the registration form begins, clearly distinguishing the hero/meta region from the interactive registration area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow textual block sitting at the bottom of the meta stack, serving as the final piece of key event information before transitioning to the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration form sits below the event hero and meta stack, functioning as the primary interactive focal point. It begins after a large vertical gap (approximately 64–96px) beneath the pricing info, using that negative space to separate informational content from the call-to-action. The form is aligned to the same left edge as the event title and meta text within the central column, preserving a strong vertical axis. It occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, appearing as a large, rectangular card against the matte black background. This card has generous internal padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal) and a subtle neon outline or bottom edge, reinforcing the futuristic aesthetic. Form fields are stacked vertically, each with ample spacing (16–24px) to avoid clutter; primary action buttons are placed at the bottom-right of the form content area but still within the same card boundary. There is deep negative space to the right and below the form card, making it feel like a carefully placed, premium object rather than a cramped form block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, in-flow component that extends the central column downward; its vertical position will initially appear in the lower portion of the first viewport or just below it, encouraging a subtle scroll from the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful registration, the confirmation panel occupies the exact spatial footprint of the registration form, preserving the layout’s stability. It is aligned to the same left edge and uses the same width (around 60–70% of the viewport) as the form card. Internally, it retains similar padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal), but with less dense content—a short confirmation headline, a succinct message, and any follow-up details or links. The panel may feature a more pronounced neon accent (such as a glowing border or a subtle gradient edge) to signal success without overwhelming the minimalist aesthetic. Surrounding negative space remains large on the right and below, and there is still a visual gap from the meta stack above, so the confirmation feels like a calm, self-contained step in the overall flow rather than an intrusive overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow replacement for the registration form; it either fully replaces the form component in the DOM or appears directly in the same position while the form is hidden, preserving the overall page height and scroll position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not present any UI element. Conceptually, it resides alongside the registration form and confirmation panel in the document structure, but it does not occupy visible space on the page. It reacts to registration-related state changes and updates the URL (e.g., query parameters) to reflect registration status or selected event without altering the layout. In the visual layout, the user is unaware of this component’s presence; the surrounding spacing and alignment are defined solely by the visible form and confirmation panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, zero-size component that does not affect layout flow; it operates purely as a behavioral/state layer tied to the form and confirmation panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The format and location information is already described above as part of the meta stack: it appears directly beneath the date/time info, aligned to the same vertical column of text to the right of the neon vertical bar. It forms the second line of the hero’s meta details, occupying roughly 40–60% of the central column width and staying within the upper half of the viewport on initial load. The vertical gap above and below it is small (8–12px), so it reads as a tightly connected detail in the same group as date/time and capacity, rather than as a separate section. Its placement ensures that users can understand the nature of the event (in-person vs virtual and where it happens) before deciding to scroll down to the registration form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, in-flow meta component that is vertically stacked within the hero’s meta region; it moves with the rest of the hero content as the user scrolls.

name: Careers Listing
title: Careers
description: Lists current job openings for the XR studio with filtering by department, seniority, location type, and text search. Users can quickly find XR-focused UX roles and navigate to detailed job pages.

core_features: Fetch available filter options (department, seniority, location type) from the backend using getJobFilterOptions and render them as selectable controls.
Read optional incoming URL parameters (department, seniority) and apply them as pre-selected filters on initial load.
Search and filter active job openings using searchJobs with a filters object, page, and pageSize, and render the results as a list.
Provide a text search field that passes a search term into searchJobs to match against job text fields (e.g., title/description) so users can search for XR/AR/VR-related roles.
Provide a specific filter control for XR-focused roles that restricts results via the is_xr_related field in Job when enabled.
Allow users to combine department and seniority filters (e.g., UX/UI department plus mid-level) with XR-focused filters/text search to quickly locate mid-level UX roles related to XR/AR/VR.
Display each job’s key summary information (title, department, seniority level, location/location_type) based on the Job entity and link each job to its detail page via job_detail.html?jobId={id}.
Support pagination of job results by using page and pageSize parameters in searchJobs and corresponding URL parameters in the browser.
Keep the current filter and pagination state reflected in the URL query string so that the listing is shareable and back/forward navigation-friendly.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial visit from main navigation
1. User navigates to careers.html without query parameters.
2. Page controller calls getJobFilterOptions with no parameters.
3. Page controller initializes filter controls with the options returned and no pre-selected filters.
4. Page controller constructs a default filters object (no constraints) and calls searchJobs with filters, page derived from URL (or default), and pageSize derived from URL (or default).
5. Jobs list and pagination are rendered based on the searchJobs response.
6. User can then refine results using filter controls or text search.

Workflow: Pre-filtered visit from index/about (e.g., UX/UI mid-level campaign)
1. User clicks a link on index.html or about.html that points to careers.html?department={someDepartment}&seniority={someSeniority}.
2. On load, page controller reads department and seniority from the URL query.
3. Page controller calls getJobFilterOptions to populate filter controls.
4. Once options are loaded, the controller sets the department and seniority controls to the values provided via URL (if present in the options).
5. Controller builds a filters object including department and seniority and calls searchJobs with filters and page/pageSize from URL (or defaults).
6. User sees a pre-filtered list (e.g., UX/UI mid-level roles) and can further refine with text search or XR-only toggle.

Workflow: Finding mid-level UX XR roles mentioning XR/AR/VR
1. User arrives on careers.html, possibly with pre-selected department=ux_ui_design and seniority=mid_level in the URL, or they select those options manually in the filter bar.
2. User either:
   - Enters a text search term related to XR/AR/VR in the search input, or
   - Enables the XR-focused-only toggle, which sets a filter based on Job.is_xr_related.
3. Filter bar notifies the page controller that filters changed.
4. Page controller updates the URL parameters to include department, seniority, text search (if used), XR-only flag, and resets page to 1.
5. Page controller calls searchJobs with the updated filters, page=1, and current pageSize.
6. Results list updates to show mid-level UX/UI roles that match the XR-related criteria.
7. User clicks a job title to view job_detail.html?jobId={id} in a new page.

Workflow: Adjusting filters and pagination
1. User modifies any filter control (department, seniority, location type, XR-only) or text search.
2. Filter bar emits an onFilterChange/onSearchInput event with the new filter state.
3. Page controller merges the new filter state with existing URL parameters, resets page to 1, and updates the browser URL query string (using pushState/replaceState).
4. Page controller calls searchJobs with updated filters and page=1.
5. Jobs list and pagination controls re-render with new results.
6. If user navigates through pages using pagination controls, the controller updates the page URL parameter, calls searchJobs with the same filters and new page, and updates the list.

Workflow: Navigating to job detail
1. User finds an interesting job in the jobs list.
2. User clicks on the job title or a dedicated view-details action.
3. Jobs list component navigates the browser to job_detail.html?jobId={job.id}.
4. Job detail page uses the jobId parameter to load and display full job information and application form (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Sharing or bookmarking a filtered view
1. After setting filters (e.g., department=ux_ui_design, seniority=mid_level, XR-only), user copies the current URL from the browser.
2. When this URL is opened again, the page controller reads all query parameters, applies them to the filter bar, and calls searchJobs with the corresponding filters and page.
3. The same filtered job list is reproduced without additional user input.
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization
- Trigger: careers.html loads.
- Behavior: Page controller reads URL parameters (department, seniority, locationType, q, page, pageSize, xr_only). It then calls getJobFilterOptions. Once filter options are loaded, it initializes the filter bar with selected values derived from URL where applicable and calls searchJobs with a filters object reflecting those URL parameters, plus page and pageSize.

Interaction: Department filter change
- Trigger: User selects a department option in the filter bar.
- Behavior: Filter bar emits an onFilterChange event with the new department and the rest of the current filter state. Page controller updates the department parameter in the URL (removing it if cleared), resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs with updated filters and page=1.

Interaction: Seniority filter change
- Trigger: User selects a seniority option.
- Behavior: Similar to department; URL seniority parameter is updated, page is reset to 1, and searchJobs is called with updated filters.

Interaction: Location type filter change
- Trigger: User selects a location type option (e.g., onsite/remote/hybrid from locationTypeOptions).
- Behavior: Page controller updates the locationType parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs with filters including the selected location_type.

Interaction: XR-focused toggle
- Trigger: User toggles the XR-focused-only control on or off.
- Behavior: Filter bar emits onToggleXrOnly with boolean state. Page controller updates an xr_only URL parameter (e.g., set to true or remove if false). When true, filters object includes is_xr_related=true; when false, is_xr_related is removed from filters. Page is reset to 1, and searchJobs is called with the updated filters.

Interaction: Text search input
- Trigger: User types a search string and submits (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking a search button).
- Behavior: Filter bar emits onSearchSubmit with the current search term. Page controller updates the q parameter in the URL (removing it if cleared), resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs with filters including a text search field that the backend applies to appropriate Job text fields.

Interaction: Clear all filters
- Trigger: User clicks a Clear filters action in the filter bar.
- Behavior: Filter bar emits onClearFilters. Page controller removes all filter-related parameters (department, seniority, locationType, q, xr_only) from the URL, resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs with an empty filters object (no constraints).

Interaction: Pagination click
- Trigger: User clicks next/previous or a specific page number in pagination controls.
- Behavior: Pagination component emits onPageChange with the new page number. Page controller updates the page parameter in the URL, keeps all current filters unchanged, and calls searchJobs with the same filters and the new page value.

Interaction: Page size change (if exposed)
- Trigger: User changes page size in a page-size selector.
- Behavior: Pagination component emits onPageSizeChange with the new pageSize. Page controller updates pageSize parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs with filters and new pageSize.

Interaction: Job detail navigation
- Trigger: User clicks on a job title or view-details link/button in the jobs list.
- Behavior: Jobs list component navigates to job_detail.html?jobId={job.id}. No filters are passed; job_detail.html reads jobId to load the specific job.

Interaction: Browser back/forward navigation
- Trigger: User uses browser back/forward buttons after changing filters or pagination.
- Behavior: Because all filter state and pagination are reflected in URL parameters, the page controller listens to URL changes (popstate). When URL changes, it re-reads parameters and calls searchJobs with filters/page/pageSize derived from the URL, keeping UI and results in sync with navigation history.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter and pagination state. On load and on any history navigation event, the controller parses the current URL and maps parameters to the filters object for searchJobs as follows: 1) department -> filters.department: If the department parameter is present and matches one of the values returned by getJobFilterOptions.departmentOptions, filters.department is set to that value; otherwise department is omitted from filters. 2) seniority -> filters.seniority_level: If present and recognized in getJobFilterOptions.seniorityOptions, filters.seniority_level is set; otherwise omitted. 3) locationType -> filters.location_type: If present and recognized in getJobFilterOptions.locationTypeOptions, filters.location_type is set; otherwise omitted. 4) q -> filters.text or equivalent backend-supported text field: If present and non-empty, filters includes a text search field with this value; if not present or empty, the text search field is omitted. 5) xr_only -> filters.is_xr_related: If xr_only is present and evaluates to true (based on an agreed scheme, e.g., '1', 'true'), filters.is_xr_related is set to true; if not present or false, filters.is_xr_related is omitted, meaning no constraint on XR-related jobs. 6) page -> page argument to searchJobs: If page is present and a valid positive integer, it is used; otherwise a default (first page) is used internally without being treated as job data. 7) pageSize -> pageSize argument to searchJobs: If pageSize is present and valid, it is used; otherwise an internal default is applied for pagination. After parsing, the controller calls searchJobs(filters, page, pageSize). Any interaction that changes filters or pagination updates the corresponding URL parameters (using pushState or replaceState) before calling searchJobs again. This ensures that the view can always be reconstructed from the URL alone and that pre-selected filters coming from index.html or about.html via department and seniority are applied consistently on initial load.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL state, and communication between filter bar, results summary, jobs list, and pagination. Uses getJobFilterOptions to obtain available filters and searchJobs to fetch job results whenever filters or pagination change.
event_handlers: onInit: On page load, parse URL parameters (department, seniority, locationType, q, xr_only, page, pageSize), call getJobFilterOptions, initialize filter bar with selected values, then call searchJobs(filters, page, pageSize) and distribute results to child components.

onFilterChange: Receives updated filter state from careers-filter-bar (department, seniority, locationType, xr_only, text search). Updates URL parameters accordingly (clearing those that are empty), resets page to 1, then calls searchJobs with the new filters.
onSearchSubmit: Receives a search term from careers-filter-bar, updates the q parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs with filters including the text search.
onClearFilters: Clears all filter-related URL parameters, sets filters to an empty object (no constraints), resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs.
onPageChange: Receives a new page number from careers-pagination-controls, updates the page parameter in the URL, and calls searchJobs with unchanged filters and the new page.
onPageSizeChange: Receives a new pageSize value, updates the pageSize parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and calls searchJobs with unchanged filters and new pageSize.
onPopState: Listens to browser history navigation events; on URL change, re-parses parameters and calls searchJobs with filters/page/pageSize derived from the new URL, updating all child components.

components.functionality: Provides interactive filter controls for department, seniority, location type, text search, and XR-focused-only toggle. Initializes available options from getJobFilterOptions and reflects any pre-selected state from URL parameters provided by the page controller.
event_handlers: onFiltersInitialized: Called by page controller with current filter state from URL (department, seniority, locationType, q, xr_only) after options are loaded; sets the initial selected values and search input.
onDepartmentChange: When user selects/clears a department, emits an event to the page controller with the new department plus the rest of the current filter state.
onSeniorityChange: When user selects/clears a seniority level, emits updated seniority and full filter state to the page controller.
onLocationTypeChange: When user changes location type, emits updated locationType and full filter state.
onToggleXrOnly: When user toggles the XR-focused-only control, emits updated xr_only boolean and full filter state to the page controller.
onSearchInputChange: Updates local search input state as user types, without triggering searchJobs directly (unless the desired behavior is live search).
onSearchSubmit: When user submits the search (e.g., presses enter or clicks a search button), emits onSearchSubmit with the current text search term to the page controller.
onClearFiltersClick: When user clicks a Clear filters control, emits onClearFilters to the page controller to reset all filters and search.

components.functionality: Displays a summary of the current search state, such as the number of matching jobs and which filters are active. Helps users understand the scope of their current view.
event_handlers: onRemoveFilterClick: When user clicks to remove a single active filter pill (e.g., a specific department or seniority), emits an onFilterChange event to the page controller with that filter cleared and other filters unchanged.

components.functionality: Renders the list of jobs returned by searchJobs. For each Job, shows key fields (title, department, seniority_level, location/location_type, posted_at as needed) and provides an actionable link to the job detail page.
event_handlers: onJobClick: When user clicks on a job title or row, navigates the browser to job_detail.html?jobId={Job.id}.
onJobKeyActivate: Supports keyboard navigation; when a job item is focused and activated via keyboard, performs the same navigation as onJobClick.

components.functionality: Displays and manages pagination of the job results list. Calculates total pages from searchJobs.totalCount and current pageSize, and provides controls for moving between pages.
event_handlers: onPageNumberClick: When a numbered page control is clicked, emits onPageChange with the selected page number to the page controller.
onNextPageClick: When next is clicked, emits onPageChange with currentPage + 1 if within bounds.
onPrevPageClick: When previous is clicked, emits onPageChange with currentPage - 1 if within bounds.
onPageSizeChange: When user selects a new pageSize, emits onPageSizeChange with the new value so the page controller can update URL and re-query searchJobs.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: All careers content sits inside a single, tall, dark hero-like section that fills at least one viewport height, with everything organized in a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered content column. The careers-page-controller behaves like the hero headline block at the upper-middle of the screen, left-aligned within the central column. Directly beneath it, also left-aligned to that same axis, the filter bar appears as a wide, pill-like control strip. Below the filter bar, the results summary sits as a small, quiet line of text, then the jobs list flows down in a vertical stack of cards. At the bottom of this stack, the pagination controls sit centered horizontally, separated by generous negative space from the last job card.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All careers content sits inside a single, tall, dark hero-like section that fills at least one viewport height, with everything organized in a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered content column. The careers-page-controller behaves like the hero headline block at the upper-middle of the screen, left-aligned within the central column. Directly beneath it, also left-aligned to that same axis, the filter bar appears as a wide, pill-like control strip. Below the filter bar, the results summary sits as a small, quiet line of text, then the jobs list flows down in a vertical stack of cards. At the bottom of this stack, the pagination controls sit centered horizontally, separated by generous negative space from the last job card.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column spans roughly 60–70% of the page width, leaving 15–20% dark margin on each side to echo the cinematic hero framing. Vertically, the top ~30–40% of the viewport is dominated by the careers-page-controller (title + supporting copy), with the filter bar entering the field of view in the lower part of that zone, so it feels anchored to the hero rather than to the browser edge. The results summary occupies a slim band (about 5–10% of the vertical space above the fold), and the jobs list takes the bulk of the remaining vertical area, but each job card has generous vertical spacing to keep density low. The pagination controls occupy a dedicated band of space at the bottom of the content, with at least 64–96px of breathing room above and below on larger screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is deliberately sparse and airy. Each component is visually isolated by large vertical gaps (32–64px) and sits within the same roomy central column. The page-controller title and copy have substantial breathing room above and below, echoing a hero. The filter bar is not crammed with controls; it has ample internal padding and clear separation between filter groups. The results summary is a single, quiet line. Job cards are large, with plenty of padding (24–32px) and at least 24px of space between cards. Pagination elements are spaced out and not crowded, maintaining a premium, editorial pace rather than a dense, utilitarian listing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the page-controller block (page title and supporting line), set high but not at the absolute top edge, similar to a hero headline. A neon-accented element (such as a vertical bar or subtle rule) draws the eye downward from the title into the supporting copy. Below this, the filter bar is the first interactive cluster the eye encounters, forming the natural next step: refine your search. From there, the line of results summary provides context and gently leads into the job list. The user then scans down the vertically stacked job cards, one by one, with generous whitespace marking the transitions. Finally, visual attention is drawn to the horizontally centered pagination at the bottom, which acts as the scroll cue to continue exploring more roles.

layout.overall_description: The careers listing page reads like an extended version of the hero layout: a dark, matte background with a single, wide content column floating in the center of the screen, framed by generous left and right margins. Near the top, after the global nav, the careers-page-controller establishes a bold, XR-flavored headline and a short, neon-accented supporting line, all left-aligned within the central column. This upper block sits with ample vertical breathing room so the typography feels large and cinematic. Just below, still clearly within the hero field, the careers-filter-bar appears as a sleek, pill-shaped strip spanning most of the central column width, housing filters with a clean, horizontal structure. Slightly below and aligned to the same left axis, a compact careers-results-summary line communicates how many roles match the current filters. From there, the page opens into a tall, scrolling vertical gallery of job cards (careers-jobs-list), each card left-aligned to the same visual column but surrounded by generous negative space. The bottom of this column resolves into a centered band of pagination controls (careers-pagination-controls), subtly highlighted with the neon accent, sitting in a mostly empty lower zone so the user intuitively understands they can move to additional pages of roles. The overall effect is a calm, futuristic, editorial listing that preserves the original hero’s spacious, high-end feel while supporting practical navigation and filtering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The careers-page-controller occupies the upper-central portion of the page, acting as a hero header for the listing. It sits below the global nav, with a generous vertical gap of roughly 64–96px from the nav’s bottom edge to its own top. The component is constrained to the main content column, which spans about 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered horizontally, leaving equal dark margins (about 15–20% each side). Within this column, all text is left-aligned. The primary careers heading (e.g., “Careers” or “Join Our XR Collective”) is placed slightly above the vertical center of the initial viewport, occupying approximately 1–2 lines with large, bold type and about 32–48px of space above and below the headline. Directly beneath the headline, aligned to the same left edge, a thin neon vertical bar (around 2–3px wide and 40–64px tall) is positioned at the far left of a horizontal row, with a single line or short paragraph of supporting copy to its right. The bar’s top aligns roughly with the first baseline of the supporting text, echoing the hero’s subheading style. The supporting copy has ample line spacing and about 16–24px of space above and 32–48px below, creating a clear visual separation before the filter bar begins. Overall, the careers-page-controller feels like a self-contained hero block that claims the top 30–40% of the viewport height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrolling document; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down, but because of its vertical size and spacing, it will typically be the only major block fully visible above the fold on large screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The careers-filter-bar is positioned directly beneath the careers-page-controller, maintaining roughly 32–48px of vertical space between the bottom of the supporting copy and the top of the filter bar. It sits within the same central content column, but its visual form is a wide, pill-shaped container that spans about 80–90% of that column’s width, centered within the column so there is a slender margin of background on either side. The pill has generous internal padding: approximately 16–24px vertically and 24–32px horizontally, giving each filter control ample breathing space. Filter groups (department, seniority, location type, search) are arranged horizontally from left to right, each taking up a logical segment of the pill, with at least 16–24px separation between groups. On larger screens, the textual filters (dropdowns or chips) align to the left, while the search field sits toward the right side of the pill, but all remain within the same horizontal band. The top edge of the filter bar sits roughly at or just below the vertical midpoint of the initial viewport, so as users arrive, the filter bar feels naturally tied to the hero header above without crowding it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky element: once the user scrolls past its initial position, the filter bar pins to the top of the viewport (just below the global nav) while preserving its full width within the central column. When sticky, it retains a bit of top margin (e.g., 8–16px) beneath the nav to maintain the airy feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The careers-results-summary appears immediately below the filter bar, separated by a modest but clear vertical gap of about 16–24px to keep the density low while visually linking the two. It is left-aligned to the same main text column as the hero copy, beginning at the left edge of the filter bar’s inner content rather than the pill’s rounded outline, so the text aligns neatly with the filter labels above. The summary itself is a single line of text (for example, “12 roles matching your filters”), occupying only a small portion of the column width on the left, leaving the right side mostly empty to emphasize spaciousness. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space beneath the summary before the first job card begins, allowing it to serve as a quiet, transitional label into the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling naturally with the content, always visually tethered to the filter bar above and the job list below through consistent margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The careers-jobs-list occupies the main body of the page below the results summary, forming a tall, vertically scrolling column of job cards. The entire list is constrained to the same central content column (60–70% page width), with cards left-aligned to the primary text axis. Each job card spans roughly 100% of the width of this column (minus internal padding), so cards feel substantial and editorial rather than compact. There is generous vertical spacing between cards: about 24–32px of margin separating each card from the next. Each card itself has ample internal padding (about 24px vertical and 24–32px horizontal), with content arranged in a clean left-to-right structure: job title and key metadata on the left, and any small call-to-action or icon on the far right, both aligned along the same baseline. The list continues down the page, with consistent spacing all the way to the final card, leaving at least 48–64px of empty space below the last card before the pagination controls to keep the lower area feeling open rather than cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the main scrollable content. On very long lists, the list scrolls beneath the sticky filter bar at the top, so job cards appear to slide under a persistent control layer while remaining fully within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The careers-pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the jobs list section, separated from the last job card by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to create a distinct, calm footer to the listing. The pagination container is horizontally centered within the central content column but does not necessarily span its full width; instead, the controls themselves (page numbers, previous/next arrows) sit in a compact horizontal row that occupies roughly 30–40% of the column width in the center, leaving symmetrical empty space to the left and right. Elements within the pagination row are spaced out with at least 16–24px between page numbers and controls, echoing the airy feel of the rest of the page. The entire pagination block sits within a band of negative space so that the area below it (down to the next section or page footer) has another 64–96px of empty height, creating a visual sense that you’ve reached the natural end of the current result set.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to the list; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the bottom of the jobs list and does not fix to the viewport. It acts as a natural scroll terminus for each page of results.

name: Job Detail & Application
title: Job Detail & Application
description: Displays a specific job’s details based on jobId and allows candidates to submit a text-based application via an on-page form.

core_features: Load job details for the given jobId from the URL using getJobDetail and render title, department, seniority, description, responsibilities, requirements, experience range, and location/remote policy.
Handle invalid or missing jobId by showing an error state and disabling the application form, with navigation back to the careers listing.
Show job status based on the job record (e.g., whether the role is active) and allow or prevent applications accordingly.
Provide an in-page primary call-to-action button (e.g., 'Apply Now') that focuses or scrolls to the application form.
Render an application form bound to the current jobId, capturing full name, email, headline/subject, portfolio URL, cover message, current location, LinkedIn URL, and an optional CV-uploaded flag.
Validate application input on the client side (e.g., required fields, email format) before sending data to submitJobApplication.
Submit the application using submitJobApplication with values from the form and the current jobId.
Display submission results (success or failure) using the response from submitJobApplication, including the returned message.
Outline basic next steps or timeline expectations for candidates after a successful submission (using generic instructional text, not hardcoded job data).
Provide navigation back to the main careers page via a breadcrumb or 'Back to all roles' link.
user_workflows: View job details and decide to apply: 1) User opens job_detail.html with a jobId URL parameter from careers.html or index.html. 2) The page reads jobId from the URL. 3) The page calls getJobDetail with that jobId. 4) When the response returns, the Job data populates the job header and description components. 5) If the job is active, the Apply button and application form are enabled; otherwise, a notice is shown that the role is not accepting applications.

Handle missing or invalid jobId: 1) User opens job_detail.html without a jobId or with a jobId that returns no job from getJobDetail. 2) The page shows an error state indicating the job cannot be found. 3) The application form and Apply button are disabled or hidden. 4) A navigation link back to careers.html is prominently available.

Start application from job details: 1) User reviews the job information. 2) User clicks the 'Apply Now' button in the job header or a fixed CTA. 3) The page scrolls or jumps to the application form section and focuses the first input field. 4) Any previous submission result message is cleared when starting a new attempt.

Complete and submit application: 1) User fills in full name and email (required), plus optional headline, portfolio URL, cover message, current location, LinkedIn URL, and CV-uploaded indicator. 2) On clicking the form submit button, client-side validation runs. 3) If validation fails, the form highlights the problematic fields and blocks the submission. 4) If validation passes, the form sends submitJobApplication with jobId from the URL and the current form values, including cvUploaded. 5) The submit button is disabled and a loading state is shown while waiting for the response.

Interpret submission response: 1) When submitJobApplication returns, the page checks the success flag. 2) If success is true, the page shows a confirmation message, optionally referencing the response.message, and outlines general next steps (such as expected response timelines) and may clear or lock the form to prevent duplicate submissions. 3) If success is false, the page shows an error message using response.message and re-enables the submit button so the user can correct issues and retry.

Navigate back to all roles: 1) From the job details or after submission, the user can click the breadcrumb or 'Back to all roles' link. 2) The page navigates to careers.html without additional parameters (or with any default parameters defined elsewhere).
interactions: Initial data load: On page load or when the jobId URL parameter changes, the page triggers getJobDetail with the current jobId. The job detail view updates when the response arrives.
Breadcrumb/back link click: Clicking the breadcrumb or 'Back to all roles' link navigates to careers.html, discarding any application form state on this page.
Apply button click: Clicking the Apply button scrolls or jumps to the application form component and sets focus to the first input if possible.

Form field input: As the user types or edits form fields (full name, email, headline, portfolio URL, cover message, current location, LinkedIn URL, CV-uploaded flag), the form component updates its internal state and clears any field-level error messages once the values become valid.

Form submit: Clicking the submit button triggers validation; if valid, it calls submitJobApplication using jobId from the URL and current form data. The submit button enters a loading/disabled state until the request resolves.

Submission success handling: On a successful response, the form sets an internal application status to 'submitted', stores the returned JobApplication object for potential display (e.g., timestamp, status), shows the confirmation message, and may disable further edits or hide the form.
Submission error handling: On an unsuccessful response, the form sets an error state, displays the response message, and re-enables submission so the user can retry.
Job inactive handling: If the Job entity indicates the job is inactive, the page shows an informational message and hides or disables the application form and Apply button.
Job not found handling: If getJobDetail returns no job for the supplied jobId, the page shows a not-found message and suggests returning to careers.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the jobId URL parameter. On each load of job_detail.html, the page reads jobId from the URL query string (e.g., job_detail.html?jobId=...). If jobId is present, the page calls getJobDetail(jobId) to retrieve a Job. If jobId is missing, the page enters a 'missing-jobId' state: no API call is made, job detail content is not shown, and the application form is disabled. If getJobDetail returns a job, the page enters a 'job-loaded' state and binds all job-dependent components to that Job. If the Job has is_active set to false, the page enters a 'job-inactive' sub-state where the application form and Apply button are disabled or hidden. On submit, the form uses the same jobId from the URL when calling submitJobApplication, ensuring the application is tied to the correct Job. Application status (e.g., idle, submitting, submitted, error) is managed as in-memory page state, not in the URL or localStorage. If desired, after a successful submission the page may update the URL to include an additional parameter (e.g., job_detail.html?jobId=...&submitted=true) purely to control whether the success message should be pre-shown on reload; this should be read on load and used to set an initial 'submitted' view state, but the canonical job context always comes from jobId. All navigation back to the careers listing uses a simple link to careers.html with no reliance on localStorage.

components.functionality: Reads the jobId from the URL query string and retrieves the corresponding Job by calling getJobDetail. Manages loading, success, and error states and passes the Job data to downstream components.
event_handlers: onPageInit: Read jobId from URL and, if present, invoke getJobDetail(jobId).
onUrlParamChange(jobId): Re-invoke getJobDetail with the new jobId when the URL changes.
onGetJobDetailSuccess(response): Store response.job as the current Job and signal child components to render job details.
onGetJobDetailError(error): Enter an error state indicating that job details could not be loaded and signal UI to render a not-found or error message.

components.functionality: Displays a message when jobId is missing or getJobDetail returns no Job, and prompts the user to return to the careers listing.
event_handlers: onJobDataResolved(job): If job is null or undefined, show a not-found message and hide job-dependent sections.

components.functionality: Shows key information about the job, such as title, department, seniority level, location type, and other high-level metadata. Provides a primary Apply button when the job is active.
event_handlers: onJobLoaded(job): Populate the header fields using the Job properties.
onClickApplyNow: Trigger scroll or focus to the job-application-form component, and optionally set the application view state to 'active'.

components.functionality: Renders structured job meta-information such as experience_min_years and experience_max_years as a range, location, location_type, and posted_at if available. Indicates whether the role is XR-related using is_xr_related.
event_handlers: onJobLoaded(job): Compute and display derived meta text based on Job fields (e.g., formatting experience range and location).

components.functionality: Displays the main textual content of the job, including the job description, responsibilities, and requirements. Sections are only rendered if the corresponding fields exist in the Job record.
event_handlers: onJobLoaded(job): Render description, responsibilities, and requirements sections using the Job fields.
onJobInactive(job): Optionally overlay a notice that the role is no longer accepting applications while still allowing the description to be viewed.

components.functionality: Indicates whether the job is currently open for applications based on Job.is_active and conditionally enables or disables the application form and Apply button.
event_handlers: onJobLoaded(job): If job.is_active is true, set status to 'open' and allow applications; if false, set status to 'closed' and disable the Apply button and job-application-form.
onStatusChange: Notify other components (such as job-application-form) when the job becomes inactive so they can update their UI.

components.functionality: Provides a breadcrumb or simple back link allowing the user to return to the careers listing page.
event_handlers: onJobLoaded(job): Optionally display the current job title as the final breadcrumb segment without affecting navigation behavior.

components.functionality: Captures candidate data for the current job and submits it using submitJobApplication. Handles validation, loading states, and redirects success or error states to the status component.
event_handlers: onJobLoaded(job): If job.is_active is true, enable the form and internally store job.id for use in submissions; if false or job is null, disable the form.

onFieldChange(fieldName, value): Update local form state for fullName, email, headline, portfolioUrl, coverMessage, currentLocation, linkedinUrl, cvUploaded and clear any validation error for this field.

onSubmit: Validate required fields (at minimum fullName and email, and optionally any others defined as required in UX), ensure jobId is available, then call submitJobApplication(jobId, fullName, email, headline, portfolioUrl, coverMessage, currentLocation, linkedinUrl, cvUploaded). Set a 'submitting' state while awaiting the response.

onSubmitJobApplicationSuccess(response): If response.success is true, store response.jobApplication as the current JobApplication, set internal status to 'submitted', clear or lock the form inputs per UX rules, and notify job-application-status-display to show a confirmation using response.message.

onSubmitJobApplicationError(responseOrError): If response.success is false or the request fails, set an error state and notify job-application-status-display to show the error message, then clear the 'submitting' state to re-enable the submit button.
onJobInactive(job): Clear or disable the form and prevent submission if the job is no longer active.

components.functionality: Shows real-time feedback for the application process, including validation errors, submission in-progress state, success confirmations, and error messages.
event_handlers: onSubmitting: Display a loading or 'sending' status while submitJobApplication is in progress.
onValidationError(errors): Display field-level and/or form-level validation error messages received from job-application-form before any API call.

onSubmitJobApplicationSuccess(response): Display a success confirmation using response.message and indicate that the application has been received. Optionally reference JobApplication.status and created_at if present in response.jobApplication.
onSubmitJobApplicationError(responseOrError): Display a clear error message using any message from the API response or a generic fallback if unavailable.
onStartNewApplication: Clear prior messages when the user begins editing fields after a successful or failed submission, allowing a clean retry state.

components.functionality: Provides generic textual guidance on what happens after an application is submitted (e.g., approximate review timelines or how candidates will be contacted). This content is static UX copy and does not depend on specific job data.
event_handlers: onJobApplicationSubmitted(jobApplication): Show or highlight this block after a successful submission to ensure the candidate sees next-step information.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, vertically stacked content column centered within a wide, dark canvas, mirroring the hero-style mockup. At the very top, a horizontally centered breadcrumb pill acts as the page-level navigation anchor. Below it, the job header area behaves like a hero: large, left-aligned job title with supporting meta and status. Directly beneath that, the job description continues down the same invisible column. The application form follows as a distinct but aligned block in the same column, with application status and next-steps content directly adjacent/stacked, preserving a clear top-to-bottom reading flow with minimal lateral movement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, vertically stacked content column centered within a wide, dark canvas, mirroring the hero-style mockup. At the very top, a horizontally centered breadcrumb pill acts as the page-level navigation anchor. Below it, the job header area behaves like a hero: large, left-aligned job title with supporting meta and status. Directly beneath that, the job description continues down the same invisible column. The application form follows as a distinct but aligned block in the same column, with application status and next-steps content directly adjacent/stacked, preserving a clear top-to-bottom reading flow with minimal lateral movement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout uses a central content container that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving 15–20% margins on the left and right. The top hero band (breadcrumb + job header + status/meta) occupies about the upper 55–65% of the initial viewport height, leaving a generous lower band of negative space before the user scrolls into the detailed description and application sections. Vertical spacing between major blocks (hero, description, application) follows a 64–96px rhythm, with tighter 16–32px spacing between related elements (title, meta, status) to keep them visually grouped. The application section is visually substantial but still respects the airy, cinematic feel with ample padding inside its panel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page remains low-density and premium. Each component (breadcrumb, hero header, description, application) is isolated with generous negative space around it. Text is arranged in short, high-impact groups: a bold job title, a compact meta line, then a clearly separated description block, and a restrained, minimalist form. Decorative or structural elements like a neon vertical accent line are used to anchor key content (e.g., description intro, next-steps info) rather than fill space, ensuring the layout feels intentional and non-cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the breadcrumb pill at the top center, grounding the user in the site structure. It then drops into the hero-style job header: the bold job title is the primary focal point, followed immediately by the job status and meta summary. From there, the vertical flow continues down into the job description, guided by a left-edge neon vertical accent that echoes the mockup. After absorbing the role details, attention naturally moves to the application form, which sits as a strong, framed block in the same column. Within the form area, any application status feedback and next steps appear immediately around or below the form, completing the flow from understanding the role to taking action, without breaking the linear scroll experience.

layout.overall_description: The Job Detail & Application page feels like a focused, editorial hero extended into a single, cinematic scroll. Against a matte black backdrop, a wide, pill-shaped breadcrumb bar sits at the top center, floating with generous breathing room beneath the browser chrome. Below, the content resolves into a tall hero section where a large, bold job title anchors the left side of a central content column, framed by wide dark margins on both sides. Just below and adjacent to the title, a compact row of meta information and a glowing status pill provide quick context without overpowering the headline. As the user scrolls, the hero seamlessly gives way to a deeply spaced, left-aligned job description, introduced by a neon vertical accent along the left margin of the text column. Further down, the application form emerges as a distinct, subtly elevated panel aligned to the same column, with ample inner padding and minimal but clear field labels, keeping the aesthetics quiet and technical. Directly above or below the form, the application status feedback and next-step guidance appear in their own spatially separated strips, matching the low-density, XR-studio feel. The entire layout reads as one continuous vertical story: from orientation, to understanding the role, to applying, all within a single, disciplined column of content against a wide, immersive dark field.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-viewport overlay centered over the main content area. It sits above the matte black background and temporarily obscures the breadcrumb, hero, description, and form when data is being fetched. The loader's visual focus is a minimal, futuristic loading indicator placed dead-center both horizontally and vertically. The dark overlay maintains the same wide side margins implied by the content container but extends edge-to-edge to dim the entire page, reinforcing the cinematic feel while keeping the user anchored in a single immersive space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay centered in the viewport, covering the entire scrollable area while active, then fully removed from the layout (not occupying space) once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When shown, this message occupies the hero zone where the job header would normally be. The message is left-aligned within the central content column, starting roughly one-third down from the top of the viewport, mirroring the position of the typical job title. A large, bold headline (e.g., 'Position no longer available') occupies approximately 50–60% of the central column width, with a single paragraph of explanation below, spaced by about 24px. The surrounding area remains largely empty, with wide side margins and extra vertical space above and below, preserving the low-density, premium feel even in an error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; it replaces the job header, meta, description, and application sections, leaving the breadcrumb above intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job header forms the core of the hero area. It is positioned below the breadcrumb pill with a generous vertical gap of about 64px, starting slightly above the vertical center of the initial viewport. The job title text is left-aligned within the central content container, which spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is horizontally centered, leaving expansive dark margins on both sides. The title itself occupies about half to two-thirds of the container width, set in large, bold type that dominates the midsection of the screen. There is ample negative space above the title and to its right, echoing the mockup's editorial hero style and ensuring the title is the primary focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the top hero section; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job meta summary sits directly beneath the job header, aligned to the same left edge of the content column. It is separated from the title by about 24–32px of vertical space, keeping it clearly connected yet visually subordinate to the headline.
