The meta content (such as location, team, employment type) is arranged in a single horizontal row or two closely stacked lines, occupying roughly the same maximum width as the title but with much smaller font size. The row hugs the left side of the column and leaves ample empty space to the right, maintaining the clean, low-density aesthetic. Together with the status indicator, this area occupies a relatively shallow vertical band under the title, preserving most of the hero's negative space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero content stack, moving with the scroll right under the job header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job description begins just below the hero band, separated from the meta summary by a generous 64–80px vertical gap to signal transition from hero to body content. It continues the same central column alignment, with text left-aligned and line lengths contained to roughly 60–65 characters for readability. At the very left edge of this text column, a thin neon vertical line runs alongside the first block of description content or section heading, extending down for one or two paragraphs' height, visually echoing the mockup’s neon accent that precedes body copy. Section headings (Responsibilities, Requirements, etc.) are stacked vertically with 32–48px of spacing between them, creating clearly separated blocks of copy. The overall description area may span multiple viewport heights but always stays within the same centered container with wide lateral margins and consistent vertical rhythm, keeping the page feeling like a continuous editorial scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that follows the hero; it scrolls naturally, occupying a substantial vertical stretch below the header/meta region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The job status indicator appears in close proximity to the job meta summary, visually tied to the title while remaining compact. It is placed to the right of the first meta row or directly under it, aligned to the same left edge but offset with a small vertical or horizontal gap of about 16px. The indicator itself is a pill-shaped or tag-like element with a subtle neon glow, occupying a small fraction of the width (roughly 10–15% of the content column) and a shallow height, so it does not compete with the title. Its position just below the title line of sight ensures that, after reading the role name, the user can immediately see whether the role is open, closing, or filled, while the surrounding hero space remains largely uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and tightly anchored near the job header and meta summary, moving with them in the hero section as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is rendered as a wide, pill-shaped bar centered horizontally near the very top of the page content, around 32–48px below the browser chrome or global site nav. It spans approximately 60–70% of the viewport width, matching the central content container width or slightly exceeding it to feel like a framing device for the page. Inside the pill, the breadcrumb items are left-aligned, starting near the left edge, while the pill itself is visually centered, leaving equal negative space on both sides. Vertical padding inside the pill is generous (around 16–20px) to give it a substantial, premium feel while keeping the overall height to roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height. There is ample empty space between the breadcrumb pill and the job header below, reinforcing the hero-like hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the scrollable content; it scrolls away with the page (not sticky), maintaining the clean, uninterrupted hero experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application form occupies a distinct section below the job description, aligned exactly with the same central content column. It begins after a generous vertical gap of about 80–96px from the last description section, clearly marking the shift from reading to action. The form is housed in a subtle, full-width (relative to the content container) panel that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered horizontally, with soft rounded corners and generous inner padding of 32–40px on all sides, giving it a premium, card-like presence. Form fields are arranged in a simple vertical stack, left-aligned with labels and inputs forming a clean, linear column, each field separated by about 16–24px. There is ample negative space at the top of the panel for a concise application heading and intro, and extra space at the bottom beneath the primary submit button, so the form feels calm and deliberate rather than dense. The dark background around the panel remains largely empty, preserving the immersive, futuristic atmosphere.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored in the main scroll, following the description; its panel visually grounds the lower portion of the page without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application status display is positioned in close relation to the application form, typically within the same panel or immediately above it. When inside the form panel, it appears as a horizontally spanning strip at the top, just below any form title, separated by about 16px of vertical space. It occupies the full internal width of the form container but remains shallow in height, akin to a slim banner or notification bar. Text is left-aligned, matching the form's alignment, and there is ample padding (12–16px) around the message to maintain the comfortable spacing of the overall layout. If used to confirm successful submission, it can also appear just above the form panel, still aligned to the content column, with 24–32px of separation, so users immediately see feedback as they scroll back to the form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to the form; it appears and disappears without changing broader page structure, simply expanding or collapsing the space above/below the form fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The next-steps information sits directly below the application form, aligned to the same central column. It begins after a moderate vertical gap of about 40–56px from the bottom of the form panel, clearly associated but not crowded. This block is narrower in height and can either share the same width as the content container or be slightly narrower (about 50–60% of viewport width) to feel like a focused note. On the left edge of this block, a thin neon vertical line runs down beside the text, visually echoing the accent used in the job description and reinforcing the brand’s XR aesthetic. The copy itself is set in one or two short paragraphs, left-aligned, with ample space around it and no additional clutter, effectively serving as a calm, reassuring closing note at the end of the application journey.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the end of the main content flow; it scrolls naturally and does not stick, acting as a soft terminus to the page’s vertical narrative.

name: Contact & Partnerships
title: Contact & Partnerships
description: Page for submitting contact and partnership inquiries, including project quotes and agency/white-label collaboration requests.

core_features: Initialize the page by fetching dynamic options for inquiry types, areas of interest, and regions using getContactInquiryOptions.
Display a configurable 'Inquiry Type' selector whose options are populated from getContactInquiryOptions and optionally pre-selected based on the inquiryType URL parameter.
Provide an 'Areas of Interest / Services' multi-select, populated from getContactInquiryOptions, allowing users to indicate multiple service interests.
Provide geographic fields where users select one or more regions from getContactInquiryOptions and optionally add a free-text description for more specific geographic details.
Provide a budget field where users can specify a minimum budget value relevant to their inquiry.
Capture contact details including contact name, company, email, optional phone, and a free-text message describing the inquiry or partnership needs.
Validate required fields (including inquiry type, contact name, and email) and basic formats (such as email and numeric budget) before submission.
Submit the inquiry via submitContactInquiry, mapping form fields to the expected parameters and handling loading, success, and error states.
Display a confirmation state after successful submission using the response from submitContactInquiry, including the response message that may contain expected response time information.
After successful submission, provide contextual navigation links that lead to relevant service_category.html pages using URL parameters, depending on the selected inquiry type.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Direct general inquiry
1. User navigates to contact.html without URL parameters.
2. The page controller calls getContactInquiryOptions to retrieve inquiryTypeOptions, areasOfInterestOptions, and regionOptions.
3. Once options load, the inquiry type selector is populated. No initial selection is applied because there is no inquiryType URL parameter.
4. The user selects an inquiry type from the dynamically loaded options.
5. The user selects one or more areas of interest from the multi-select, based on the loaded areasOfInterestOptions.
6. The user selects one or more regions from the region options and optionally enters a free-text regions description.
7. The user optionally enters a budget minimum value.
8. The user fills out contact name, company (optional), email, phone (optional), and a message describing their inquiry.
9. The user submits the form.
10. The form component validates required fields and formats; if validation passes, it calls submitContactInquiry with the current form values.
11. While waiting for the response, the form shows a submitting state (e.g., disabling inputs and submit button at the functional level).
12. If submitContactInquiry returns success=true, the page renders a confirmation component using response.contactInquiry and response.message, and hides or disables the main form.
13. For a general inquiry type, the confirmation component may show a generic post-submission state without additional navigation links (logic does not hardcode text, it only uses the response data plus generic actions).

Workflow 2: Project quote inquiry with guided follow-up navigation
1. User clicks a quote-related call to action on another page (such as a services page), which links to contact.html with a URL parameter inquiryType set to an inquiry type representing a project quote.
2. On contact.html load, the page controller calls getContactInquiryOptions.
3. After options load, the controller checks whether the inquiryType URL parameter matches one of the returned inquiryTypeOptions. If it matches, the inquiry type selector is automatically set to that value; otherwise, no pre-selection is applied.
4. The user reviews the pre-selected inquiry type and, if desired, can change it, which also updates the URL inquiryType parameter.
5. The user selects relevant areas of interest and regions, optionally specifying an approximate budget minimum.
6. The user completes contact fields and describes the project requirements in the message field.
7. The user submits the form, triggering validation and a call to submitContactInquiry.
8. On success, the confirmation component is displayed with response.message and an internal success state indicating a project quote inquiry type.
9. Because the inquiry type corresponds to a quote request, the confirmation component displays a contextual link labelled functionally (e.g., a button or link) that, when clicked, navigates to service_category.html with the URL parameter serviceId set to "training_simulation".
10. The user can click this link to explore related service categories. The navigation uses standard browser navigation to service_category.html?serviceId=training_simulation.

Workflow 3: Agency / partnership inquiry with white-label focus and follow-up navigation
1. User arrives on contact.html from an agency- or partnership-focused call to action, with a URL parameter inquiryType set to a value that represents an agency partner or partnership inquiry.
2. On load, the page controller calls getContactInquiryOptions to populate the form option sets.
3. After loading, the controller validates the inquiryType from the URL against inquiryTypeOptions. If valid, the inquiry type selector is pre-selected accordingly.
4. The user confirms that the inquiry type matches their intent (for example, an agency partner inquiry) and adjusts if needed, which updates the URL parameter.
5. The user selects relevant areas of interest from the options, including any items related to white-label or XR production if they exist in the loaded data, and marks applicable regions and geographic details.
6. The user fills out contact name, company, email, optional phone, and uses the message field to describe partnership or white-label needs.
7. The user optionally includes a budget minimum aligned with expected project sizes.
8. On submit, the form validates inputs and calls submitContactInquiry with all relevant fields.
9. On success, the confirmation component displays the server-provided message and sets an internal state indicating that the inquiry type is partnership-related.
10. Because the inquiry type matches a partnership or agency type, the confirmation component shows a contextual navigation option that links to service_category.html with serviceId set to "marketing_brand".
11. When the user clicks this link, the page navigates to service_category.html?serviceId=marketing_brand to explore marketing and brand-related service categories.
interactions: On page load, the page controller automatically triggers getContactInquiryOptions and maintains loading and error states that gate rendering of the form.

When the page has an inquiryType URL parameter, the controller validates it against inquiryTypeOptions after they are fetched; if valid, it pre-selects the value in the inquiry type selector; if invalid, it leaves the selector unselected.

When the user changes the inquiry type in the selector, an onChange handler updates the internal form state and synchronizes the inquiryType URL parameter using the browser history API (e.g., updating the query string without a full reload).

When the user interacts with the areas of interest multi-select, an onChange handler updates the form's areasOfInterest array while keeping it in sync with the available options from getContactInquiryOptions.
When the user selects or deselects regions, an onChange handler updates the form's regions array and validates that selected values are contained within the regionOptions list.
When the user types in the free-text regions description, the onInput handler updates the corresponding regionsDescription field in the form state.
When the user inputs a budget minimum, the onInput handler enforces numeric-only constraints and clears or flags the input if it cannot be parsed as a number.
When the user edits contact name, company, email, phone, and message, onInput handlers update those fields in the form state and clear any prior validation errors associated with them.

On form submit, an onSubmit handler: (1) prevents default browser submission; (2) runs validation for required fields and simple formats; (3) if valid, sets a submitting state and calls submitContactInquiry with the current form values; (4) if invalid, prevents the API call and displays error messages.

When submitContactInquiry resolves successfully with success=true, an onSubmitSuccess handler stores response.contactInquiry and response.message, transitions the page into a submitted state, and hides or disables the form component while rendering a confirmation component.

If submitContactInquiry returns success=false or the call fails technically, an onSubmitError handler surfaces a generic error message and allows the user to retry submission without losing their form data.

Within the confirmation component, if the stored inquiry type in the submitted contactInquiry corresponds to a project quote, an onClick handler on a follow-up link navigates the user to service_category.html with serviceId set to "training_simulation".

Within the confirmation component, if the stored inquiry type corresponds to a partnership or agency partner inquiry, an onClick handler on a follow-up link navigates the user to service_category.html with serviceId set to "marketing_brand".
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters and in-memory state (not localStorage) to manage behavior. On initialization, the page reads the inquiryType parameter from the query string (if present). It then invokes getContactInquiryOptions to load inquiryTypeOptions, areasOfInterestOptions, and regionOptions. After options are loaded, the controller checks whether the inquiryType parameter value matches any item in inquiryTypeOptions. If it does, the form's inquiryType field is set to that value; if not, the inquiryType field remains unset, and the URL parameter is effectively ignored. When the user changes the inquiry type via the selector, the page updates its internal form state and also updates the inquiryType query parameter in the URL (adding, replacing, or removing it depending on the current selection) using the browser history API, ensuring back/forward navigation keeps inquiry type in sync. The form maintains separate state slices for: (a) options (loaded via getContactInquiryOptions), with loading and error flags; (b) current form values aligned with ContactInquiry fields; (c) validation errors; and (d) submission status (idle, submitting, success, error). On successful submission, the response from submitContactInquiry (contactInquiry and message) is stored in state, and a 'submitted' flag is set. When submitted is true, the form is hidden or disabled, and the confirmation component is rendered. The confirmation component derives whether to show follow-up navigation to specific service_category.html URLs by examining the inquiry_type value in the stored contactInquiry object. All dynamic values (inquiry options, areas of interest, regions, and confirmation messages) come from getContactInquiryOptions or submitContactInquiry responses; no static option lists or messages are hardcoded.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, option loading, URL parameter handling, and high-level state (loading, error, submitted). It calls getContactInquiryOptions on load, validates any inquiryType query parameter, and passes derived state to child components such as the inquiry form and confirmation panel.

Encapsulates the call to getContactInquiryOptions and exposes the resulting inquiryTypeOptions, areasOfInterestOptions, and regionOptions, along with loading and error indicators, to the rest of the page.

Renders a single-select input for choosing the inquiry type. It uses inquiryTypeOptions from getContactInquiryOptions as its option list, applies any preselected value derived from the URL, and updates both form state and the URL parameter when changed.

Renders a multi-select control allowing users to choose one or more service areas. It uses areasOfInterestOptions from getContactInquiryOptions, and updates the ContactInquiry.areas_of_interest array in form state.

Groups region-related inputs: a multi-select of available regions and a free-text input for additional geographic description. It synchronizes selections with ContactInquiry.regions and ContactInquiry.regions_description.
Provides a numeric input for the minimum budget. It enforces numeric validation at the field level and updates ContactInquiry.budget_minimum in form state.
Collects core contact information required to follow up on the inquiry: contact name, company, email, phone, and message. It manages field-level validations such as required and email format.

Wraps all form fields (inquiry type, areas of interest, regions, budget, contact details) into a single submit operation. It orchestrates client-side validation, calls submitContactInquiry when valid, and communicates submission state back to the page controller.

Executes submitContactInquiry with the current form state when requested by the form container. It handles mapping between internal state field names and API parameters, and returns the response to the caller.

Displays submission-related status to the user: idle, submitting, success, or error. It responds to state changes from the submission handler and form container to show or hide appropriate functional messages.

Rendered only after a successful submitContactInquiry call. It shows confirmation details based on contactInquiry and uses the response.message for user-facing text. It decides which contextual follow-up navigation options to show based on contactInquiry.inquiry_type.

Exposes navigation actions that are conditionally displayed within the confirmation panel. If the submitted inquiry type indicates a project quote, it activates navigation to service_category.html?serviceId=training_simulation. If it indicates a partnership or agency inquiry, it activates navigation to service_category.html?serviceId=marketing_brand.
Shows an error message and retry control if getContactInquiryOptions fails, preventing the form from rendering unusable controls until options are successfully loaded.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, dark hero-like viewport for the Contact & Partnerships experience. At the top, the page controller defines a wide, pill-style nav/brand band. Below that, all interactive content (headline, description, form, status, and confirmation) is arranged in a single vertical column, left-aligned within a centered content container that follows a 12-column grid. The form fields stack in a linear order: inquiry qualifiers at the top (type, interests, region, budget), followed by contact details, then submission and feedback (status banner, confirmation, follow-up links). Background behaviors (loading, submission) are handled invisibly by controller/API components but surface visually through the status banner and error message, all inside the same column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, dark hero-like viewport for the Contact & Partnerships experience. At the top, the page controller defines a wide, pill-style nav/brand band. Below that, all interactive content (headline, description, form, status, and confirmation) is arranged in a single vertical column, left-aligned within a centered content container that follows a 12-column grid. The form fields stack in a linear order: inquiry qualifiers at the top (type, interests, region, budget), followed by contact details, then submission and feedback (status banner, confirmation, follow-up links). Background behaviors (loading, submission) are handled invisibly by controller/API components but surface visually through the status banner and error message, all inside the same column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 10–15% of the viewport is reserved for the hero-style nav/brand band and the page title area, controlled by the page controller and form container. The main inquiry form column occupies the central 40–50% of the viewport height on initial load, with large dark margins above and below. Horizontally, the content container spans about 60–70% of the page width, with 15–20% margins on each side. Status and error banners occupy a narrow band at the top of the form column, while the follow-up navigation links sit in the lower portion of the column, with generous spacing above them and a mostly empty lower viewport, echoing the original hero’s scroll-chevron space.
layout_strategies.content_density: All elements are laid out with very low density, using generous vertical spacing (24–64px) between sections and fields. Each logical cluster—form header, qualifier fields, contact details block, status/confirmation, and follow-up links—feels isolated within the dark background. Inputs do not stretch edge to edge; they occupy a comfortable portion of the column, leaving breathing room to the right. No element feels crowded; labels, text, and cards are set with ample padding and margins to preserve a premium, cinematic feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye first hits the top nav/brand band, then drops to the left-aligned page headline and short description. From there, the flow moves down through the primary inquiry controls (inquiry type, areas of interest, region, budget), then into the contact details block. Once the user submits, the eye is drawn immediately to the status banner (if present) and then to the large, central confirmation panel that replaces the form content. Finally, attention is guided down to the follow-up navigation links near the lower center of the page, occupying the space analogous to the original scroll-chevron cue.

layout.overall_description: The Contact & Partnerships page reads like a single, cinematic hero scene on a matte black background. A wide, pill-shaped nav/brand band anchors the very top, centered horizontally and spanning most of the width. Beneath it, a tall, invisible content column sits in the middle of the viewport, left-aligned within a 12-column grid and framed by generous dark margins on both sides. At the top of this column, a bold, oversized headline and a brief, neon-accented description establish context. Directly below, sculpted with large vertical gaps, the key inquiry controls and contact fields stack in a deliberate rhythm—broad, minimal inputs occupying the left portion of the column and fading into empty space on the right. Any status or error messaging appears as slim, high-contrast bands near the top of the form column, while a successful submission swaps the entire stack for a crisp confirmation panel in the same position. Toward the bottom of the viewport, with ample negative space above and below, a small cluster of follow-up navigation links sits near the center, echoing the original hero’s bottom scroll cue and signaling the next step in the experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller defines a full-viewport, dark hero section that acts as the canvas for the entire Contact & Partnerships experience. It establishes a centered content container that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving 15–20% matte-black margins on both sides. At the very top (occupying roughly the upper 10–15% of the viewport), it positions a wide, pill-shaped nav/brand bar that is horizontally centered and slightly inset from the page edges, mimicking the hero-style navigation of the mockup. Below this band, it vertically centers the main form container within the remaining height, but biased slightly upwards so that the top of the form column sits in the upper-middle of the viewport. The controller also reserves an empty band across the bottom 15–20% of the viewport, keeping it largely free of content so that status, confirmation, and follow-up elements can breathe and the overall composition remains airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a full-bleed, scrollable hero section. The top nav band may remain visually anchored near the top on scroll (sticky within the first screen height), while the central column content scrolls normally within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inquiry-options-loader operates invisibly within the bounds of the inquiry-form-container. It does not occupy its own visible space; instead, its loading and error states influence the presence and content of the status banner and the options within the inquiry type and areas of interest fields. In loading states, the fields it powers may show subtle, inline skeleton content or dim placeholders but remain positioned where they normally appear in the vertical form stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it affects the content within existing form fields without changing the layout or the spatial flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inquiry-type-selector is the first and most prominent form field in the central column. It sits directly below the form’s introductory copy (headline and short description) inside the inquiry-form-container, with a generous vertical gap of approximately 32–48px from the text above. Horizontally, it aligns to the left edge of the content column and spans roughly 60–70% of that column, leaving a comfortable dark margin on the right. The control itself may resemble a pill or segmented toggle set, with ample internal padding to match the premium aesthetic. It occupies about one field-row in height, with sufficient white space above and below to visually separate it from other fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical form stack, anchoring the top of the interactive sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The areas-of-interest-multiselect sits immediately beneath the inquiry-type-selector, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It remains left-aligned with the same column edge and shares a similar width—about 60–70% of the content column—maintaining a straight left visual line down the page. If it uses multi-pill or tag-style options, they wrap within this width, but the block as a whole still feels like a single, contained band of controls. The right side of the column remains open, preserving the low-density, editorial appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly after the inquiry-type-selector in the central form column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The region-selector-group appears as the next logical cluster below the areas-of-interest-multiselect, offset by roughly 24–40px of vertical spacing to read as its own segment. It occupies the full width of the central column but organizes its internal controls horizontally—e.g., region dropdown on the left, optional timezone or market subtype on the right—creating a low-slung, two- or three-column row within the group. This row still adheres to the left alignment of the column and does not extend beyond its 60–70% width, leaving the extreme right side of the page in darkness. The grouping visually feels like a single band across the column, with consistent internal padding and alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, behaving as a compact, horizontal group within the otherwise vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-minimum-field is positioned below the region-selector-group with a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px, but it is intentionally narrower to reinforce the sense of negative space. It aligns to the same left edge as the other fields but only spans roughly 40–50% of the column width, leaving a conspicuous, empty dark area to its right. This creates a visual taper before the more substantial contact details section. The field’s label and input are set on a single vertical axis, maintaining a clean, minimalist line down the left side of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, acting as a transitional element between the qualifier controls and the contact details section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-section occupies a visually significant block beneath the budget-minimum-field, separated by approximately 32–48px of vertical space to mark a new phase of the form. At its top, a small section label and optional neon-accented vertical bar sit flush with the left edge, echoing the mocked-up subheading treatment and guiding the eye downward. Within this section, individual fields (such as name, email, company, and project description) are stacked vertically with generous 16–24px gaps between them. Each field spans about 70–80% of the column width, slightly wider than the budget field, yet still leaving a clean, empty margin to the right. The entire section forms a tall, cohesive block in the middle of the column, occupying roughly a third of the viewport height on desktop when all fields are visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the main body of the form and anchoring the center of the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inquiry-form-container is the primary structural wrapper inside the central column, sitting below the top nav band and page title within the hero-like viewport. It begins approximately 15–20% down from the top of the viewport, so that its header (headline + brief description) appears in the upper-middle of the screen. Horizontally, the container aligns to the center of the page but restricts all content to a left-aligned column that spans about 60–70% of the page width. The internal layout stacks in this order: a bold, large-scale page title at the top, then a single line or short paragraph of descriptive copy with a thin neon vertical bar on its left edge, followed by the submission-status-banner (when active), then the vertical sequence of fields: inquiry-type-selector, areas-of-interest-multiselect, region-selector-group, budget-minimum-field, and the contact-details-section. At the bottom of the container, the submit button and any inline form-level notes align with the left edge of the fields, with 32–48px of spacing above them. The container leaves generous empty space between its bottommost element and the bottom of the viewport, preserving the cinematic feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, serving as the main vertical stack for all interactive form content. On successful submission, its inner form elements are replaced by the confirmation-panel while the container’s positioning remains fixed within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-inquiry-submission-handler has no direct visual footprint. It lives conceptually at the bottom of the inquiry-form-container, attached to the submit button. When active, it influences the button state (e.g., showing a subtle loading state within the same button area) and triggers updates to the submission-status-banner and confirmation-panel. It does not add any additional spacing or visible element to the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it operates behind the scenes, modifying the state of existing elements in their current positions without affecting layout geometry.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-banner appears as a thin but visually striking band within the inquiry-form-container. When present, it is positioned just below the descriptive subheading and neon accent line, and above the first form field (inquiry-type-selector), separated by about 16–24px of vertical space both above and below. It spans the full width of the content column (matching the widest field width, roughly 60–70% of the page width), with internal padding reminiscent of the nav pill—around 12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally. Its background or border uses a neon accent color to stand out against the matte black, but the height remains modest so it doesn’t overwhelm the primary form content. When hidden, the fields simply shift upward, preserving the overall vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the column; it appears and disappears in place, pushing adjacent content up or down without overlaying it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation-panel replaces the form contents inside the inquiry-form-container once a submission succeeds. It occupies the same left-aligned column and width (about 60–70% of the viewport) and is vertically positioned in the same upper-middle region of the viewport as the original form, maintaining the hero-like composition. At the top of the panel, a succinct, bold confirmation message mirrors the visual weight of the former page headline, followed by a short explanatory line or summary of the submission. The panel uses generous vertical spacing—24–40px—between its elements, and its overall height is slightly shorter than the full form, leaving even more negative space below. This ensures that, even on larger displays, the confirmation reads as a clean, isolated block floating within the dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form container, fading or sliding into the same slot where the form fields previously sat. It does not scroll independently but moves with the page as a single block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The follow-up-navigation-links appear just below the main content of the confirmation-panel (or beneath the submit button area when the form is still visible), separated by a generous vertical gap of around 32–48px. They are horizontally centered relative to the main content column but may extend slightly toward the page center, making them sit visually near the middle of the viewport width. Typically presented as a small cluster of text links or subtle pill buttons (e.g., ‘Back to work’, ‘Explore services’), they occupy only a short horizontal row, leaving a large expanse of dark space around them. Vertically, they tend to fall into the lower third of the viewport on desktop, echoing the position and emphasis of the scroll chevron from the mockup and subtly inviting the user to continue exploring.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the layout, always following the form or confirmation content. On shorter viewports, they may be pushed just below the fold but remain within the same linear scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The options-load-error-message appears within the inquiry-form-container when the inquiry-options-loader fails. It is positioned in the same band as the submission-status-banner would be—just beneath the descriptive subheading and above the first form field—so the user’s eye encounters it immediately after reading the intro text. It spans the same width as the column (about 60–70% of the viewport) and uses a slim, high-contrast bar with a neon-red or alert-colored accent line or border to distinguish it from success or neutral statuses. The height remains modest, with 12–16px of internal vertical padding, so it feels sharp and precise rather than bulky. When visible, it pushes the form fields downward slightly but preserves the generous spacing pattern above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a fixed slot in the vertical stack when needed and collapsing entirely when not present, without overlapping other elements.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays static About-page content for the XR studio and provides navigation to related sections such as Services, Case Studies, Contact, and Careers.

core_features: Fetch and render the About page content using the getStaticPageContent interface with the appropriate page slug.
Support deep-linking to specific sections within the About content using a URL parameter (e.g., ?section=some-section-id).
Provide an in-page section navigator (table of contents) derived dynamically from the loaded HTML content to help users jump between sections (e.g., mission, team, approach).
Offer clear navigation actions to related pages (Services, Case Studies, Contact, Careers) via dedicated CTA links.
Synchronize user navigation (clicking internal section links) with URL state so that the current section is reflected in the query parameters and can be bookmarked or shared.
user_workflows:
View general About information:
1. User navigates to about.html without URL parameters.
2. The page initializes and calls getStaticPageContent with pageSlug corresponding to the About page.
3. The response title and contentHtml are stored in page state.
4. The content renderer component displays contentHtml.
5. The section navigation component parses the rendered content (e.g., heading elements with IDs) to build a clickable section list.
6. User reads content and optionally uses the section navigation to jump to specific areas.

Deep-link to a specific section (e.g., from another page or external link):
1. User opens about.html with a section parameter, for example about.html?section=team (exact value must match an element ID in contentHtml).
2. The page initializes and calls getStaticPageContent for the About page.
3. Once contentHtml is rendered, the section view component looks up the element whose ID equals the section parameter value.
4. If found, the component scrolls that element into view and marks it as the active section.
5. The section navigation component highlights the corresponding section entry as active based on the same section parameter.

Navigate between sections using in-page navigation:
1. User clicks a section entry in the section navigation (e.g., one representing a mission or team section derived from headings in contentHtml).
2. The section navigation component prevents default full-page navigation and updates the URL query parameter section to the clicked section’s ID using history.pushState or a full reload (implementation-dependent).
3. The section view component listens for URL changes, reads the new section parameter, and scrolls the corresponding element into view.
4. The active state in the section navigation is updated to reflect the newly selected section.

Navigate to Services page from About:
1. User scrolls through the About content and reaches a CTA intended to send them to the Services page.
2. User clicks the CTA.
3. The CTA component triggers navigation to services.html (optionally with additional query parameters if needed).
4. The browser loads services.html.

Navigate to Case Studies page from About:
1. User reaches the link or CTA that leads to the Case Studies listing.
2. User clicks it.
3. The CTA component navigates the browser to case_studies.html.

Navigate to Contact page from About:
1. User reaches the CTA that invites them to talk to the team.
2. User clicks the CTA.
3. The CTA component navigates the browser to contact.html (optionally including a query parameter to indicate the source page, if desired).

Navigate to Careers page from About:
1. User reaches the section or block discussing open roles or joining the team.
2. User clicks the corresponding link or CTA.
3. The CTA component navigates the browser to careers.html.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Trigger: about.html loads.
- Action: Call getStaticPageContent with pageSlug set to identify the About page.
- Result: Store the returned title and contentHtml in the page’s state and render them.

Section-based deep link on load:
- Trigger: about.html loads with a URL parameter section=<sectionId>.
- Action: After contentHtml has been rendered, locate the DOM element in the rendered content whose ID matches <sectionId>.
- Result: Scroll to that element and mark it as the active section.

Click a section navigation item:
- Trigger: User clicks a section entry in the section navigation component.
- Action: Read the target section ID from the clicked item, update the URL’s section parameter, and scroll the matching element into view.
- Result: Content jumps to the selected section; navigation updates to highlight the active section.

Browser back/forward navigation affecting section:
- Trigger: User uses the browser back/forward buttons after having navigated between sections.
- Action: The section navigation and section view components listen for URL changes (popstate or full reload) and re-apply the logic for the current section parameter.
- Result: Page scroll position and active section update to reflect browser history state.

Click "Explore our services" CTA:
- Trigger: User clicks the CTA leading to the Services page.
- Action: Navigate to services.html.
- Result: Browser loads the services.html page.

Click "View our work" link:
- Trigger: User clicks the link leading to Case Studies.
- Action: Navigate to case_studies.html.
- Result: Browser loads the case_studies.html page.

Click "Talk to our team" CTA:
- Trigger: User clicks the CTA leading to the Contact page.
- Action: Navigate to contact.html, optionally including a query parameter such as source=about if desired.
- Result: Browser loads the contact.html page.

Click "Join the team" link:
- Trigger: User clicks the link leading to Careers.
- Action: Navigate to careers.html.
- Result: Browser loads the careers.html page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page maintains minimal client-side state derived from the getStaticPageContent response and the URL query parameters. The primary URL parameter used is `section`.

- `section` (string, optional):
  - Purpose: Identify which subsection of the About page should be considered active and scrolled into view.
  - Source of truth: The URL query string (e.g., about.html?section=team-section or any ID that exists in the rendered contentHtml).
  - On initial load:
    - The page reads `section` from the URL.
    - The page calls getStaticPageContent for the About page.
    - After contentHtml is rendered, the page attempts to find an element whose ID matches the `section` value. If found, it scrolls to that element and marks it active.
    - If no `section` parameter is provided or the ID is not found, the page simply stays at the top of the content without an active section.
  - On section navigation click:
    - The section navigation component updates the URL to include `section=<targetSectionId>`.
    - If using history.pushState, the page does not reload; instead, listeners respond to the URL change and scroll accordingly.
    - If using full-page reload navigation, the same initial load logic applies.
  - On browser history navigation (back/forward):
    - The page listens for URL changes and re-applies the logic to scroll to the section indicated by the current `section` parameter.

No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. All state that should be shareable or restorable is expressed through URL parameters and the server-provided static content for this page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page adopts a single, vertically stacked hero section that fills the initial viewport, mirroring the mockup. At the very top, a wide, pill-shaped global nav is centered horizontally. Below, in the upper-middle of the viewport, a left-aligned hero content column contains the About-page headline and a concise descriptive line. The copy is minimal and editorial rather than marketing-heavy. The bottom of the hero centers a scroll cue that stands alone, detached from the text column. Any subsequent About content (studio story, approach, team, links to Services/Case Studies/Careers) continues below this hero within the same central column alignment, but these are treated as separate sections stacked beneath the fold.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page adopts a single, vertically stacked hero section that fills the initial viewport, mirroring the mockup. At the very top, a wide, pill-shaped global nav is centered horizontally. Below, in the upper-middle of the viewport, a left-aligned hero content column contains the About-page headline and a concise descriptive line. The copy is minimal and editorial rather than marketing-heavy. The bottom of the hero centers a scroll cue that stands alone, detached from the text column. Any subsequent About content (studio story, approach, team, links to Services/Case Studies/Careers) continues below this hero within the same central column alignment, but these are treated as separate sections stacked beneath the fold.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero consumes one full viewport height on desktop. The pill-shaped nav occupies roughly the top 10–15% of the screen height, with generous breathing room above and below. The hero headline and subcopy sit in the next 40–50% of the viewport but use only a central content column spanning about 60–70% of page width, leaving 15–20% dark margins on both left and right. The lower 15–20% of the hero remains mostly empty, with only the centrally aligned scroll indicator. Below the hero, each About section uses similar central-column width and generous vertical margins (64–96px between sections) to preserve the cinematic, premium feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is intentionally low. Each major element in the hero (nav pill, headline block, scroll cue) is visually isolated with large gaps of negative space between them. Copy blocks are short—one main headline line and a single-sentence subcopy. Subsequent About sections favor concise paragraphs, limited bullet clusters, and large headings, avoiding crowded layouts. Padding inside elements (like nav pill or any feature blocks below the hero) stays generous (16–24px vertical, 24–40px horizontal), reinforcing the premium, editorial aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey begins at the top-center nav pill, which anchors brand and primary navigation. From there, the eye drops down and slightly left to the bold About-page hero headline—this is the dominant focal point. The neon vertical accent line to the left of the subcopy guides the gaze straight down from the headline to the supporting text. After reading this line, the user’s attention naturally falls to the bottom center, where a neon double-chevron scroll indicator invites them to continue downward. Scrolling moves the user into the deeper About content, which continues this top-to-bottom flow within the same central column and clear section breaks.

layout.overall_description: On the About page, the initial viewport is a dark, immersive hero that feels like an editorial spread. A matte-black canvas spans edge to edge. At the very top, floating slightly below the browser chrome, a wide pill-shaped navigation bar is centered horizontally, spanning about 70–80% of the page width. It sits in its own band of space, with comfortable breathing room above and below, and appears as a sleek control surface against the dark background. Directly under this, but with a substantial vertical gap, the hero’s content column begins: aligned slightly left of center within the central grid, occupying roughly 60–70% of the page width. Here, a large, bold About headline for the XR studio dominates the mid-upper portion of the screen—big, high-contrast typography set against the dark field. Just below it, aligned to the same left edge, a thin neon vertical bar sits flush with the hero text block and precedes a single-line subheading that succinctly frames the studio’s mission or philosophy. The vertical bar and subcopy sit close together, but they are separated from the headline above by a clear margin, preserving clarity and hierarchy. The lower portion of the viewport remains mostly empty, emphasizing the cinematic feel. At the clear vertical center of the page’s bottom edge, a small, neon double-chevron scroll indicator glows subtly, floating above the emptiness and clearly signaling to scroll down. As the user scrolls, new About sections appear below the hero in the same central column alignment—each section separated by tall vertical spacing—creating a continuous, elegant narrative that always feels anchored within the same invisible grid and surrounded by generous dark margins.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays dynamically loaded privacy policy content, with support for section-level navigation, deep links, and print-friendly viewing.

core_features: Fetch and render the full privacy policy content using the getStaticPageContent interface and a pageSlug derived from the URL.
Display the policy title and HTML content returned by getStaticPageContent without hardcoding any policy text.
Provide an automatically generated in-page table of contents based on headings within the loaded policy content to help users jump to relevant sections (e.g., data collection, cookies, user rights).
Support deep linking to specific sections of the privacy policy via URL parameters so users can be taken directly to topics like cookies or data subject rights.
Offer section-level anchor links so users can copy a direct URL to a specific part of the policy (for sharing from legal notices, emails, or cookie banners).
Provide a print-friendly mode of the privacy policy, controlled by URL parameters, allowing users to print or save the policy as a PDF.

Ensure that cookie-related explanations and privacy preference management information are accessible within the loaded policy content, relying entirely on the dynamic HTML returned from getStaticPageContent.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing the Privacy Policy from the footer
1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the footer, which navigates to privacy.html with an optional pageSlug URL parameter.
2. On page load, the static-page loader component extracts the pageSlug parameter or derives a default slug from the URL and calls getStaticPageContent with that value.
3. The API returns an object containing the policy title and contentHtml.
4. The content viewer component renders the title and injects the contentHtml into the page.
5. The table-of-contents component scans the rendered content for headings and builds a clickable list of sections.
6. User scrolls through or clicks items in the table of contents to jump to specific sections such as data collection, cookies, or user rights.

Workflow: Opening a deep link to a specific privacy section (e.g., from an email or cookie banner)
1. User clicks an external link that points to privacy.html with URL parameters specifying the target section (e.g., privacy.html?section=cookies) and optionally a specific pageSlug.
2. On page load, the static-page loader calls getStaticPageContent using the pageSlug derived from the URL.
3. Once contentHtml is rendered, the content viewer component reads the section URL parameter and locates the corresponding heading/anchor within the DOM.
4. The content viewer scrolls the page to the matching section and applies a temporary highlight or focus state for that section.
5. If the user changes the section parameter in the URL (e.g., via the table of contents), the content viewer updates the scroll position to the new section.

Workflow: Printing or saving the Privacy Policy
1. User navigates to privacy.html normally or via an external link that may include a mode parameter (e.g., privacy.html?mode=print).
2. On page load, the static-page loader retrieves the privacy policy via getStaticPageContent.
3. The mode controller component reads the mode URL parameter.
4. If mode indicates print view, the mode controller activates print-friendly behavior (for example, applying a print mode state and invoking the browser's print dialog in response to an explicit user action).
5. If the user toggles print mode via a control on the page, the mode controller updates the URL mode parameter and adjusts the page state accordingly.
6. User prints or saves the policy and can return to normal mode by removing or changing the mode parameter.

Workflow: Sharing a specific section of the Privacy Policy
1. User scrolls to a section heading (e.g., the section that explains cookie usage or data access rights) within the rendered content.
2. The section-anchor-link component attached to that heading offers an action (such as a clickable icon) to copy a deep link.
3. When the user activates this action, the component constructs a URL that includes the current pageSlug (if any) and the section identifier in the URL parameters.
4. The component copies this URL to the clipboard or updates the browser's address bar using the History API without reloading the page.
5. User shares that URL via email or another medium; recipients who follow the link arrive directly at the same section following the deep-link workflow.
interactions: On initial page load, parse URL parameters (e.g., pageSlug, section, mode) and call getStaticPageContent with the resolved pageSlug.

On successful response from getStaticPageContent, update the page state with the returned title and contentHtml, then trigger initialization of dependent components (e.g., table of contents, section anchors, scroll-to-section).
When a user clicks a table-of-contents entry, update the section parameter in the URL using pushState/replaceState and scroll to the corresponding heading in the content.

When a user activates a section-level anchor/link control, generate a URL including the appropriate section parameter and either copy it to the clipboard or update the browser address bar without reloading.
When the URL's section parameter changes (either from user interaction within the page or from navigation/back-forward actions), re-run the logic that scrolls to and highlights the target section.
When a user activates a print-mode control, update the mode parameter in the URL, switch the internal state to print mode, and optionally prompt a browser print action in response to that user event.

When the user navigates using browser back/forward, listen for URL parameter changes and synchronize the view (content scroll position, mode) with the new parameters without re-calling getStaticPageContent unless pageSlug changes.

If the pageSlug parameter changes (e.g., navigating between different localized or versioned privacy policies), re-call getStaticPageContent with the new slug and fully re-render the title and contentHtml.
page_functionality.state_logic: Page state is driven entirely from URL parameters and the response of getStaticPageContent, with no use of localStorage. The primary URL parameters are: (1) pageSlug: optional; if present, it is passed directly to getStaticPageContent. If absent, a default slug is derived from the current URL (for example, based on the filename or path segment) and used for the call. Changing pageSlug in the URL triggers a new getStaticPageContent request and a full refresh of the displayed policy content. (2) section: optional; represents the logical identifier of a policy section (for example, mapped to an id attribute on headings within contentHtml). After contentHtml is rendered, the page looks up the target section by this identifier and scrolls to that element, applying any focus or highlight behavior. Updates to the section parameter (via in-page navigation or browser navigation) only affect scroll/selection state and do not cause another getStaticPageContent call, unless pageSlug also changes. (3) mode: optional; controls page mode such as normal or print. The mode controller reads this parameter and adjusts internal state to reflect the selected mode. For example, a print mode might adjust layout behavior and can trigger the browser's print dialog after the user clicks a print action. Changing mode in the URL updates the internal state synchronously; it does not require a new getStaticPageContent call. All derived state (current section, scroll position, view mode) is recomputed based solely on the current URL parameters and the content returned from getStaticPageContent.

components.functionality: Initializes the Privacy Policy page by resolving the pageSlug from URL parameters or the current path, calling getStaticPageContent with that slug, and providing the returned title and contentHtml to child components. It also listens for changes to the pageSlug parameter (for example, from navigation to another version or locale of the privacy policy) and re-fetches content as needed.

Renders the privacy policy title and HTML content provided by the loader component. After rendering the contentHtml, it initializes section anchors (e.g., assigning ids to headings if not already present) and coordinates with the section navigation logic to scroll to the section specified by the section URL parameter. It also exposes hooks for other components (such as the table of contents) to query the structured outline of headings.

Builds an interactive list of sections within the privacy policy by scanning the rendered contentHtml for heading elements (e.g., h2, h3). Each entry in the table of contents corresponds to a heading and, when clicked, updates the section parameter in the URL and requests the content viewer to scroll to that heading. It also tracks which section is currently in view and can reflect that state (for example, by highlighting the active entry).

For each major section heading within the privacy policy, manages a control that allows users to obtain a deep link to that section. When the control is activated, it constructs a URL that includes the current pageSlug (if present) and the corresponding section identifier, then either copies this URL to the clipboard or updates the browser's address bar using the History API. This enables external references to specific policy sections, such as cookie information or user rights.

Controls the display mode of the Privacy Policy page, especially for print-friendly viewing. It reads the mode URL parameter on initialization to set the initial view mode. When the user triggers a mode change (for example, by clicking a 'Print' or 'Standard view' control), it updates the mode parameter in the URL, adjusts the internal mode state, and may initiate the browser print flow in direct response to user action. It ensures that the visible state of the page remains in sync with the mode parameter when users navigate using back/forward.

Centralizes parsing and updating of URL parameters relevant to the Privacy Policy page (pageSlug, section, mode). It provides helper methods to other components to read current values and to update them using pushState/replaceState without causing full page reloads. It also listens for popstate events so that when the user navigates back or forward, interested components (loader, content viewer, table of contents, mode controller) are notified of the new parameter values and can update their state accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The privacy policy page uses a single, tall hero-like document section with a strong, linear top-to-bottom flow. At the very top of the content area, a wide, pill-shaped table of contents bar (local page nav) echoes the hero nav from the mockup. Below it, separated by generous vertical spacing, sits the left-aligned page title ('Privacy Policy') within a central content column. Directly beneath the title, also left-aligned to the same column, is a brief introductory line or meta text, visually anchored by a thin vertical neon line. The main privacy policy text then flows downward in a single, left-aligned column. Along the right edge of this column, a subtle vertical anchor spine provides section-level navigation and deep-link feedback. Utility controls like the view mode switch sit at the top-right of the content column, visually light and secondary.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The privacy policy page uses a single, tall hero-like document section with a strong, linear top-to-bottom flow. At the very top of the content area, a wide, pill-shaped table of contents bar (local page nav) echoes the hero nav from the mockup. Below it, separated by generous vertical spacing, sits the left-aligned page title ('Privacy Policy') within a central content column. Directly beneath the title, also left-aligned to the same column, is a brief introductory line or meta text, visually anchored by a thin vertical neon line. The main privacy policy text then flows downward in a single, left-aligned column. Along the right edge of this column, a subtle vertical anchor spine provides section-level navigation and deep-link feedback. Utility controls like the view mode switch sit at the top-right of the content column, visually light and secondary.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The core content column occupies roughly 60–65% of the viewport width on desktop, centered with equal dark margins of about 17–20% on each side, preserving the cinematic framing. The top table-of-contents pill spans slightly wider (around 70–80% of the viewport) to echo the full-width nav feel, while remaining horizontally centered. Vertically, the top TOC pill and title block occupy the upper 30–40% of the initial viewport; the body content then flows through the remaining height and continues below the fold. Major vertical separations (between TOC, title, intro, and body sections) use 48–64px spacing to maintain the airy feel, with larger 64–96px breaks before new major policy sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low and spacious. The TOC items are separated with noticeable gaps rather than compact lists. The main text uses comfortable line lengths and ample line height, with clear spacing between paragraphs and sections. No element touches the viewport edges; everything is cushioned with generous top, bottom, and side margins. Utility elements (mode controls, anchors) are minimalistic and visually light, avoiding clutter around the main reading area.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the top TOC pill, establishing context for the document. From there, the gaze drops to the bold 'Privacy Policy' heading in the mid–upper portion of the viewport. It then moves slightly down to the intro/meta text, guided by the vertical neon accent line. As the user scrolls down, attention shifts to the main policy content in the central column. The vertical anchor spine on the right acts as a subtle, persistent cue for where you are in the document and how to jump between sections, analogous to the mockup’s scroll indicator. The view mode control sits quietly in the periphery (top-right), available but not competing for attention.

layout.overall_description: The privacy policy page feels like a premium, editorial document staged within a dark, futuristic environment. A wide, pill-shaped table-of-contents bar floats near the top of the content area, centered horizontally with generous dark space around it, echoing the hero navigation from the mockup. Beneath it, within a centered but left-aligned content column, a large, bold 'Privacy Policy' title anchors the composition, with a single line of intro or meta copy directly below, preceded by a thin vertical neon line that visually tethers the text and adds a tech accent. Below this header block, the privacy policy unfolds as a tall, scrollable column of text, contained within roughly two-thirds of the page width and surrounded by deep, matte black margins on both sides, keeping the reading experience focused and calm. Along the right edge of this text column, a slender vertical spine with small neon markers remains sticky as the user scrolls, indicating the current section and offering quick jumps—an evolved, document-centric echo of the mockup’s scroll chevron. A discreet view-mode control sits near the top-right of the column, letting users toggle a print-friendly layout without disturbing the composition. System components like loading states and URL sync operate invisibly, so the user primarily experiences a clean, cinematic reading surface framed in darkness, with a strong, top-to-bottom narrative flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The static page loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that temporarily replaces the document view while privacy content is being fetched. When active, it covers the entire browser window from edge to edge, including the central content column and surrounding dark margins. The background is a slightly lighter or subtly textured matte black than the base page to differentiate the loading state, while still fitting the futuristic aesthetic. At the exact center of the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, a small but sharp neon-accented loader (e.g., a ring, line, or minimal glyph) pulses or animates, occupying roughly 5–8% of the viewport width. This central loader is the single focal point, surrounded by generous empty space. When loading completes, the overlay fades out, revealing the underlying layout; when inactive, this component has no visible footprint and does not occupy any space in the document flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-screen overlay positioned fixed to the viewport; sits above all other components when active and is completely removed from visual layout when inactive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content viewer is the primary reading area, structured as a tall, scrollable column centered within the viewport. On desktop, it occupies about 60–65% of the overall page width and is centered horizontally, leaving substantial matte-black margins on both sides (approximately 17–20% of the width each). The left edge of this column defines the main alignment axis for the title, intro text, and all policy sections. Vertically, the content viewer begins just below the hero-style header block (title and intro), leaving about 48–64px of vertical space between the intro and the first policy heading. The text within the viewer is left-aligned with a comfortable line length (roughly 65–80 characters per line) and generous line height, reflecting the low-density, editorial style. Each major section of the privacy policy is separated by 48–64px of vertical space, with section headings slightly larger and bolder but still within a refined, tech-forward type system. The right edge of the content column leaves a narrow gutter (about 5–8% of the page width) before the vertical anchor spine, ensuring the text feels contained and not cramped by navigation controls. On smaller screens, the column shrinks proportionally and margins reduce, but the single-column, left-aligned reading flow is preserved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow but vertically scrollable as the main page content; acts as the anchor region for section anchors and deep-link targets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents is rendered as a large, pill-shaped bar near the very top of the privacy policy section, visually echoing the hero nav from the mockup. It spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop and is horizontally centered, leaving equal dark space on both sides. The pill’s height is generous—around 56–72px—to create a substantial but sleek band across the upper portion of the page. Inside the pill, the TOC items (section titles or short labels) are arranged left-aligned within the pill’s inner padding, forming a horizontal row of links or compact chips with sizable gaps (16–24px) between them. The left edge of the TOC content loosely aligns with the left edge of the main content column below, creating a subtle vertical relationship. Above the pill, there is a small amount of breathing room (24–32px) from any global site header; below it, a larger vertical gap (48–64px) separates the pill from the 'Privacy Policy' title, preserving the tiered, spacious hierarchy. On scroll, the TOC can optionally become sticky: once the user scrolls past it, it pins itself to the top of the viewport within the central width, maintaining its width and pill shape but slightly reducing vertical padding to feel more compact while sticking. On smaller devices, it can collapse into a horizontal scrollable pill or a dropdown while remaining visually distinct and centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially static at the top of the document; optionally becomes sticky to the top of the viewport within the central content width once scrolled, preserving central alignment and pill shape.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section anchor controller manifests as a slim vertical navigation spine aligned with the right side of the main content region, serving as a continuous, document-level scroll cue. Visually, it is a thin vertical line—2–3px wide—with a neon accent color, running parallel to the text column. On desktop, it is positioned roughly where the content column meets the right inner margin: about 5–8% of the viewport width to the right of the text edge, so it feels adjacent but not intrusive. The vertical spine begins near the mid-height of the viewport and extends downward; its top aligns approximately with the lower part of the hero title block when at rest. Along this line, small nodes or chevrons represent individual sections of the policy. The currently active section is highlighted with a brighter neon marker and slightly larger hit area (e.g., 12–16px), while inactive sections are smaller, dimmer dots. Clicking a marker scrolls smoothly to the corresponding section inside the content viewer. This spine remains visible as the user scrolls, maintaining a constant position relative to the viewport. The effect is analogous to the mockup’s bottom-centered scroll chevron but adapted into a continuous, right-side rail for a long-form document. On narrow screens, the spine can be hidden or condensed into an icon-triggered overlay to avoid competing for limited width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a sticky/fixed element relative to the viewport, anchored to the right side of the central content area; its markers are bound to scroll position and update as the user moves through sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view mode controller is a compact, pill-like control cluster that sits in the upper-right region of the central content area, providing quick access to standard vs. print-friendly viewing. On desktop, it aligns horizontally with the 'Privacy Policy' heading or just slightly above or below it, anchored to the right edge of the main content column, so that the heading is on the left and the control is on the right, framing the top of the document. The control itself is modest in size—approximately 120–180px wide and 32–40px tall—with subtle neon accents only on hover or active states to avoid outshining the main typographic hierarchy. A typical configuration would present two options (e.g., 'Default' and 'Print') as inline toggle buttons within the pill, with clear spacing and high-contrast labels. There is 16–24px of horizontal spacing between the rightmost edge of the heading text and the left edge of this controller, ensuring they feel related but not cramped. Vertically, 24–32px of space separates this controller from any elements above or below, keeping it unobtrusive. When the user scrolls past the hero header area, the mode controller may optionally stick to the top-right of the content column, shrinking slightly in height to reduce dominance, so it remains accessible while reading long sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially part of the normal document flow, aligned to the top-right of the main content column; can become sticky within the bounds of that column so that it remains visible near the top-right corner during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager operates entirely behind the scenes and has no visual representation. Conceptually, it is bound to the main content column and the section navigation elements: as users click TOC items or section markers along the right spine, this component updates the URL with deep-link hashes corresponding to the active section. Likewise, when a user lands on a URL with a hash, it ensures the page scrolls to the correct section within the content viewer and that the appropriate TOC item and anchor marker appear active. Visually, the layout remains untouched; the user only perceives smoother navigation, consistent highlighting, and shareable URLs while the dark, minimal composition stays clean and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No spatial footprint; functions purely as a behavioral layer tied to scroll events and navigation interactions, without affecting layout or positioning of any visible components.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Displays the dynamically managed Terms of Use content, supports deep-linking into specific sections, and allows navigation to related legal documents such as the Privacy Policy.

core_features: Retrieve the Terms of Use content from the backend using getStaticPageContent, with the pageSlug derived from the current route or an explicit URL parameter.
Render the returned title and contentHtml, including all legal sections and any embedded links, in a scrollable layout.

Support deep-linking to specific sections of the Terms via a URL query parameter (for example, section) and/or hash fragment, automatically scrolling to the corresponding anchor after content is loaded.
Provide an in-page navigation structure (such as a table of contents) generated dynamically from headings found in contentHtml, enabling quick jumps between sections.
Synchronize the user’s current section with the URL (query parameter and/or hash) when they navigate using the in-page navigation, enabling sharable links to specific clauses.
Handle clicks on links within contentHtml that reference the Privacy Policy so that the browser navigates to privacy.html while preserving or appropriately updating URL parameters and fragments.
user_workflows: Read the full Terms of Use: (1) The user navigates to terms.html from the footer or a direct external link. (2) The terms-page-init component derives the pageSlug from the route or an optional query parameter and calls getStaticPageContent(pageSlug). (3) While waiting for a response, the page shows a loading state. (4) On success, the title and contentHtml are passed to terms-content-viewer for rendering and to terms-toc-generator for building an in-page navigation structure. (5) The user scrolls through the content or uses the generated navigation to jump between sections.

Open a specific section via deep link: (1) The user opens terms.html with a URL that includes a query parameter indicating a target section (for example, section={someSectionId}) and/or a hash fragment. (2) terms-page-init loads the content via getStaticPageContent as usual. (3) After contentHtml is rendered, section-anchor-sync reads the current URL parameters and fragment, locates the matching anchor in the DOM (such as an element whose id matches the parameter value), and scrolls it into view. (4) terms-toc-generator highlights the corresponding entry in the in-page navigation to reflect the active section. (5) The user can copy and share the URL, and others opening it will land on the same section.

Navigate to the Privacy Policy from within the Terms: (1) While reading, the user sees one or more links in the rendered contentHtml that reference the Privacy Policy. (2) When the user clicks such a link, legal-crosslink-handler ensures the click is treated as a standard navigation to privacy.html (optionally preserving relevant query parameters or appending new ones if required by the routing scheme). (3) The browser navigates to privacy.html, where a separate instance of getStaticPageContent (on that page) loads and renders the Privacy Policy.
interactions: Page load: Triggered when the browser navigates to terms.html. terms-page-init reads URL parameters, determines the appropriate pageSlug, and calls getStaticPageContent. It updates component state to loading, success, or error based on the response.
