Scroll within terms content: As the user scrolls through the rendered terms, terms-toc-generator listens to scroll events, detects which section heading is currently nearest to the top of the viewport, and updates the active state of the corresponding in-page navigation item. section-anchor-sync can optionally update the URL query parameter or hash to reflect this active section.

Click on an in-page navigation item (e.g., table of contents): When the user clicks a navigation item generated from contentHtml headings, onTocItemClick calculates the target section identifier, updates the URL (for example, sets or replaces the section query parameter without reloading the page), and scrolls the associated section into view.

Click on an in-content anchor link pointing within the same page: If the rendered contentHtml includes internal anchors (for example, links whose href references a local anchor), onInternalLinkClick prevents unnecessary full page reloads, updates the URL hash and/or section query parameter to the target anchor, and scrolls to the corresponding section.

Click on a Privacy Policy link within the terms content: When the user clicks a link in contentHtml that targets the Privacy Policy, onPrivacyLinkClick lets the browser navigate to privacy.html (or the equivalent route) while optionally normalizing or appending query parameters (for example, propagating a language or region parameter if used by the routing system).

Browser back/forward navigation: When the user uses the browser’s back or forward buttons and the URL query parameters or hash fragment change, onUrlChange in section-anchor-sync responds by scrolling to the section indicated by the updated URL state and informing terms-toc-generator so it can update the active navigation item.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the URL and the response from getStaticPageContent. On initial load, the page inspects the current URL. If a pageSlug query parameter is present, its value is used directly in the getStaticPageContent(pageSlug) call. If it is absent, a default slug is derived from the route or filename associated with this Terms of Use page. The API response (title and contentHtml) defines the legal content that is rendered; no content is hardcoded in the page. For in-page navigation, a query parameter (for example, section) and/or the URL hash fragment represent the currently active section. After contentHtml is rendered, the section-anchor-sync component reads these values and scrolls to the matching anchor if it exists. When the user clicks table-of-contents entries or internal section links, the page updates the URL using the History API (for example, using pushState or replaceState) to set or change the section query parameter and/or hash, without reloading the page. If both a section query parameter and a hash are present, the implementation can prioritize the query parameter when determining which section to scroll to, while keeping the hash synchronized if necessary. The page does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence; all navigable state (such as which section is active) is represented via URL parameters and fragments so it can be shared and is compatible with standard navigation behavior.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column or similar desktop grid with a centered content container and wide left/right margins. Content appears left-aligned within this central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the cinematic hero from the mockup at the top, then transitions into a long-form, left-aligned Terms of Use content area. At the very top, a pill-shaped global nav sits centered horizontally across the matte-black background. Below this, occupying the upper-middle of the viewport, a hero block introduces the page with a bold 'Terms of Use' headline and a concise supporting line (e.g., last updated date / legal context) aligned to a single vertical content axis. A subtle vertical neon accent anchors the supporting line, mirroring the mockup. At the bottom center of the hero viewport, a neon double-chevron scroll cue prompts users to move into the legal text. Below the hero fold, the Terms content is presented in a single, left-aligned column that respects the same central grid, with optional in-page section links and related legal navigation (e.g., Privacy Policy) positioned near the top of the content column. All content flows linearly top-to-bottom, with no competing sidebars, preserving the focused, editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the cinematic hero from the mockup at the top, then transitions into a long-form, left-aligned Terms of Use content area. At the very top, a pill-shaped global nav sits centered horizontally across the matte-black background. Below this, occupying the upper-middle of the viewport, a hero block introduces the page with a bold 'Terms of Use' headline and a concise supporting line (e.g., last updated date / legal context) aligned to a single vertical content axis. A subtle vertical neon accent anchors the supporting line, mirroring the mockup. At the bottom center of the hero viewport, a neon double-chevron scroll cue prompts users to move into the legal text. Below the hero fold, the Terms content is presented in a single, left-aligned column that respects the same central grid, with optional in-page section links and related legal navigation (e.g., Privacy Policy) positioned near the top of the content column. All content flows linearly top-to-bottom, with no competing sidebars, preserving the focused, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section roughly occupies the full initial viewport height on desktop. The pill-shaped nav at the very top uses about 10–15% of the vertical space, the hero title and supporting text sit within the next 40–50%, and the bottom 15–20% remains mostly empty to frame the scroll indicator. Horizontally, all primary content (hero text, legal text, section index, related links) sits within a central content container that spans about 60–70% of the page width, leaving 15–20% margin on each side. Within that central container, the main text column uses approximately 70% of the container’s width, maintaining generous inner margins and line lengths that feel premium and readable. Vertical spacing between major page bands (hero → terms content sections → footer) follows the 64–96px rhythm, while spacing between legal subsections respects a smaller but still airy scale (24–48px) so that each section breathes.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains intentionally low and calm, even though this is a text-heavy legal page. The hero is extremely sparse, with a single headline, one-line support text, and a scroll cue surrounded by negative space. In the Terms content area, paragraphs are set with generous line-height and 24–32px spacing between paragraphs or list groups. Section headings have substantial top margins (48–64px) to clearly separate them and maintain a cinematic, editorial rhythm. Lists and definitions are not cramped; each bullet or clause gets enough vertical padding to avoid a wall-of-text feeling. There are no dense tables or sidebars; instead, in-page anchors, section numbers, and document metadata are all treated minimally and aligned to the main text column. Related-document links (like Privacy Policy) appear as a small, isolated cluster with ample padding, not a crowded menu.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey mimics the mockup’s strong vertical flow. The eye first lands on the centered pill-shaped nav at the top, establishing brand and global navigation. It then drops to the large, bold 'Terms of Use' headline in the upper-middle of the hero. From there, the eye is gently led to the supporting text line, visually anchored by a thin vertical neon bar on its left. This accent line subtly points downwards towards the bottom-center scroll chevron, which acts as a clear invitation to move into the main Terms content. As the user scrolls, the focus shifts to the left-aligned legal text column. Section titles, numbered clauses, and occasional neon-accented in-page anchors create a vertical rhythm that guides readers sequentially from one section to the next. Related legal documents are placed near the top of the Terms content area so that, after reading the introductory information, a user can easily pivot to the Privacy Policy or other documents without disrupting the primary vertical scan path.

layout.overall_description: The Terms of Use page reads like a premium, editorial legal document set inside a futuristic, XR-driven hero shell. On a dark, matte black canvas, a wide, pill-shaped navigation bar floats at the very top center, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width with generous padding, giving the impression of a single, sleek interface element suspended in space. Below it, the middle of the viewport is dominated by a bold, large-scale 'Terms of Use' headline left-aligned within an invisible central column; the headline sits slightly above the vertical center, leaving plenty of breathing room above and below. Directly beneath this title, still aligned to the same left edge, runs a thin vertical neon line (about the height of one or two lines of text) leading into a single, concise line of supporting copy that might describe the document or indicate its last updated date. The surrounding area is intentionally sparse, with wide dark margins on both sides and no additional visual noise. At the bottom center of the hero, floating in the lower 15–20% of the screen, a neon double-chevron scroll indicator signals that more content lies below, isolated against the empty dark space. When users scroll, the hero gives way to a long-form content section anchored to the same left-hand column. The Terms text is laid out in a comfortable, medium-width text column centered within the page, with wide lateral margins preserving the cinematic look. Each major section (e.g., 1. Introduction, 2. User Obligations) begins with a clear heading that is visually separated from the previous content by generous vertical spacing, giving each section a sense of importance. Short, minimal in-page navigation—such as a compact list of section links and related documents like the Privacy Policy—appears near the top of this content area, subtly integrated without breaking the single-column flow. Throughout, neon accents are used sparingly to highlight active section anchors or links, while the overall experience remains calm, spacious, and editorial, guiding the user down a clean, linear reading path from hero to final clause.
